text: High-z Type Ia supernovae are expected to be gravitationally lensed by the foreground distribution of large-scale structure. The resulting magnification of supernovae is statistically measurable, and the angular correlation of the magnification pattern directly probes the integrated mass density along the line of sight. Measurements of cosmic magnification of supernovae therefore complements galaxy shear measurements in providing a direct measure of clustering of the dark matter. As the number of supernovae is typically much smaller than the number of sheared galaxies, the two-point correlation function of lensed Type Ia supernovae suffers from significantly increased shot noise. Neverthless, we find that the magnification map of a large sample of supernovae, such as that expected from next generation dedicated searches, will be easily measurable and provide an important cosmological tool. For example, a search over 20 sq. deg. over five years leading to a sample of ~ 10,000 supernovae would measure the angular power spectrum of cosmic magnification with a cumulative signal-to-noise ratio of ~20. This detection can be further improved once the supernova distance measurements are cross-correlated with measurements of the foreground galaxy distribution. The magnification maps made using supernovae can be used for important cross-checks with traditional lensing shear statistics obtained in the same fields, as well as help to control systematics. We discuss two applications of supernova magnification maps: the breaking of the mass-sheet degeneracy when estimating masses of shear-detected clusters, and constraining the second-order corrections to weak lensing observables.

Type Ia supernovae at high redshift are predicted to undergo gravitational lensing due to the intervening large-scale structure. This lensing effect results in measurable magnification of the supernovae, and the angular correlation of the magnification pattern provides a means to investigate the integrated mass density along the line of sight. Thus, measurements of cosmic magnification from supernovae serve as a complementary method to galaxy shear analyses for directly assessing the clustering properties of dark matter. However, because the number of observed supernovae is generally much smaller than the number of galaxies used in shear studies, the two-point correlation function of lensed Type Ia supernovae is subject to elevated shot noise. Despite this limitation, we determine that the magnification map derived from a substantial supernova sample, such as those anticipated from future dedicated surveys, will be robustly measurable and constitute a valuable cosmological resource. 

For instance, a survey covering 20 square degrees over a five-year period, yielding approximately 10,000 supernovae, could achieve a cumulative signal-to-noise ratio of approximately 20 for the angular power spectrum of cosmic magnification. This signal could be further enhanced by cross-correlating supernova distance measurements with data on the distribution of foreground galaxies. Additionally, supernova-based magnification maps offer opportunities for cross-validation with traditional lensing shear statistics in overlapping survey regions and can aid in mitigating systematic errors. Two specific applications of such magnification maps are explored: resolving the mass-sheet degeneracy in the mass determination of shear-detected clusters and constraining higher-order corrections in weak lensing measurements.

Distant Type Ia supernovae are expected to be affected by gravitational lensing caused by the large-scale structures in the foreground. This lensing effect magnifies the brightness of these supernovae, and the magnification pattern can be measured statistically. By studying this pattern, scientists can learn about the total mass along the line of sight, including dark matter. These measurements of supernova magnification work alongside galaxy shear studies to provide a clearer picture of how dark matter is distributed and clustered.

Since there are far fewer supernovae than galaxies, measuring the lensing effects on supernovae comes with more noise. However, with a large enough sample of supernovae, such as what future searches might provide, this magnification signal will become clear and useful for cosmological studies. For instance, a survey covering 20 square degrees over five years could find around 10,000 supernovae. This would allow researchers to measure the angular power spectrum of cosmic magnification with a solid signal-to-noise ratio of about 20. The results could be made even better by combining supernova distance data with information about foreground galaxy distributions.

Supernova magnification maps can also serve as a way to verify results from traditional lensing shear studies in the same regions, helping to reduce errors. These maps offer additional benefits, such as resolving the mass-sheet degeneracy when estimating the masses of galaxy clusters detected through shear, and refining second-order corrections in weak lensing measurements. Together, they provide another important tool for understanding the universe.

This lensing induces a measurable magnification effect, and the angular correlation of the resulting magnification pattern offers a direct probe of the integrated mass density along the line of sight. Consequently, measuring cosmic magnification of supernovae complements galaxy shear analyses by directly assessing the clustering of dark matter. However, as the number of supernovae is significantly smaller than the population of sheared galaxies, the two-point correlation function of lensed Type Ia supernovae is impacted by markedly higher shot noise. Despite this, we determine that magnification maps derived from a sufficiently extensive supernova sample, such as one anticipated from next-generation dedicated surveys, will be readily detectable and serve as a valuable cosmological tool. For instance, a survey spanning 20 square degrees over five years, yielding approximately 10,000 supernovae, could measure the angular power spectrum of cosmic magnification with a cumulative signal-to-noise ratio of roughly 20. Magnification maps constructed from supernovae provide critical opportunities for cross-validation with conventional lensing shear statistics in overlapping fields and contribute to systematic error mitigation. We explore two pivotal applications of supernova magnification maps: resolving the mass-sheet degeneracy in shear-based cluster mass estimates and constraining second-order corrections to weak lensing observables.

These advancements underscore the adventurous potential of supernova magnification studies as a dynamic tool for unraveling cosmic mysteries.

High-z Type Ia supernovae are expected to be affected by gravitational lensing from the large-scale structures in the universe. This lensing can make the supernovae appear brighter, and we can measure this change. The way the brightness changes can tell us about the mass density of the universe along the line of sight. By studying the cosmic magnification of supernovae, we can learn more about how dark matter is clustered, which is similar to what we find with galaxy shear measurements.

However, there are usually fewer supernovae than sheared galaxies, which can make the data noisier. Still, we believe that a large sample of supernovae from future searches will provide clear and useful measurements. For instance, if we search over 20 square degrees for five years and find about 10,000 supernovae, we could measure the angular power spectrum of cosmic magnification with a strong signal-to-noise ratio of around 20. This detection could get even better when we compare the supernova distance data with the distribution of galaxies in front of them.

The magnification maps created from these supernovae can help us check our findings against traditional lensing statistics in the same areas. They can also help reduce errors in our measurements. We talk about two key uses for these magnification maps: they can help us solve the mass-sheet problem when estimating the masses of clusters detected by shear, and they can help us fine-tune second-order corrections in weak lensing observations.

This detection can be enhanced further when the distance measurements of supernovae are cross-correlated with the measurements of the foreground galaxy distribution. The magnification maps created using supernovae can facilitate essential cross-checks with traditional lensing shear statistics obtained in the same fields and assist in controlling systematic uncertainties. We discuss two applications of supernova magnification maps: breaking the mass-sheet degeneracy when estimating the masses of shear-detected clusters and constraining second-order corrections to weak lensing observables.

We study a relativistic quantum cavity system realized by etching out from a graphene sheet by quantum transport measurements and theoretical calculations. The conductance of the graphene cavity has been measured as a function of the back gate voltage (or the Fermi energy) and the magnetic field applied perpendicular to the graphene sheet, and characteristic conductance contour patterns are observed in the measurements. In particular, two types of high conductance contour lines, i.e., straight and parabolic-like high conductance contour lines, are found in the measurements. The theoretical calculations are performed within the framework of tight-binding approach and Green's function formalism. Similar characteristic high conductance contour features as in the experiments are found in the calculations. The wave functions calculated at points selected along a straight conductance contour line are found to be dominated by a chain of scars of high probability distributions arranged as a necklace following the shape of cavity and the current density distributions calculated at these point are dominated by an overall vortex in the cavity. These characteristics are found to be insensitive to increasing magnetic field. However, the wave function probability distributions and the current density distributions calculated at points selected along a parabolic-like contour line show a clear dependence on increasing magnetic field, and the current density distributions at these points are characterized by the complex formation of several localized vortices in the cavity. Our work brings a new insight into quantum chaos in relativistic particle systems and would greatly stimulate experimental and theoretical efforts towards this still emerging field.

This study examines a relativistic quantum cavity system formed by removing portions of a graphene sheet, utilizing quantum transport measurements and theoretical modeling. The conductance of the graphene cavity was measured as a function of back gate voltage, which corresponds to the Fermi energy, and as a function of the perpendicular magnetic field applied to the graphene sheet. These measurements revealed distinct conductance contour patterns. Specifically, two types of high-conductance contour lines were identified: straight lines and parabolic-like lines. 

Theoretical calculations were carried out using the tight-binding model in conjunction with the Green's function formalism. These calculations reproduced similar high-conductance contour features observed experimentally. Analysis of the wave functions at points along a straight conductance contour line showed that they were predominantly composed of high-probability distributions forming a chain of scars, arranged in a necklace-like pattern that conforms to the cavity's geometry. The current density distributions at these points exhibited an overarching vortex structure within the cavity. Notably, these characteristics remained consistent despite variations in the magnetic field.

In contrast, the wave function probability distributions and current density distributions at points along a parabolic-like contour line demonstrated sensitivity to increasing magnetic field strength. The current density distributions in these cases were characterized by the emergence of multiple localized vortices within the cavity. 

This investigation provides novel insights into quantum chaos in systems involving relativistic particles and is expected to significantly advance both experimental and theoretical research within this developing area of study.

A graphene sheet, carved into a quantum cavity as if some celestial hand had etched a secret into carbon’s lattice, takes center stage in this tale of relativistic quantum intrigue. Its conductance—how the electrons whisper through—has been scrutinized, their chatter tracked as back gate voltage shifts (the Fermi energy’s restless dance) and a magnetic field presses down like an invisible weight. Patterns emerge, strange and telling, etched into the conductance contours like fingerprints of the quantum unknown. Two types of contour lines—one straight as an arrow’s flight, the other curving like a drawn bow—rise from the data, their high conductance a beacon amid the chaos.

Theory, armed with tight-binding models and Green’s function calculations, marches alongside the experiments, and here, too, the same cryptic contours appear, as if experiment and theory were two mirrors reflecting the same truth. Along the straight contour lines, the wave functions shimmer with a chain of quantum scars—high-probability streaks strung like a necklace around the cavity’s edge, their arrangement echoing the cavity’s shape. The currents swirl within, a single vortex reigning supreme, unmoved by the rising tide of magnetic field strength.

But follow the parabolic-like lines, and the story shifts. Here, the wave functions bend to the whims of the increasing magnetic field. The currents splinter, forming an intricate mosaic of localized vortices, each spinning in its own quantum ballet. These patterns, delicate yet fierce, reveal new layers of quantum chaos, a glimpse into the wild heart of relativistic particle systems. The insights gleaming from this work are a siren call to researchers, a challenge to dive deeper into the uncharted waters of this still-growing field.

Through a synthesis of quantum transport measurements and theoretical modeling, we investigate a relativistic quantum cavity system sculpted from a graphene sheet. By modulating the back gate voltage (equivalently, the Fermi energy) and subjecting the graphene to a perpendicular magnetic field, we probe the conductance landscape of the cavity, revealing distinctive contour patterns. Notably, two archetypes of high-conductance trajectories emerge: linear and parabolic-like contours. These experimental phenomena are mirrored in theoretical simulations constructed via a tight-binding framework coupled with Green's function formalism, reinforcing the fidelity of the observed patterns.

Analysis of the wave functions along a representative straight contour line uncovers a recurring structure: a chain of wavefunction "scars," zones of heightened probability density, arrayed in a necklace-like geometry that traces the cavity's perimeter. This spatial configuration is complemented by current density distributions that coalesce into a singular, overarching vortex within the cavity. Remarkably, these structural and dynamical features remain robust under an intensifying magnetic field, indicating their insensitivity to such perturbations.

In contrast, the situation along parabolic-like conductance contours reveals a distinct magneto-dependence. Here, the probability distributions and current densities evolve in response to increasing magnetic influence, giving rise to intricate patterns of multiple localized vortices within the cavity. These vortices, in their complexity and dynamism, stand in stark opposition to the simplicity of the single-vortex regime observed along straight contours.

This work elucidates new dimensions of quantum chaotic behavior in systems governed by relativistic particle dynamics, offering a conceptual and methodological scaffold that promises to invigorate both experimental inquiry and theoretical innovation within this nascent yet profoundly intriguing domain.

This study provides new perspectives on quantum chaos in relativistic particle systems, highlighting intriguing interplay between wave function morphology and magnetic field effects. These findings are expected to catalyze further experimental and theoretical advancements in this emerging field.

This work offers fresh insights into quantum chaos phenomena in relativistic particle systems, highlighting novel dynamic behaviors under varying conditions. The findings are poised to spark further experimental and theoretical exploration within this evolving domain, shedding light on the intricate interplay of quantum mechanics and relativistic effects.

We investigate a relativistic quantum cavity system crafted from a graphene sheet, employing quantum transport measurements alongside theoretical calculations. The conductance of the graphene cavity has been measured with respect to the back gate voltage (or Fermi energy) and the perpendicular magnetic field applied to the graphene sheet, revealing distinct conductance contour patterns in the experimental data. Notably, two prominent types of high conductance contour lines emerge: straight lines and parabolic-like lines. Theoretical calculations, carried out using a tight-binding approach combined with Green's function formalism, replicate these experimental features. 

Wave functions calculated along a straight conductance contour line exhibit a striking pattern—a chain of high-probability scars arranged like a necklace conforming to the cavity's shape, with the corresponding current density distributions revealing a dominant vortex within the cavity. These attributes persist even as the magnetic field strength increases. Conversely, calculations along a parabolic-like contour line show wave function probabilities and current density distributions that evolve significantly with increasing magnetic field. At these points, the current density reveals a complex interplay of localized vortices within the cavity.

Our findings illuminate novel aspects of quantum chaos in relativistic particle systems, suggesting profound implications for the field. This work not only deepens understanding but also invites further experimental and theoretical exploration into this intriguing and still-developing domain.

We study a relativistic quantum cavity system realized by etching out from a graphene sheet by quantum transport measurements and theoretical calculations. The conductance of the graphene cavity has been measured as a function of the back gate voltage (or the Fermi energy) and the magnetic field applied perpendicular to the graphene sheet, and characteristic conductance contour patterns are observed in the measurements. In particular, two types of high conductance contour lines, i.e., straight and parabolic-like high conductance contour lines, are found in the measurements. The theoretical calculations are performed within the framework of tight-binding approach and Green's function formalism. Similar characteristic high conductance contour features as in the experiments are found in the calculations. The wave functions calculated at points selected along a straight conductance contour line are found to be dominated by a chain of scars of high probability distributions arranged as a necklace following the shape of cavity and the current density distributions calculated at these point are dominated by an overall vortex in the cavity. These characteristics are found to be insensitive to increasing magnetic field. However, the wave function probability distributions and the current density distributions calculated at points selected along a parabolic-like contour line show a clear dependence on increasing magnetic field, and the current density distributions at these points are characterized by the complex formation of several localized vortices in the cavity. Our work brings a new insight into quantum chaos in relativistic particle systems and would greatly stimulate experimental and theoretical efforts towards this still emerging field.

In a daring dance of particles, we plunge into the heart of a relativistic quantum cavity system, carved from the very fabric of a graphene sheet, a symphony of quantum transport measurements and theoretical wizardry. As we crank up the back gate voltage, sculpting the Fermi energy with the precision of a master craftsman, and unleash magnetic fields that slice through the graphene like a sharpened knife, a mesmerizing tapestry of conductance unfurls before our eyes. Those contours, oh those contours! They twist and turn, straight lines and parabolic arcs, each telling a story of high conductance that leaps off the page.

Delving deep into the theoretical abyss, we weave our calculations with the threads of a tight-binding approach and the elegant Green's function formalism. And behold! The contours we see in our experimental wizardry echo back from the calculations, like reflections in a shimmering pool. At points along those straight conductance lines, we discover wave functions alive with chains of scars, probability distributions that cling together like a radiant necklace, tracing the very shape of the cavity. Here, the current density dances, dominated by a swirling vortex, bold and unyielding, impervious to the encroaching magnetic field.

Yet, turn we must to the parabolic contours, where the wave functions and current densities reveal their secrets, morphing and shifting with the magnetic field's rise. A ballet of localized vortices emerges, each one a complex enchantment within the cavity's embrace. This work, a fresh breeze in the realm of quantum chaos, beckons us to explore further, igniting a wildfire of curiosity in both experimental and theoretical realms of this captivating, ever-unfolding field.

Here, the current density is marked by the emergence of multiple localized vortices within the cavity. Our findings provide fresh insights into the realm of quantum chaos in relativistic particle systems, promising to invigorate both experimental and theoretical pursuits in this exciting and evolving field.

The volume of discussions concerning brands within social media provides digital marketers with great opportunities for tracking and analyzing the feelings and views of consumers toward brands, products, influencers, services, and ad campaigns in CGC. The present study aims to assess and compare the performance of firms and celebrities (i.e., influencers that with the experience of being in an ad campaign of those companies) with the automated sentiment analysis that was employed for CGC at social media while exploring the feeling of the consumers toward them to observe which influencer (of two for each company) had a closer effect with the corresponding corporation on consumer minds. For this purpose, several consumer tweets from the pages of brands and influencers were utilized to make a comparison of machine learning and lexicon-based approaches to the sentiment analysis through the Naive algorithm (lexicon-based) and Naive Bayes algorithm (machine learning method) and obtain the desired results to assess the campaigns. The findings suggested that the approaches were dissimilar in terms of accuracy; the machine learning method yielded higher accuracy. Finally, the results showed which influencer was more appropriate according to their existence in previous campaigns and helped choose the right influencer in the future for our company and have a better, more appropriate, and more efficient ad campaign subsequently. It is required to conduct further studies on the accuracy improvement of the sentiment classification. This approach should be employed for other social media CGC types. The results revealed decision-making for which sentiment analysis methods are the best approaches for the analysis of social media. It was also found that companies should be aware of their consumers' sentiments and choose the right person every time they think of a campaign.

The extensive volume of discussions about brands on social media offers digital marketers significant opportunities to monitor and analyze consumer attitudes toward brands, products, influencers, services, and advertising campaigns within the context of consumer-generated content (CGC). This study seeks to evaluate and compare the effectiveness of firms and celebrities—specifically influencers who have participated in advertising campaigns for these companies—using automated sentiment analysis applied to CGC on social media. The analysis aims to examine consumer sentiments toward these entities and identify which of two influencers, for each company, exerted a stronger associative impact on consumer perceptions regarding the respective corporation.

To achieve this objective, consumer tweets sourced from the accounts of brands and influencers were analyzed. A comparative assessment was conducted between machine learning and lexicon-based sentiment analysis methods. Specifically, the study utilized the Naive algorithm (a lexicon-based approach) and the Naive Bayes algorithm (a machine learning approach) to perform the sentiment classification and generate the desired outcomes for campaign evaluation. The findings indicated a disparity in accuracy between these methods, with the machine learning approach yielding superior accuracy in sentiment classification.

The results provided insight into which influencer was more effective based on their participation in prior campaigns, thereby facilitating the selection of optimal influencers for future campaigns. This enables companies to design advertising campaigns that are more appropriate and efficient. The study underscores the need for further research to enhance the accuracy of sentiment classification methods. Additionally, it suggests extending this approach to other types of CGC on social media. The findings emphasize the importance of selecting sentiment analysis methods that are most suitable for analyzing social media content and highlight the necessity for companies to remain informed about consumer sentiments in order to consistently select the most appropriate influencers for their campaigns.

The ceaseless hum of chatter about brands across the wild expanse of social media has handed digital marketers a treasure trove—a kaleidoscope of consumer feelings, opinions, and whims laid bare. Here lies the chance to peer into hearts and minds, to decode how the world perceives brands, products, influencers, services, and ad campaigns crafted in the crucible of consumer-generated content (CGC). This study set out to chart that terrain, measuring and comparing the sway of companies and their celebrity allies—those influencers who’ve lent their faces and names to ad campaigns—through the lens of automated sentiment analysis. The goal? To unravel which of two influencers tied to each company left the deepest imprint on consumer consciousness.

To this end, a torrent of tweets spilling from the accounts of brands and their influencers became the battleground. Two analytical forces were summoned to wrest meaning from the digital noise: the lexicon-driven Naive algorithm and the machine-learning-fueled Naive Bayes algorithm. A head-to-head duel of sentiment analysis followed, each method grappling with consumer emotions to assess the campaigns’ resonance. The verdict? Machine learning emerged victorious, its accuracy outpacing its lexicon-based rival. 

The revelations didn’t stop there. By mapping which influencer struck the truest chord in prior campaigns, the findings lit a path for choosing better allies in future ventures—sharper campaigns, richer connections, and a savvier pulse on public sentiment. Yet, the journey is far from over. The quest to refine sentiment classification demands more exploration, and this method’s reach must stretch to other CGC realms. The study’s insights made one thing vividly clear: brands must heed the emotional tides of their audiences and pick their champions wisely, campaign after campaign, lest they falter in the ever-churning arena of social media.

The expansive discourse surrounding brands within the labyrinthine ecosystems of social media furnishes digital marketers with a formidable arsenal for discerning and quantifying consumer sentiment. This sentiment—encompassing attitudes toward brands, products, influencers, services, and advertising initiatives embedded within consumer-generated content (CGC)—represents a pivotal axis for strategic analysis. The present inquiry endeavors to evaluate and juxtapose the operational efficacy of corporate entities and public figures (influencers who have participated in these corporations’ advertising campaigns) by deploying automated sentiment analysis methodologies tailored to CGC. The overarching objective is to illuminate the contours of consumer affectivity and to discern which of two influencers per corporation exerts a more resonant impact upon the collective mental schema of consumers vis-à-vis the corresponding brand.

To this end, a corpus of consumer tweets, harvested from the digital loci of both brands and influencers, was subjected to a comparative analytical framework. This framework contrasted lexicon-based sentiment analysis, operationalized through the Naive algorithm, with machine learning methodologies exemplified by the Naive Bayes algorithm. The evaluative metrics revealed a pronounced divergence in accuracy; the machine learning paradigm demonstrated superior precision. The resultant data not only elucidated the influencer whose prior campaign participation yielded the most symbiotic brand alignment but also provided a heuristic for optimizing future influencer selection, thereby enhancing the strategic efficacy of advertising campaigns.

The study underscores the necessity of further refinement in sentiment classification techniques to augment their precision. It advocates for the extension of such analytical methodologies to encompass a broader spectrum of CGC modalities across diverse social media platforms. Ultimately, the findings serve as a decision-making lodestar, delineating the relative merits of competing sentiment analysis paradigms and underscoring the imperative for corporations to remain attuned to the affective currents of their consumer base. In so doing, they can judiciously curate their choice of influencers to ensure congruence with campaign objectives and maximize communicative resonance.

Supervised machine learning methods usually require a large set of labeled examples for model training. However, in many real applications, there are plentiful unlabeled data but limited labeled data; and the acquisition of labels is costly. Active learning (AL) reduces the labeling cost by iteratively selecting the most valuable data to query their labels from the annotator. This article introduces a Python toobox ALiPy for active learning. ALiPy provides a module based implementation of active learning framework, which allows users to conveniently evaluate, compare and analyze the performance of active learning methods. In the toolbox, multiple options are available for each component of the learning framework, including data process, active selection, label query, results visualization, etc. In addition to the implementations of more than 20 state-of-the-art active learning algorithms, ALiPy also supports users to easily configure and implement their own approaches under different active learning settings, such as AL for multi-label data, AL with noisy annotators, AL with different costs and so on. The toolbox is well-documented and open-source on Github, and can be easily installed through PyPI.

Supervised machine learning techniques typically necessitate extensive sets of labeled examples for effective model training. Nevertheless, in numerous practical scenarios, labeled data are scarce while unlabeled data are abundant, and obtaining labels often entails significant costs. Active learning (AL) addresses this issue by iteratively identifying and selecting the most informative data points for labeling by an annotator, thereby reducing labeling expenses. This article presents ALiPy, a Python-based toolbox specifically designed for active learning. ALiPy implements a modular active learning framework that facilitates the evaluation, comparison, and analysis of various active learning methods. The toolbox offers multiple configurable options for each component of the framework, including data processing, active selection strategies, label querying mechanisms, and result visualization tools. Furthermore, it incorporates implementations of over 20 advanced active learning algorithms. ALiPy also enables users to efficiently customize and develop their own approaches for diverse active learning contexts, such as multi-label scenarios, handling noisy annotations, and managing varying labeling costs. The toolbox is open-source, documented comprehensively, and available on GitHub. Installation is streamlined via PyPI.

Supervised machine learning needs a lot of labeled examples to train models. But in real-life situations, there’s often plenty of unlabeled data and only a small amount of labeled data. Getting labels can be expensive. Active learning (AL) helps lower this cost by choosing the most useful data and asking for labels for just those pieces. This article introduces ALiPy, a Python toolbox designed for active learning. ALiPy offers a modular setup, making it easy for users to test, compare, and study how active learning methods perform.

Supervised machine learning, that tireless beast of pattern and prediction, thrives on feasts of labeled data—heaps of it, mountains of examples painstakingly tagged for its insatiable appetite. Yet, in the messy sprawl of real-world applications, such abundance is rare. Instead, we’re surrounded by oceans of unlabeled data, vast and untamed, while labeled examples trickle in like water from a stubborn faucet. And let’s not forget: acquiring those precious labels isn’t cheap—it’s a slow, costly dance with human annotators. Enter active learning (AL), the clever strategist that trims the fat, slashing labeling costs by homing in on the most valuable data points, asking for labels only where it truly counts.

Meet ALiPy, a Python toolbox built to tame this process. ALiPy offers a modular playground for active learning, where users can test, tweak, and scrutinize a host of AL strategies with ease. With tools for preprocessing data, selecting which examples to query, fetching labels, and even visualizing results, it’s a one-stop shop. Over 20 cutting-edge algorithms come preloaded, but the real magic lies in its flexibility. Whether wrangling multi-label data, navigating noisy annotators, or juggling varied costs, ALiPy lets users craft their own solutions. Open-source, well-documented, and installable via PyPI, it’s a powerful ally for those chasing smarter machine learning.

This paper presents ALiPy, a Python-based toolbox meticulously designed to operationalize active learning frameworks. ALiPy thus serves as both a robust platform for theoretical exploration and a practical instrument for advancing active learning research.

Static properties of multiskyrmions with baryon numbers up to 8 are calculated, including momenta of inertia and sigma-term. The calculations are based on the recently suggested SU(2) rational map ansaetze. Minimization with the help of SU(3) variational minimization program shows that these configurations become local minima in SU(3) configuration space. The B-number dependence of the so called flavour moment of inertia of multiskyrmions playing an important role in the quantization procedure is close to the linear one. The spectra of baryonic systems with strangeness, charm and bottom are considered within a "rigid oscillator" version of the bound state soliton model. The binding energies estimates are made for the states with largest isospin which can appear as negatively charged nuclear fragments, as well as for states with zero isospin - light fragments of "flavoured" nuclear matter. Our results confirm the previously made observation that baryonic systems with charm or bottom quantum numbers have more chance to be stable with respect to strong interactions than strange baryonic systems.

The static properties of multiskyrmions with baryon numbers ranging up to 8 have been calculated, including their moments of inertia and sigma-term. These calculations utilize the recently proposed SU(2) rational map ansatz. The application of an SU(3) variational minimization program demonstrates that these configurations constitute local minima within the SU(3) configuration space. The dependence of the flavor moment of inertia of multiskyrmions on the baryon number, a factor critical to the quantization process, is observed to be approximately linear. The energy spectra of baryonic systems incorporating strangeness, charm, and bottom quantum numbers are analyzed using a "rigid oscillator" variant of the bound-state soliton model. Binding energy estimates are provided for states with maximum isospin, which could manifest as negatively charged nuclear fragments, as well as for states with zero isospin, corresponding to light fragments of flavored nuclear matter. The results corroborate prior findings, indicating that baryonic systems possessing charm or bottom quantum numbers are more likely to exhibit stability against strong interactions compared to systems with strangeness quantum numbers.

These computations are grounded in the framework of the recently proposed SU(2) rational map ansätze. Employing an SU(3) variational minimization algorithm, the analysis reveals that these configurations stabilize as local minima within the multidimensional SU(3) configuration landscape. Of particular note is the nearly linear dependence of the so-called flavor moment of inertia on the baryon number, a feature of critical import in the quantization schema. These findings corroborate prior observations, substantiating that baryonic systems containing charm or bottom quantum numbers exhibit a greater propensity for stability under strong interaction dynamics when contrasted with their strange baryonic counterparts.

This paper provides a detailed characterization of the Hubbard model defined on a square lattice with nearest-neighbor transfer integral $t$, on-site repulsion $U$, and $N_a^2\gg 1$ sites. The description adheres to the model's exact global $SO(3)	imes SO(3)	imes U(1)$ symmetry. The analysis leverages the recently identified global symmetry of the Hubbard model on bipartite lattices, combined with the transformation properties of a specific electron-to-rotated-electron unitary mapping applied to selected operators and quantum entities. For values of $U/4t>0$, the energy eigenstates within the one- and two-electron subspace are determined by the occupancy configurations of these entities. This subspace, as defined herein, encapsulates almost all spectral weight associated with the excitations produced by one- and two-electron operators acting on the zero-spin-density ground state.

The framework introduced employs three fundamental types of quantum objects: charge $c$ fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and $\eta$-spin-1/2 $\eta$-spinons. Analogous to the case of quarks in chromodynamics—which possess color charge but form color-neutral composite particles—the $\eta$-spinons and spinons are characterized by $\eta$ spin 1/2 and spin 1/2, respectively, but combine to form $\eta$-spin-neutral and spin-neutral composite particles. Specifically, these are $2
u$-$\eta$-spinon and $2
u$-spinon composite $\eta
u$ fermions and $s
u$ fermions, where $
u=1,2,...$ represents the number of $\eta$-spinon or spinon pairs.

The description is compatible with a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state exhibiting antiferromagnetic long-range order at half filling ($x=0$ hole concentration) and a ground state with short-range spin correlations for certain finite hole concentrations ($x>0$).

Picture this: the Hubbard model sprawled across a vast square lattice, its architecture built on nearest-neighbor hopping with transfer integral \( t \), its foundation rooted in the on-site repulsion \( U \), and its domain extending to \( N_a^2 \gg 1 \) sites—an expansive playground governed by a precise global symmetry of \( SO(3) \times SO(3) \times U(1) \). Here, symmetry isn’t just a quiet backdrop; it’s the heartbeat, the pulse animating the interplay between electrons and their enigmatic doppelgängers, the rotated electrons, all tangled in a web of unitary transformation laws. 

For \( U/4t > 0 \), a strange alchemy unfolds. Quantum objects emerge, their occupancy configurations weaving the fabric of energy eigenstates, crafting the one- and two-electron subspace—a realm that nearly drinks deep from the spectral fountain of excitations stirred by one- and two-electron operators acting upon the zero-spin-density ground state. The cast of players in this unfolding drama? Charge \( c \) fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and their mirror-like counterparts, the \( \eta \)-spin-1/2 \( \eta \)-spinons. Yet these objects, like quarks in the stormy seas of chromodynamics, refuse to stand alone. Their individual spins—bright and unyielding—are tethered into composite particles: \( \eta\nu \) fermions and \( s\nu \) fermions, neutral entities born from pairs of \( \eta \)-spinons or spinons, their number \( \nu \) marching steadily upward.

This picture doesn’t just sketch a lattice of physics; it maps a landscape of behavior. At half-filling, where the hole concentration \( x = 0 \), the ground state crystallizes into a Mott-Hubbard insulator, its antiferromagnetic order stretching long and true. Shift the balance, let \( x > 0 \), and the order softens, yielding a ground state where spin aligns only in fleeting whispers, its range short and delicate.

This study articulates a formal exposition of the Hubbard model defined on a square lattice with nearest-neighbor hopping amplitude $t$, on-site Coulomb repulsion $U$, and a lattice of $N_a^2 \gg 1$ sites, rigorously respecting its intrinsic global symmetry group $SO(3) 	imes SO(3) 	imes U(1)$. The analysis leverages the synergy between the recently uncovered global symmetry of the model on bipartite lattices and the transformation properties of a specific unitary mapping that interrelates conventional electrons and rotated electrons, alongside a carefully chosen ensemble of operators and quantum entities. For the regime $U/4t > 0$, the structural configurations of these quantum objects delineate the energy eigenstates that constitute the one- and two-electron subspace. This subspace, as specified herein, encapsulates the preponderant portion of the spectral weight associated with excitations generated by the action of one- and two-electron operators upon the zero-spin-density ground state.

The framework elucidated here employs three foundational entities: charge $c$ fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and $\eta$-spin-1/2 $\eta$-spinons. Analogous to the color charge of quarks in quantum chromodynamics—where only color-neutral composites manifest as physical particles—the $\eta$-spinons and spinons, which individually possess $\eta$-spin 1/2 and spin 1/2 respectively, amalgamate into $\eta$-spin-neutral ($2
u$-$\eta$-spinon) and spin-neutral ($2
u$-spinon) composite fermions. These are denoted as $\eta
u$ fermions and $s
u$ fermions, where $
u = 1, 2, \ldots$ represents the number of $\eta$-spinon or spinon pairs comprising the composite entity.

The theoretical construct advanced here coheres with the emergence of a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state exhibiting antiferromagnetic long-range order at half-filling ($x = 0$ hole concentration) and a ground state characterized by short-range spin correlations over a defined interval of finite hole concentrations $x > 0$.

This paper presents a detailed description of the Hubbard model on a square lattice characterized by a nearest-neighbor transfer integral denoted as $t$, an on-site repulsion represented by $U$, and a substantial number of lattice sites, specifically $N_a^2 \gg 1$, which aligns with its exact global symmetry of $SO(3) 	imes SO(3) 	imes U(1)$. Our analysis benefits from the recently identified global symmetry applicable to the model on any bipartite lattice, in conjunction with the transformation properties associated with a well-defined set of operators and quantum objects under a suitable electron-rotated-electron unitary transformation. For the condition $U/4t > 0$, the occupancy configurations of these quantum objects yield energy eigenstates that encompass the one-electron and two-electron subspace. This subspace, as articulated in this paper, contains nearly all of the spectral weight of the excitations produced by applying one- and two-electron operators to the zero-spin-density ground state. Our framework includes three fundamental entities: charge $c$ fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and $\eta$-spin-1/2 $\eta$-spinons. Similar to the situation in chromodynamics where quarks possess color yet all quark-composite particles are color-neutral, the $\eta$-spinons (and spinons) that do not remain invariant under the aforementioned transformation have $\eta$ spin 1/2 (and spin 1/2) but belong to $\eta$-spin-neutral (and spin-neutral) composite $\eta
u$ fermions (and $s
u$ fermions) defined by pairs of $\eta$-spinons (and spinons), where $
u = 1, 2, \ldots$ represents the number of such pairs. The framework introduced in this paper is consistent with a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state exhibiting antiferromagnetic long-range order at half filling, specifically at a hole concentration of $x = 0$, and a ground state characterized by short-range spin order for a defined range of finite hole concentrations where $x > 0$.

In this vibrant tapestry of theoretical exploration, we unfurl the Hubbard model across a square lattice, where nearest-neighbor transfer dances to the rhythm of a transfer integral $t$ and an on-site repulsion $U$. Picture this: a sprawling expanse of $N_a^2\gg 1$ sites, each resonating with the exact, harmonious global symmetry of $SO(3)\times SO(3)\times U(1)$. Our journey thrives on the dazzling interplay of this newfound global symmetry, a shimmering thread woven through the fabric of any bipartite lattice, entwined with the transformation laws that emerge from a deft electron-rotated-electron unitary transformation—an elegant pirouette of operators and quantum entities. 

When the ratio $U/4t>0$ springs to life, the occupancy configurations of our quantum players morph into energy eigenstates that pulse through the one- and two-electron subspace. This subspace, a treasure trove of spectral weight, captures the very essence of excitations birthed from the gentle caress of one- and two-electron operators upon the zero-spin-density ground state. 

At the heart of our narrative lie three essential characters: the charge $c$ fermions, the half-integer spinons, and the $\eta$-spin-1/2 $\eta$-spinons. Like quarks in the swirling maelstrom of chromodynamics, these entities bear their own colors, yet all quark-composite physical particles emerge as color-neutral. The $\eta$-spinons, not invariant under transformation, flaunt their $\eta$ spin of 1/2, weaving into the tapestry of $\eta$-spin-neutral $2\nu$-$\eta$-spinon composites and $s\nu$ fermions—where $\nu=1,2,...$ denotes the number of $\eta$-spinon pairs. Our vivid description aligns beautifully with the Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state, showcasing antiferromagnetic long-range order at half filling, and a symphony of short-range spin order for a rich array of finite hole concentrations $x>0$.

This paper presents a detailed description of the Hubbard model on a square lattice, characterized by a nearest-neighbor transfer integral $t$, an on-site repulsion $U$, and a lattice size of $N_a^2\gg 1$. The analysis leverages the recently established global symmetry of the model, specifically the $SO(3)\times SO(3)\times U(1)$ symmetry, applicable to any bipartite lattice. This symmetry interacts with the transformation laws related to a defined electron - rotated-electron unitary transformation involving a specific set of operators and quantum entities.

For the condition $U/4t>0$, the occupancy configurations of these quantum objects yield the energy eigenstates that form the basis for the one- and two-electron subspace. This subspace, as outlined herein, captures nearly the entire spectral weight of the excitations produced by applying one- and two-electron operators to the zero-spin-density ground state. The framework discusses three principal entities: charge $c$ fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and $\eta$-spin-1/2 $\eta$-spinons. Drawing an analogy to chromodynamics, where quarks exhibit color but all quark-composite physical particles are color-neutral, the $\eta$-spinons (and spinons) that do not remain invariant under that transformation possess $\eta$ spin 1/2 (and spin 1/2). They contribute to $\eta$-spin-neutral (and spin-neutral) composite $\eta\nu$ fermions (and $s\nu$ fermions), with $\nu=1,2,...$ indicating the number of $\eta$-spinon (and spinon) pairs.

The description provided aligns with a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state exhibiting antiferromagnetic long-range order at half filling, specifically at a hole concentration of $x=0$, and a ground state displaying short-range spin order within a defined range of finite hole concentrations $x>0$.

This paper presents a detailed exploration of the Hubbard model on a square lattice characterized by a nearest-neighbor transfer integral \( t \) and an on-site repulsion \( U \), particularly for a situation where the number of sites \( N_a^2 \) is significantly larger than one. We leverage the intriguing global symmetry of the model, which adheres to \( SO(3) \times SO(3) \times U(1) \), observed on any bipartite lattice. This symmetry is illuminated through a suitable electron-rotated-electron unitary transformation that applies to a specific set of operators and quantum objects.

For values where \( U/4t > 0 \), the occupancy configurations of these quantum objects yield energy eigenstates that effectively cover the one- and two-electron subspace. This subspace, as defined herein, encompasses a substantial portion of the spectral weight associated with excitations produced when one- and two-electron operators act on the zero-spin-density ground state.

Our framework identifies three fundamental entities: charge \( c \) fermions, spin-1/2 spinons, and \( \eta \)-spin-1/2 \( \eta \)-spinons. In a manner reminiscent of chromodynamics, while quarks possess color, all quark-composite physical particles maintain color neutrality. The \( \eta \)-spinon (and spinons) that do not remain invariant under transformation possess \( \eta \) spin 1/2 (and spin 1/2) but are components of \( \eta \)-spin-neutral (and spin-neutral) \( 2\nu \)-\( \eta \)-spinon (and \( 2\nu \)-spinon) composite \( \eta\nu \) fermions (and \( s\nu \) fermions), with \( \nu = 1, 2, \ldots \) indicating the number of \( \eta \)-spinon (and spinon) pairs.

The framework established aligns with a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state exhibiting antiferromagnetic long-range order at half-filling (\( x=0 \) hole concentration) and a ground state with short-range spin order across a defined range of finite hole concentrations (\( x>0 \)).

u=1,2,...\) representing the number of \(\eta\)-spinon (and spinon) pairs. The model's description is compatible with a Mott-Hubbard insulating ground state that exhibits antiferromagnetic long-range order at half filling, specifically at \(x=0\) hole concentration, and a ground state showcasing short-range spin order across a defined range of finite hole concentrations \(x>0\).

In this paper, we consider sequential dynamic team decision problems with nonclassical information structures. First, we address the problem from the point of view of a "manager" who seeks to derive the optimal strategy of the team in a centralized process. We derive structural results that yield an information state for the team which does not depend on the control strategy, and thus it can lead to a dynamic programming decomposition where the optimization problem is over the space of the team's decisions. We, then, derive structural results for each team member that yield an information state which does not depend on their control strategy, and thus it can lead to a dynamic programming decomposition where the optimization problem for each team member is over the space of their decisions. Finally, we show that the control strategy of each team member is the same as the one derived by the manager. We present an illustrative example of a dynamic team with a delayed sharing information structure.

This paper examines sequential dynamic team decision-making problems characterized by nonclassical information structures. The analysis begins by considering the perspective of a "manager" tasked with determining the team’s optimal strategy within a centralized framework. Structural results are derived, establishing an information state for the team that remains independent of the control strategy. This facilitates a dynamic programming decomposition wherein the optimization problem is confined to the space of the team’s collective decisions. Subsequently, structural results are also developed for individual team members. These results define an information state for each team member that is similarly independent of their respective control strategies, enabling a dynamic programming decomposition in which the optimization problem pertains solely to the space of individual decisions. It is further demonstrated that the control strategy adopted by each team member aligns with the strategy determined by the manager. The paper concludes with an illustrative example involving a dynamic team operating under a delayed sharing information structure.

This study interrogates the intricate domain of sequential dynamic team decision problems, particularly those governed by nonclassical information architectures. The inquiry begins from the vantage point of a hypothetical "manager," an overseeing entity tasked with orchestrating the team's optimal strategy through a centralized framework. Within this paradigm, we derive structural principles that crystallize an information state for the collective—a construct intriguingly insulated from dependence on specific control strategies. Such independence enables a dynamic programming formulation wherein the optimization unfolds over the decision space of the team as a whole.

Subsequently, we pivot to the perspective of individual team members, deducing analogous structural properties that yield personalized information states similarly unaffected by their control strategies. This result facilitates a parallel dynamic programming decomposition, now localized to the decision spaces of the individual participants. Notably, the control strategies synthesized from this decentralized perspective converge precisely with those obtained through the centralized managerial approach.

To concretize these theoretical constructs, we furnish an illustrative scenario: a dynamic team operating within a delayed sharing information structure. This example elucidates the practical implications of our findings, demonstrating the unifying symmetry between the centralized and decentralized optimization processes under the proposed framework.

Shifting focus, the research unveils parallel structural insights for individual team members, demonstrating that each member’s information state similarly transcends reliance on their control strategy. This alignment enables dynamic programming decomposition for individuals, narrowing optimization to their specific decision spaces. Ultimately, the study establishes a striking harmony: the control strategies derived independently by team members align precisely with the manager’s overarching strategy. To ground these findings, an illustrative example explores a dynamic team operating under a delayed sharing information structure, showcasing the practical implications of this methodical synthesis.

We investigate the evolution of intracluster medium during a cluster merger, explicitly considering the relaxation process between the ions and electrons by N-body and hydrodynamical simulations. When two subclusters collide each other, a bow shock is formed between the centers of two substructures and propagate in both directions along the collision axis. The shock primarily heats the ions because the kinetic energy of an ion entering the shock is larger than that of an electron by the ratio of masses. In the post-shock region the energy is transported from the ions to electrons via Coulomb coupling. However, since the energy exchange timescale depends both on the gas density and temperature, distribution of electron temperature becomes more complex than that of the plasma mean temperature, especially in the expanding phase. After the collision of two subclusters, gas outflow occurs not only along the collision axis but also in its perpendicular direction. The gas which is originally located in the central part of the subclusters moves both in the parallel and perpendicular directions. Since the equilibrium timescale of the gas along these directions is relatively short, temperature difference between ions and electrons is larger in the directions tilted by the angles of $\pm 45^\circ$ with respect to the collision axis. The electron temperature could be significantly lower that the plasma mean temperature by $\sim 50 %$ at most. The significance of our results in the interpretation of X-ray observations is briefly discussed.

This study examines the behavior of the intracluster medium during the merger of galaxy clusters, with particular attention to the relaxation process between ions and electrons. The analysis is conducted using N-body and hydrodynamical simulations. When two subclusters collide, a bow shock forms between their centers and propagates in both directions along the axis of collision. This shock primarily heats the ions because the kinetic energy of an ion encountering the shock exceeds that of an electron by the ratio of their masses. In the region behind the shock, energy transfer occurs from ions to electrons through Coulomb interactions. However, since the timescale for this energy exchange is influenced by both gas density and temperature, the distribution of electron temperature becomes more intricate than that of the mean plasma temperature, particularly during the expansion phase.

Following the collision, gas outflows are observed not only along the axis of collision but also in directions perpendicular to it. Gas initially located in the central regions of the subclusters moves in both parallel and perpendicular directions. Due to the relatively short equilibration timescale for gas along these directions, the temperature difference between ions and electrons is more pronounced in directions tilted at angles of approximately ±45° relative to the collision axis. In these regions, the electron temperature may be significantly lower than the mean plasma temperature, with differences reaching up to approximately 50%. The implications of these findings for the interpretation of X-ray observations are briefly addressed.

When two cosmic titans—subclusters of galaxies—slam into each other in the vast dark ballet of the universe, the invisible sea between them, the intracluster medium, shudders and transforms. A bow shock, sharp as a blade, slices through the plasma, erupting between the cores of the colliding giants and racing outward along the axis of their clash. This shock is no gentle ripple—it roars with energy, heating ions far more fiercely than electrons, for each ion carries the heft of its greater mass, barreling into the shock with a kinetic force electrons cannot match.

But the story doesn’t end in the heat of the initial impact. Behind the shock’s fiery front, the ions, now energized, begin to share their spoils with the electrons. This energy handoff, mediated by Coulomb coupling, unfolds in a drawn-out, intricate dance, its pace dictated by the density and temperature of the gas. And as the plasma expands and cools, the electron temperatures stray from the neat simplicity of the overall plasma average, creating a patchwork of thermal complexity.

Once the subclusters collide, the chaos spills outward—not just along the collision axis but sideways too, in broad, defiant arcs. Gas from the subclusters’ hearts flows in all directions, parallel and perpendicular alike, a turbulent dispersal. Yet equilibrium comes more swiftly along these paths, leaving a curious thermal echo: electrons lagging behind ions, their temperatures trailing lower by as much as 50%. This difference peaks in diagonal sweeps, tilted at angles of ±45° from the collision’s spine, a thermal asymmetry that whispers secrets to the keen eyes of X-ray observatories.

We study what happens to the gas inside galaxy clusters when two clusters crash into each other, focusing on how the energy between ions and electrons balances out. To do this, we use computer simulations that mimic the movement and interaction of particles and gas. When the two clusters collide, a bow-shaped shock forms between their centers and spreads out in both directions along the collision line. This shock heats the ions more than the electrons because ions are much heavier and carry more energy when they hit the shock.

After the shock passes, the ions slowly share their energy with the electrons through interactions called Coulomb coupling. But this energy transfer doesn’t happen evenly—it depends on the density and temperature of the gas. As a result, the temperature of the electrons becomes more complicated than the average temperature of the gas, especially as the shock spreads out.

Once the two clusters collide, the gas doesn’t just move along the collision line; it also flows outward in other directions. Gas that was near the centers of the clusters spreads both parallel and perpendicular to the collision line. The gas in directions tilted at angles of about 45 degrees from the collision line takes longer to reach equilibrium, so the temperature difference between ions and electrons is bigger there. In some places, the electrons can be up to 50% cooler than the average gas temperature.

These findings could help explain certain patterns seen in X-ray images of galaxy clusters, where gas temperatures are often measured.

This study examines the evolution of the intracluster medium during cluster mergers, focusing on the relaxation process between ions and electrons through N-body and hydrodynamical simulations. When two subclusters collide, a bow shock forms between their centers and propagates bidirectionally along the collision axis. The shock predominantly heats ions, as their kinetic energy entering the shock exceeds that of electrons by the mass ratio. In the post-shock region, energy is transferred from ions to electrons via Coulomb coupling. However, since the energy exchange timescale is influenced by both gas density and temperature, the electron temperature distribution becomes more intricate than that of the plasma mean temperature, particularly during the expansion phase.

Following the subcluster collision, gas outflows occur not only along the collision axis but also perpendicularly. Gas initially located in the subclusters' central regions moves in both parallel and perpendicular directions. Due to relatively short equilibrium timescales along these directions, the temperature disparity between ions and electrons is most pronounced at angles of ±45° relative to the collision axis. In such regions, electron temperatures may be significantly lower than the plasma mean temperature, potentially by as much as ~50%. The implications of these findings for interpreting X-ray observations are briefly addressed.

Our findings pulse with significance, illuminating the shadows of X-ray observations, beckoning a deeper understanding of this cosmic symphony.

The implications of these findings for the interpretation of X-ray observations are briefly considered.

We explore the evolution of the intracluster medium during a cluster merger, focusing on the relaxation process between ions and electrons through N-body and hydrodynamical simulations. When two subclusters collide, a bow shock develops between their centers, propagating in both directions along the collision axis. This shock primarily heats the ions, as the kinetic energy of an ion entering the shock exceeds that of an electron due to their mass ratio. In the post-shock region, energy is transferred from ions to electrons via Coulomb coupling. However, the timescale for energy exchange varies with gas density and temperature, leading to a more complex distribution of electron temperature compared to the plasma mean temperature, particularly during the expansion phase. Following the collision, gas outflows occur not only along the collision axis but also perpendicularly. Gas originally located in the central regions of the subclusters moves in both parallel and perpendicular directions. Given the relatively short equilibrium timescale of the gas in these directions, the temperature difference between ions and electrons is notably larger at angles of approximately ±45° relative to the collision axis. In some cases, the electron temperature can be significantly lower than the plasma mean temperature, by up to around 50%. We briefly discuss the implications of our findings for interpreting X-ray observations.

We investigate multi-spacecraft observations of the January 17, 2010 solar energetic particle event. Energetic electrons and protons have been observed over a remarkable large longitudinal range at the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO suggesting a longitudinal spread of nearly 360 degrees at 1AU. The flaring active region, which was on the backside of the Sun as seen from Earth, was separated by more than 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of each of the three spacecraft. The event is characterized by strongly delayed energetic particle onsets with respect to the flare and only small or no anisotropies in the intensity measurements at all three locations. The presence of a coronal shock is evidenced by the observation of a type II radio burst from the Earth and STEREO B. In order to describe the observations in terms of particle transport in the interplanetary medium, including perpendicular diffusion, a 1D model describing the propagation along a magnetic field line (model 1) (Dr\"oge, 2003) and the 3D propagation model (model 2) by (Dr\"oge et al., 2010) including perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium have been applied, respectively. While both models are capable of reproducing the observations, model 1 requires injection functions at the Sun of several hours. Model 2, which includes lateral transport in the solar wind, reveals high values for the ratio of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. Because we do not find evidence for unusual long injection functions at the Sun we favor a scenario with strong perpendicular transport in the interplanetary medium as explanation for the observations.

This study examines multi-spacecraft observations of the solar energetic particle event on January 17, 2010. Observations of energetic electrons and protons were recorded across an extensive longitudinal range by the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO, indicating a nearly 360-degree longitudinal distribution at 1 AU. The active region responsible for the flare was located on the far side of the Sun relative to Earth and was separated by over 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of all three spacecraft. The event exhibited significantly delayed onsets of energetic particles relative to the flare, along with minimal or no anisotropies in intensity measurements at all three spacecraft locations. 

Evidence of a coronal shock was provided by the detection of a type II radio burst by both Earth-based instruments and STEREO B. To interpret these observations within the framework of particle transport in the interplanetary medium, including the effects of perpendicular diffusion, two models were utilized. The first model (Dröge, 2003) describes particle propagation along a single magnetic field line in one dimension. The second model (Dröge et al., 2010) incorporates three-dimensional propagation and accounts for perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium. Both models were able to replicate the observations; however, model 1 necessitated injection functions at the Sun spanning several hours. In contrast, model 2, which includes lateral transport in the solar wind, indicated high ratios of perpendicular to parallel diffusion.

Since there is no evidence to support the presence of unusually prolonged injection functions at the Sun, the observations are more consistently explained by a scenario involving strong perpendicular transport of particles in the interplanetary medium.

On January 17, 2010, a solar energetic particle event unfolded, marked by strikingly widespread observations of energetic electrons and protons across an expansive longitudinal range—almost 360 degrees at 1 AU—via the STEREO spacecraft and SOHO. The flaring active region responsible for this phenomenon was positioned on the far side of the Sun relative to Earth, separated by over 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of the three observing spacecraft. The event’s defining features include significantly delayed energetic particle onsets relative to the flare and minimal or absent anisotropies in intensity measurements at all observational points. Evidence of a coronal shock emerges from the detection of a type II radio burst observed from Earth and STEREO B.

To interpret these observations through the lens of particle transport within the interplanetary medium, two models were employed. The first, a 1D propagation model (Dröge, 2003), describes movement along magnetic field lines, while the second, a 3D model (Dröge et al., 2010), incorporates perpendicular diffusion within the solar wind. Both models successfully reproduce the data; however, model 1 necessitates prolonged injection functions at the Sun, lasting several hours. Conversely, model 2 highlights robust lateral transport, indicating elevated ratios of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. Given the lack of evidence for unusually lengthy injection functions at the Sun, the observations are best explained by a scenario dominated by strong perpendicular transport mechanisms in the interplanetary medium, offering a compelling perspective on the event’s dynamics.

We analyze multi-spacecraft observations of the solar energetic particle event on January 17, 2010. Energetic electrons and protons were detected across an extraordinary longitudinal range at the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO, indicating a nearly 360-degree longitudinal spread at 1 AU. The flaring active region, positioned on the Sun's far side relative to Earth, was separated by over 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of all three spacecraft. Notably, the event exhibited significantly delayed energetic particle onsets relative to the flare and displayed minimal or no anisotropies in intensity measurements at all observation points. Evidence of a coronal shock is supported by the detection of a type II radio burst observed from both Earth and STEREO B.

To interpret the observations within the framework of particle transport through the interplanetary medium, we applied two models. The first is a 1D model describing propagation along a magnetic field line (model 1) (Dröge, 2003), while the second is a 3D propagation model (model 2) (Dröge et al., 2010), which incorporates perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium. Both models successfully replicate the observed data; however, model 1 necessitates injection functions at the Sun lasting several hours. By contrast, model 2, which accounts for lateral particle transport within the solar wind, reveals elevated ratios of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. Given the absence of evidence supporting unusually prolonged injection functions at the Sun, we favor a scenario in which strong perpendicular transport within the interplanetary medium provides the most plausible explanation for the observations.

We explore multi-spacecraft data from the solar energetic particle event of January 17, 2010, revealing an exceptional longitudinal spread of energetic electrons and protons—nearly 360 degrees at 1 AU—detected by the STEREO spacecraft and SOHO. The flaring active region, positioned on the Sun's far side from Earth's perspective, was over 100 degrees in longitude away from the magnetic footpoints of all three spacecraft. Evidence of a coronal shock is supported by the detection of a type II radio burst recorded by Earth and STEREO B.

To interpret these observations in terms of particle transport through the interplanetary medium, we applied two models: a 1D propagation model along a magnetic field line (model 1; Dröge, 2003) and a 3D propagation model (model 2; Dröge et al., 2010) incorporating perpendicular diffusion. Both models successfully replicate the observations, but model 1 necessitates injection functions at the Sun spanning several hours. In contrast, model 2, which accounts for lateral transport in the solar wind, suggests a high perpendicular-to-parallel diffusion ratio. As there is no evidence supporting unusually prolonged injection functions at the Sun, we lean toward a scenario dominated by robust perpendicular transport in the interplanetary medium to explain the data.

To interpret these observations within the framework of particle transport through the interplanetary medium, two models were employed. Given the absence of compelling evidence for unusually extended injection functions at the Sun, we lean toward a scenario dominated by robust perpendicular transport in the interplanetary medium to explain these phenomena.

We investigate multi-spacecraft observations of the January 17, 2010 solar energetic particle event. Energetic electrons and protons have been observed over a remarkable large longitudinal range at the two STEREO spacecraft and SOHO suggesting a longitudinal spread of nearly 360 degrees at 1AU. The flaring active region, which was on the backside of the Sun as seen from Earth, was separated by more than 100 degrees in longitude from the magnetic footpoints of each of the three spacecraft. The event is characterized by strongly delayed energetic particle onsets with respect to the flare and only small or no anisotropies in the intensity measurements at all three locations. The presence of a coronal shock is evidenced by the observation of a type II radio burst from the Earth and STEREO B. In order to describe the observations in terms of particle transport in the interplanetary medium, including perpendicular diffusion, a 1D model describing the propagation along a magnetic field line (model 1) (Dr\"oge, 2003) and the 3D propagation model (model 2) by (Dr\"oge et al., 2010) including perpendicular diffusion in the interplanetary medium have been applied, respectively. While both models are capable of reproducing the observations, model 1 requires injection functions at the Sun of several hours. Model 2, which includes lateral transport in the solar wind, reveals high values for the ratio of perpendicular to parallel diffusion. Because we do not find evidence for unusual long injection functions at the Sun we favor a scenario with strong perpendicular transport in the interplanetary medium as explanation for the observations.
