Ah, the sentimentalists! The grand majority, the easy dreamers who dance lightly over the rugged terrain of social questions, painting rosy pictures while the foundations tremble below. Just as in the intricate machinery of the human body, where the dance of normal physiology presents a labyrinth of challenges, so too does the heart of society pulse with complexity. We grasp at the tendrils of knowledge, but oh, how scanty it is! Most folks cling to the shadows of diseases and remedies, like moths to a flickering flame, while the true essence of society remains shrouded in mist.

Peering into the architecture and pulse of societal organs is a daunting expedition, akin to scaling a craggy mountain, and we’ve barely grazed the surface of this vast landscape. Our hopes of uncovering the hidden treasures buried within can only be realized through a meticulous pilgrimage through history’s winding corridors and a careful dissection of our institutions. I dare say, no discipline—save the lofty realm of mathematics—has ever laid such a heavy burden on the human intellect as sociology does.

We cannot conduct experiments in this sprawling field, for we cannot toy with the sands of time, nor can we play with the lives of men and women. We juggle a dizzying array of variables, trying to weigh their worth, seeking to untangle their interwoven fates. Yet, many of these elusive threads defy measurement, slipping through our fingers like smoke, leaving us with no notation to ground our thoughts. Perhaps, one day, we’ll borrow the tools of other sciences, but for now, our only path forward lies in honing our judgment through the patient observation of sociological phenomena and their intricate sequences.

However, the general public's grasp of physiology is alarmingly limited, with prevailing knowledge primarily focused on ailments and remedies. A similar pattern emerges in our understanding of society. Investigating the structure and functions of societal organs is a complex endeavor, and our progress thus far has been minimal. Significant advancement in this field hinges on extensive historical study and meticulous analysis of institutions. In fact, I would argue that sociology demands an intellectual rigor that rivals, if not surpasses, that required by advanced mathematics. Conducting experiments in sociology poses a unique challenge; we cannot manipulate time or the lives of individuals in a controlled manner. We must navigate a multitude of variables, assess their significance, and derive outcomes, often lacking standard units of measurement or comparative frameworks, not to mention the absence of a notation system to assist us. I believe we will eventually need to incorporate methods from other scientific disciplines, but for now, the only pathway to advance sociology lies in cultivating a discerning judgment through the diligent study of sociological phenomena and their interrelations.

President Lincoln in a note to Mayor Ramsey once wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." A college officer in my position at the opening of the college year would not be inclined to agree with Mr. Lincoln, for half the correspondence which comes to my desk during the summer months has to do with men who either want to work, or who say they do, in order that they may defray their college expenses. There are so many of them that their correspondence becomes almost depressing at times, for I realize the disappointments and the difficulties which very many of these boys will encounter after they reach college, and their unfitness to do any definite work well. "I have been out of high school three years," one young fellow writes, "and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This man, who has given all his time to work for three years and who has done nothing more than live, expects easily to carry a college course which in itself requires most of a man's time to do justice to, and at the same time to earn his living on the side. I get many such letters from those who feel that earning one's living and going to college are in no way incompatible. So much has been written about the fellows who have started to college without a cent and who have later been valedictorians of their classes and ultimately President of the United States, or at least a member of his cabinet, that the average high school boy has little conception of what sacrifice and deprivation such a procedure involves; if he did understand he would nat so often undertake it, or he would do so after more careful deliberation.

President Lincoln, in a note addressed to Mayor Ramsey, wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." However, a college administrator beginning the academic year might find it difficult to align with Lincoln’s sentiment. A significant portion of the correspondence arriving at my desk during the summer pertains to individuals seeking employment—or claiming to—in order to cover their college expenses. The sheer volume of such inquiries often feels overwhelming, as I am acutely aware of the challenges and setbacks many of these young men will face once enrolled. Furthermore, their lack of preparation for performing any specific type of work effectively adds to the concern.

One applicant, for instance, writes, "I have been out of high school for three years and have not managed to save any money. Nonetheless, I have a strong desire to attend college. Could you help me find a job where I can earn enough to cover my board, room, and any additional expenses I may incur?" This young man, who has spent three years working without accumulating savings, anticipates being able to handle the demands of a college education—an endeavor that requires most of an individual’s time and focus—while simultaneously earning enough to support himself. Letters like this are common, and they reflect a widespread belief among prospective students that earning a living and pursuing higher education are entirely compatible undertakings.

This misconception likely stems from numerous accounts extolling individuals who began college with no financial resources, later achieved academic distinction as valedictorians, and ultimately rose to prominent positions such as President of the United States or members of the Cabinet. Such narratives tend to obscure the significant sacrifices and hardships inherent in such paths. If high school students fully grasped the extent of the difficulties involved, they might either reconsider attempting this dual pursuit or approach it with greater forethought and preparation.

In a brief note to Mayor Ramsey, President Lincoln once penned these words: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." Yet, as I sit in my capacity as a college officer at the threshold of a new academic year, I must confess that my experience renders me somewhat less sanguine than Mr. Lincoln in this regard. Indeed, the summer months inundate my desk with letters from young men professing—or, at least, purporting—a desire to work, their aim being to finance their collegiate ambitions. Their missives, multitudinous as they are, seldom inspire unmitigated hope; rather, they are tinged with a certain pathos, for they presage the manifold trials and disillusionments that await many of these aspirants upon their arrival at college. Most are ill-prepared to perform any specific task with competence, a deficiency that compounds their already precarious circumstances.

One correspondent, for instance, articulates his predicament thus: "I have been out of high school three years and have not managed to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room, along with the additional funds required for my other minor expenses?" Here we encounter a young man who has devoted three full years to labor merely to subsist, yet he imagines that he can simultaneously undertake the rigorous intellectual demands of a college curriculum and support himself financially on the margins of his time. Such letters abound, each predicated on the naïve assumption that the dual endeavor of sustaining oneself materially while excelling academically is a harmonious enterprise, rather than an arduous balancing act fraught with compromises.

The mythology surrounding those who have ascended from penury to prominence—valedictorians who began their collegiate journey penniless, only to emerge as Presidents or cabinet members—has perpetuated a delusion among the average high school graduate. He envisions such triumphs without fully apprehending the profound sacrifices and relentless privations they entail. Were the magnitude of these costs more widely understood, fewer would embark on such a path so cavalierly, or they would do so only after a more sober and calculated reckoning.

President Lincoln once wrote a note to Mayor Ramsey saying, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." While Lincoln saw eagerness to work as a rare and admirable quality, someone in my position, managing college matters as a new school year begins, might not entirely share his sentiment. Over the summer, I receive piles of letters from young men who either want jobs or claim they do, hoping to cover their college costs. There are so many of these requests that they can feel overwhelming, especially because I know how many of these boys will face struggles and disappointments once they arrive on campus. Many simply aren’t prepared to do the work they’re asking for.

One young man wrote to me, “I’ve been out of high school for three years and haven’t been able to save any money. But I really want to go to college. Can you help me find a job where I can earn my board, room, and enough for my small expenses?” This fellow, having spent three years working just to get by, now hopes to manage both a full college course load—which demands most of a person’s time—and a job to cover his living expenses. He’s not alone, either. I get plenty of letters from boys who believe working while attending college is a simple and practical plan.

Stories of students who’ve started with nothing, worked hard, and become valedictorians—or even presidents—have inspired many high school boys. But the truth behind those tales is often glossed over. The sacrifice and hardship involved are enormous, more than most realize. If these boys understood the reality of what they’re taking on, they might hesitate before committing, or at least take more time to weigh their decision carefully.

The narrative of the penniless student who triumphs against all odds—emerging as class valedictorian or ascending to the presidency, if not a cabinet position—has been so widely publicized that it skews the perception of the average high school boy. Were they to truly understand, they might approach the decision with greater caution or defer such ambitions until better prepared.

This individual, having spent years merely scraping by, now imagines he can seamlessly balance the demands of a rigorous academic schedule alongside a job substantial enough to cover his living costs. Letters like his are common—driven by a belief that pursuing a degree and earning one’s keep can coexist without friction.

The mythos surrounding those legendary figures—the ones who began college penniless, climbed to valedictorian status, and ascended to the heights of power—has fostered a skewed perception among high school students. They often underestimate the grueling sacrifice and hardship such paths entail. If these young dreamers understood the weight of the challenge more fully, many might approach their aspirations with greater caution or at least with a more deliberate plan. Instead, buoyed by stories of triumph, they leap forward, unprepared for the realities that lie ahead.

President Lincoln in a note to Mayor Ramsey once wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." A college officer in my position at the opening of the college year would not be inclined to agree with Mr. Lincoln, for half the correspondence which comes to my desk during the summer months has to do with men who either want to work, or who say they do, in order that they may defray their college expenses. There are so many of them that their correspondence becomes almost depressing at times, for I realize the disappointments and the difficulties which very many of these boys will encounter after they reach college, and their unfitness to do any definite work well. "I have been out of high school three years," one young fellow writes, "and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This man, who has given all his time to work for three years and who has done nothing more than live, expects easily to carry a college course which in itself requires most of a man's time to do justice to, and at the same time to earn his living on the side. I get many such letters from those who feel that earning one's living and going to college are in no way incompatible. So much has been written about the fellows who have started to college without a cent and who have later been valedictorians of their classes and ultimately President of the United States, or at least a member of his cabinet, that the average high school boy has little conception of what sacrifice and deprivation such a procedure involves; if he did understand he would nat so often undertake it, or he would do so after more careful deliberation.

President Lincoln, in a correspondence addressed to Mayor Ramsey, stated, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." As an official at a college at the beginning of the academic year, I find it difficult to concur with Mr. Lincoln's assertion. A significant portion of the correspondence I receive during the summer pertains to individuals seeking employment, or claiming to seek it, in order to cover their college expenses. The volume of such inquiries is substantial, leading to a sense of discouragement as I contemplate the disappointments and challenges many of these students will face upon their arrival at college, as well as their inadequate preparation for fulfilling work responsibilities.

One young man wrote, "I have been out of high school three years and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This individual, who has devoted three years entirely to work without accumulating savings, expects to manage a college curriculum that demands a significant investment of time, while simultaneously earning a living. I receive numerous letters from individuals who maintain the belief that earning a living and pursuing a college education are not mutually exclusive endeavors.

A considerable amount has been documented regarding individuals who commenced their college education without financial resources, subsequently achieving the status of valedictorian or even ascending to the presidency of the United States, or at least holding a position in the presidential cabinet. Consequently, the average high school student lacks a comprehensive understanding of the sacrifices and hardships that such a journey entails. If he were fully aware of these challenges, he would likely hesitate to embark on this path or would proceed with greater caution and consideration.

In a correspondence addressed to Mayor Ramsey, President Lincoln articulated a poignant observation: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." However, as an academic administrator confronting the onset of a new academic year, I find myself at odds with Lincoln's optimism. The bulk of my summer correspondence is dominated by the entreaties of individuals who profess a desire for employment, ostensibly to alleviate their financial burdens associated with higher education. The sheer volume of these missives, each echoing a narrative of aspiration intertwined with desperation, becomes a source of profound disillusionment. I am acutely aware of the myriad obstacles and profound discontent that many of these young aspirants will inevitably face upon their arrival at the collegiate realm, compounded by their often inadequate preparation for any substantive labor.

One particular correspondent, having spent three years adrift since his departure from high school, implores: "I have been out of high school three years and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This individual, having devoted his entire existence to mere survival, harbors the expectation that he can seamlessly navigate the rigorous demands of a college curriculum—an enterprise that itself demands a near-total allocation of one’s temporal resources—while concurrently striving to earn a subsistence. 

Such letters proliferate from those who naively conflate the dual pursuits of self-sustenance and academic engagement, oblivious to the complex interplay between these two endeavors. The narrative of the underprivileged student who, with scant resources, ascends to the pinnacle of academic achievement—becoming a valedictorian or even a member of the presidential cabinet—has been romanticized to such an extent that it obscures the profound sacrifices and hardships that this trajectory entails. Were these aspirants to truly comprehend the depth of commitment and deprivation required, they might approach their ambitions with a more judicious and circumspect deliberation.

If only they could understand the weight of that journey, perhaps they would tread more cautiously or embark on it with a deeper sense of preparation and resolve.

But, although palæontology is a comparatively youthful scientific speciality, the mass of materials with which it has to deal is already prodigious. In the last fifty years the number of known fossil remains of invertebrated animals has been trebled or quadrupled. The work of interpretation of vertebrate fossils, the foundations of which were so solidly laid by Cuvier, was carried on, with wonderful vigour and success, by Agassiz in Switzerland, by Von Meyer in Germany, and last, but not least, by Owen in this country, while, in later years, a multitude of workers have laboured in the same field. In many groups of the animal kingdom the number of fossil forms already known is as great as that of the existing species. In some cases it is much greater; and there are entire orders of animals of the existence of which we should know nothing except for the evidence afforded by fossil remains. With all this it may be safely assumed that, at the present moment, we are not acquainted with a tithe of the fossils which will sooner or later be discovered. If we may judge by the profusion yielded within the last few years by the Tertiary formations of North America, there seems to be no limit to the multitude of Mammalian remains to be expected from that continent; and analogy leads us to expect similar riches in Eastern Asia, whenever the Tertiary formations of that region are as carefully explored. Again, we have as yet almost everything to learn respecting the terrestrial population of the Mesozoic epoch—and it seems as if the Western territories of the United States were about to prove as instructive in regard to this point as they have in respect of tertiary life. My friend Professor Marsh informs me that, within two years, remains of more than 160 distinct individuals of mammals, belonging to twenty species and nine genera, have been found in a space not larger than the floor of a good-sized room; while beds of the same age have yielded 300 reptiles, varying in size from a length of 60 feet or 80 feet to the dimensions of a rabbit.

Although paleontology is a relatively recent scientific discipline, the volume of material it addresses has already become substantial. Over the past fifty years, the catalog of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has expanded three- to fourfold. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, a field firmly established through the foundational work of Cuvier, was advanced with remarkable energy and success by Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in England. In more recent years, numerous researchers have continued to contribute to this area. In many groups of the animal kingdom, the number of identified fossil species rivals that of extant species. In certain cases, the fossil record even surpasses the number of currently living species. Entire orders of animals, whose existence would otherwise remain unknown, have been identified solely through fossil evidence.

Despite these achievements, it is reasonable to conclude that only a small fraction of all potential fossil discoveries has been made to date. The abundance of specimens recently unearthed from North America's Tertiary formations suggests that the continent holds an immense reservoir of mammalian fossils yet to be uncovered. By analogy, similar discoveries might be anticipated in Eastern Asia once its Tertiary formations are subjected to equally rigorous investigation. Furthermore, our understanding of the terrestrial organisms from the Mesozoic era remains minimal. Evidence suggests that the western regions of the United States may offer insights into this era comparable to those they have provided regarding Tertiary life. 

Professor Marsh has reported that, within a span of two years, fossils representing more than 160 distinct mammalian individuals, distributed across twenty species and nine genera, were recovered from an area no larger than a medium-sized room. In addition, Mesozoic-age deposits from the same region have yielded the remains of 300 reptiles, ranging in size from approximately 60 to 80 feet in length to those comparable in dimensions to a rabbit. These findings underscore the potential for continued discovery and the vast scope of paleontological research yet to be undertaken.

Though the discipline of paleontology may trace its lineage to relatively recent scientific origins, the sheer amplitude of its evidentiary corpus has already grown to astonishing proportions. Over the past half-century, the catalog of fossilized vestiges of invertebrate life forms has expanded threefold or even fourfold. The systematic deciphering of vertebrate paleontological records—initially architected with unparalleled precision by Cuvier—found its continuance in the indefatigable labors of Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in Britain. Their endeavors were succeeded, in more recent decades, by an ever-expanding cadre of investigators contributing to this burgeoning tapestry of discovery. Indeed, within numerous taxonomic strata of the animal kingdom, the inventory of fossilized species rivals—and in certain instances surpasses—the enumeration of extant taxa. Entire orders, whose ontological reality would have remained veiled in perpetual obscurity, owe their evidentiary affirmation solely to the mute testimony of fossilized remnants.

Yet, despite the seemingly boundless accumulation of paleontological artifacts, it is a reasonable conjecture that the current state of knowledge encompasses but a meager fraction—a mere tithe—of the fossils that await future revelation. The recent profusion of mammalian relics unearthed from the Tertiary strata of North America offers a tantalizing glimpse into the potential fecundity of the geological record. This abundance invites the inference that analogous treasures lie hidden beneath the Tertiary deposits of Eastern Asia, awaiting the meticulous excavation and scrutiny that will inevitably bring them to light. Moreover, the terrestrial biota of the Mesozoic epoch remains a largely uncharted frontier. The western expanses of the United States, already an abundant source of Tertiary insights, appear poised to yield similarly transformative revelations concerning Mesozoic life.

My colleague, Professor Marsh, recounts a staggering density of paleontological yield: within a mere two years, a confined area no larger than the floor of a commodious room has disclosed the fossilized remains of over 160 individual mammals, representing twenty distinct species across nine genera. Concurrently, stratigraphic layers of comparable antiquity have yielded an astonishing array of reptilian specimens—numbering 300 individuals and ranging in scale from rabbit-sized diminutiveness to leviathans stretching 60 or even 80 feet in length. In such discoveries, the latent immensity of Earth's deep biological history gradually unfurls before us, promising revelations yet untold.

Over the past fifty years, the catalog of known fossilized invertebrates has multiplied three- or fourfold. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, a discipline solidly grounded by Cuvier, was advanced with remarkable energy and achievement by Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and, notably, Owen in Britain. In more recent times, an ever-growing number of researchers have joined these efforts. Within numerous branches of the animal kingdom, the tally of identified fossil species equals, and occasionally surpasses, that of existing ones. Entire orders of creatures, whose presence would have remained unknown, owe their recognition solely to fossil evidence. Even with such progress, it is reasonable to surmise that our current knowledge represents but a fraction of the fossils yet to be unearthed.  

The prolific discoveries from North America’s Tertiary formations in recent years hint at an almost boundless reservoir of mammalian remains awaiting study. By analogy, Eastern Asia’s Tertiary formations, once explored with similar rigor, may yield comparable treasures. Furthermore, our understanding of Mesozoic terrestrial life remains in its infancy. The Western territories of the United States, which have already proven invaluable for illuminating tertiary fauna, seem poised to offer equally profound insights into Mesozoic ecosystems.

Whatever was once the custom, in these days, at least, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts and to do so promptly. The young man with the buoyancy and optimism of Mr. Skimpole, in *[Bleak House](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bleak_House),* who held that "the sordid word money should never be mentioned in the relations of friendship" is having little vogue at the present time. The enthusiastic life insurance agent, if you will admit his logic, can prove to you that if you will pay your premiums long enough they will cost you nothing, but every sensible man knows that the thing that is worth anything costs something, and the fraternity is no exception. Fraternity men in the past have too frequently taken their obligations lightly, and so in a financial way have brought fraternities into disrepute. Fraternity officers have begun strongly to emphasize the importance of financial matters in the fraternity and are insisting upon the introduction of accounting systems into all chapters which will make it possible for fraternity accounts to be kept accurately and which will impress upon the fraternity man from the outset his obligation to meet his bills when they are due. When a boy goes into a fraternity, neither he nor his father should be deceived as to the actual cost of membership, nor allowed to believe that payment may be evaded or deferred. It is ordinarily as unwise to go into a fraternity with the idea that the cost of living will thus be reduced as it is to enter matrimony with the thought that it will cost no more to support a family than an individual. With us at the University of Illinois the fraternity man is likely to be called upon to expend twenty per cent more than if he had remained outside of the organization, and if he develops popularity and contracts the joining habit, it may cost him considerably more than this.

In contemporary practice, fraternity members are expected to fulfill their financial obligations in a timely manner. Unlike the character Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, who optimistically asserted that "the sordid word money should never be mentioned in the relations of friendship," such attitudes are no longer widely accepted. While a life insurance agent may argue that consistent payment of premiums can eventually negate their cost, it is generally understood that anything of value requires financial expenditure, and fraternities are no exception. Historically, fraternity members have often failed to take their financial responsibilities seriously, which has led to reputational harm for these organizations. 

To address this issue, fraternity leadership has increasingly emphasized the importance of financial accountability. Officers are advocating for the implementation of structured accounting systems within chapters, ensuring that records are maintained accurately. This approach aims to instill a sense of responsibility in fraternity members, making clear their obligation to settle bills promptly. When a student joins a fraternity, both he and his family should be fully informed about the associated costs and should not harbor misconceptions about deferring or avoiding payment. 

Entering a fraternity with the expectation of reduced living expenses is generally impractical, much like assuming that marriage will not increase financial obligations compared to living as an individual. At the University of Illinois, it is common for fraternity members to incur expenses approximately twenty percent higher than those faced by non-members. Furthermore, individuals who achieve social popularity and develop a tendency to join multiple organizations may find their costs significantly higher than this estimate.

These days, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts on time. The carefree attitude of Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, who believed money should never come up between friends, doesn’t have much place now. Sure, a life insurance agent might tell you that paying premiums long enough makes them practically free, but everyone knows that anything worthwhile comes with a cost, and fraternities are no exception. In the past, fraternity members often didn’t take their financial responsibilities seriously, which hurt the reputation of fraternities as a whole. Because of this, fraternity leaders now put a strong focus on finances. They’re making sure chapters use proper accounting systems so bills are kept straight and members learn early on to pay what they owe, when they owe it.

When a young man joins a fraternity, neither he nor his family should be misled about the actual costs involved. It’s important they understand that membership comes with financial obligations that can’t be avoided or put off. Joining a fraternity isn’t likely to save money—it’s about as unrealistic as thinking marriage won’t cost more than living alone. At the University of Illinois, for instance, fraternity members typically spend about 20% more than they would if they stayed outside the organization. And if a member becomes popular or develops a habit of joining multiple groups, those costs could climb even higher. 

Fraternities offer much to their members, but it’s important to go into them with clear expectations about the financial commitment. A fraternity isn’t free—it’s an investment, and everyone involved should treat it that way.

Merited and timely as such criticism may appear to a transient observer of the State university, it is recognized as superficial and essentially false by all those who have felt the inner throb and glow of the enterprise. Idealist the institution is not, if idealism means a sterile yearning for the unattainable. Materialist it is not, if materialism is identified with satisfaction in the welfare of the senses. The State university to-day is at the same time intensely visionary and intensely practical: its driving power is the creative artist's desire to externalize and eternize his dream. With eyes fixed upon that end, it does not shrink from the coarse tasks of mixing pigments, quarrying stone, or melting bronze. It would honor every truth by use, and it holds that the triumph of the spiritual is the subjugation of the material. The financial support which it solicits is the means to the realization of a vision embracing almost the whole of life, and the wealth which it helps to create is but the first fruits of its contemplated harvest.

Criticism of the State university, though it may seem justified to a casual observer, is regarded as superficial and fundamentally inaccurate by those who understand the institution's deeper purpose and vitality. The university is not idealistic if idealism is defined as an unproductive aspiration for the unattainable. Nor is it materialistic if materialism is equated with mere satisfaction in physical comfort and sensory pleasures. The State university today is simultaneously highly visionary and highly pragmatic. Its motivating force resembles the creative artist’s ambition to give form and permanence to their vision. With this objective in mind, it does not hesitate to engage in demanding and practical tasks such as blending pigments, extracting stone, or casting bronze. The institution seeks to honor every truth through practical application, maintaining that the ultimate victory of the spiritual lies in mastering the material. The financial resources it requests are intended as tools to achieve a vision encompassing nearly all aspects of life, while the wealth it generates serves only as the initial outcome of its broader aspirations.

However justified such reproaches may seem to the casual onlooker skimming the surface of the State university’s vast endeavor, those who have attuned themselves to its deeper pulsations, who have felt the fervent vitality of its purpose, discern their superficiality and fundamental inaccuracy. The institution is no haven for the barren aspirations of an idealism that flutters ineffectually toward the unattainable. Nor does it sink into the inert satisfactions of materialism, if by that term we mean the complacent celebration of sensory contentment. Rather, the State university occupies a liminal space where the fervor of the visionary and the rigor of the pragmatic coalesce. Its animating force resembles the artist’s compulsion to incarnate and immortalize an imagined world; yet, in pursuit of this higher aim, it engages unflinchingly in the earthy labor of grinding pigments, hewing stone, and casting bronze.

The institution’s ethos seeks not to transcend or deny materiality but to subdue it, channeling its recalcitrant energies toward the service of enduring truths. In this, it affirms that the apotheosis of the spiritual lies in the disciplined mastery of the tangible. Financial resources, far from being an end in themselves, serve as instruments for the enactment of a vision that aspires to encompass the full breadth of human existence. The wealth it generates is merely the preliminary yield of a far more expansive and profound harvest.

Criticism, no matter how fitting or well-timed it may seem to the casual passerby sizing up the State university, crumbles as shallow and fundamentally untrue in the eyes of those who have felt the pulse, the heat, the living heartbeat of its purpose. This institution is no aloof dreamer, pining after the unreachable with limp hands and vacant sighs. Nor is it some brutish hedonist, gorging on earthly comforts and calling it a day. No, the State university stands as both a visionary and a workhorse, its soul fired by the artist’s hunger to shape the intangible into something lasting, something real. Fixated on this grand ambition, it does not flinch before the grit of its labor—mixing paints, chiseling stone, pouring bronze with hands steady and unashamed. It exalts all truths through their use, believing that the highest triumph of the spirit lies in bending the material world to its will. The money it seeks? Not an end, but a tool—a way to breathe life into a vision vast enough to cradle nearly all of human existence. And the wealth it generates? Merely the first glimmer of the bountiful harvest it dreams to sow.

Were it not for these we should have no samples to show of comedy in its purest and highest form . The enduring renown of our foremost comic dramatist rests almost entirely upon but three of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy, though undeniably brilliant, amounted to little more than a luminous exercise in mimicry, fashioned after models that his own nascent genius would soon outshine. Under his pen, tragedy appears as an aging coquette, her faded features obscured by the artificial embellishments of rouge and powder, much like the painted absurdities of Lady Wishfort. Yet these deficiencies are amply redeemed by his trio of masterful comedies, which alone suffice to anchor his legacy in perpetuity, as steadfast as the English tongue itself. Without these exemplars, we would be bereft of comedy in its most unalloyed and exalted form.

Ben Jonson, that lone crusader striving to impose a purer order upon the dramaturgical chaos of his era—when tragedy and comedy were as hopelessly entangled on the stage as they are in the tangled skein of human affairs—fell short in imbuing his intricate and monumental creations with the requisite fluidity and the essential vivacity that define the comic spirit. Turning to Congreve’s immediate precursors, we find that their collective efforts to erect an authentically English edifice of unadulterated comedy upon French foundations were marked by intrinsic limitations: Wycherley’s craftsmanship was alloyed with baser substances, and Etherege’s, though more refined, proved insubstantial—a coinage too light to carry weight against Congreve’s more substantial minting. Beyond these, the comedic landscape offered little but the unpolished ore of Shadwell’s coarse and muscular talent.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, the creative anatomy of Molière contains more than sufficient material to have fashioned a Congreve, while the equivalent proportion from Congreve’s corpus would, in turn, have sufficed to sculpt a Sheridan. Meanwhile, the robust and expansive humor that animates the comedies of Vanbrugh secures for him a seat—if not at the head, then not far removed—within the pantheon of comedic masters. Thus, within this lineage of theatrical endeavor, the contours of Congreve's achievement emerge as singularly luminous, a pinnacle unmatched by predecessors or successors alike.

The blazing renown of our mightiest comic playwright rests almost entirely—no, unshakably—on just three of his five creations. His debut comedy? A dazzling echo, a shimmering imitation of the very models it would soon outshine. As for tragedy, under his pen, it totters forth like Lady Wishfort herself—painted, powdered, and trembling beneath the weight of its own artifice. But those three comedies, oh, they tower like unyielding pillars, more than enough to bear the weight of a reputation as eternal as the language in which they were spun. Without them, we would stand bereft, clutching at shadows, with no pure and perfect comedy to hold aloft as our own.

Ben Jonson, titan though he was, strode into the fray with hopes of reforming the stage—a place where comedy and tragedy tangled together like lovers or wrestlers, mimicking the messy chaos of life itself. Yet, for all his grandeur and labor, he could not infuse his intricate, majestic works with the unforced grace, the nimble ease, that the finest comic worlds demand. And those who came just before Congreve, striving to raise an English comedy on French foundations? They stumbled. Wycherley’s substance was coarse, his metal too base. Etherege, by contrast, was all weightless glitter, a coin too light to carry value. Beyond them, the stage offered little but the crude, unrefined brawn of Shadwell, whose talent, though burly and bold, lacked the finer edge.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, we might say this: a single limb of Molière could have shaped a Congreve, and one limb of Congreve could have wrought a Sheridan. Such was the ladder of genius, each rung ascending to its own bright height. And yet, in the robust, hearty humor of Vanbrugh’s splendid comedies, we find a voice that earns its place at the master’s table—a voice that, while broad and brash, stands tall among the echoes of the great.

The enduring reputation of our most esteemed comic dramatist rests almost entirely on three of his five plays. His initial comedy amounted to little more than an impressive exercise in imitation, modeled on earlier works that his own surpassed even at this nascent stage. His attempts in tragedy, however, appear artificial and unconvincing, resembling the overly adorned and exaggerated features of Lady Wishfort. Nonetheless, his three principal comedies alone suffice to guarantee a legacy as enduring as the English language itself. Without these, we would lack exemplars of comedy at its most refined and elevated.

Ben Jonson, who uniquely sought to reform the dramatic conventions of his era—when comedy and tragedy were as interwoven on the stage as they were in life—fell short of achieving the necessary fluidity and elegance in the comedic elements of his otherwise grand and intricate compositions. Among Congreve’s immediate predecessors, those who aspired to construct a distinctively English comedy on French foundations, Wycherley’s work proved too coarse in quality, while Etherege’s was too insubstantial to rival his. Beyond these, the field offered little of value, with the dominant alternative being the unpolished and forceful output of Shadwell, whose talent, though vigorous, lacked refinement.

The broad, energetic humor of Vanbrugh’s excellent comedies secures him a significant position among the masters of the form.

The illustrious reputation of our preeminent comic dramatist is primarily anchored in a mere triad of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy resembles a dazzling imitation, an homage to predecessors whose luminescence has been eclipsed by this nascent effort of their acolyte. In contrast, his engagements with tragedy reveal a visage artificially adorned, akin to the rouged and wrinkled countenance of Lady Wishfort, a grotesque caricature of emotional depth. Yet, the three monumental comedies—those paragon creations—suffice to fortify a legacy as enduring as the language that enshrines them. Absent these masterpieces, we would possess no exemplars of comedy in its most unadulterated and exalted essence.

Ben Jonson, a solitary figure who endeavored to engender reform within the theatrical milieu of his epoch—an era where the boundaries between comedy and tragedy were as inextricable as the dualities of human existence—ultimately faltered in infusing his elaborate and resplendent oeuvre with the requisite levity and essential grace characteristic of the comic spirit. Among Congreve's immediate forerunners, those who sought to erect a new edifice of English comedy upon the foundations laid by French traditions, Wycherley emerged as too base in his artistic currency, while Etherege, lacking in substantive weight, proved insufficient for comparison. Beyond these figures, the only alternative available was the crude and unrefined ore of Shadwell's brutish and vigorous talent.

Employing an analogy drawn from Walter Savage Landor, one might contend that the corporeal essence of Molière could have fashioned a Congreve, while a mere fragment of Congreve's artistry would suffice to engender a Sheridan. The robust and expansive humor encapsulated within the commendable comedies of John Vanbrugh secures him a rightful position within the pantheon of masters, elevating his contributions to the discourse on comedic excellence.

Without them, we would lack exemplars of comedy in its most refined and elevated essence. Ben Jonson, who singularly sought to reform comedy amidst a time when comedy and tragedy were as intricately intertwined on stage as in real life, struggled to infuse his elaborate and grand creations with the necessary ease and essential grace of comic vitality and movement. Among Congreve's immediate predecessors, those who aimed to construct a new English comedy on French foundations, Wycherley proved to be of too coarse a substance, while Etherege was too insubstantial to rival him. Beyond their works, there was no other or finer currency than the crude talent of Shadwell, whose contributions were marked by a brutish and robust nature. To borrow a metaphor from Landor, we might suggest that a limb of Molière would suffice to create a Congreve, and a limb of Congreve would suffice to craft a Sheridan. The broad and hearty humor found in Vanbrugh's commendable comedies secures him a place in the pantheon of mastery.

We call it a singular fact, because we Yankees are thought to be fond of the spread-eagle style, and nothing can be more remote from that than his. We are reckoned a practical folks, who would rather hear about a new air-tight stove than about [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet our favorite teacher's practicality is not in the least of the Poor Richard variety. If he have any Buncombe constituency, it is that unrealized commonwealth of philosophers which Plotinus proposed to establish; and if he were to make an almanac, his directions to farmers would be something like this: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to get in your early Vedas." What, then, is his secret? Is it not that he out-Yankees us all? that his range includes us all? that he is equally at home with the potato-disease and original sin, with pegging shoes and the Over-soul? that, as we try all trades, so has he tried all cultures? and above all, that his mysticism gives us a counterpoise to our super-practicality?

We designate this a remarkable paradox, for it stands in stark contrast to the stereotype of the Yankee temperament, often caricatured as enamored of grandiloquent rhetoric and ostentatious display. His manner, however, is the antithesis of such posturing. We New Englanders pride ourselves on our utilitarian ethos, preferring discourse on innovations like hermetically sealed stoves over lofty meditations on [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet the practicality of our emblematic thinker bears no resemblance to the pragmatic proverbs of Poor Richard. If he commands any constituency of ideological adherents, it is likely the unmanifested republic of sages envisioned by Plotinus, a realm of pure contemplation unmoored from material concerns. Should he turn his hand to authoring an almanac, its agricultural advisories might read: “October: *Indian Summer;* the season for harvesting your primordial wisdom from the Vedas.”

What, then, accounts for his resonance? Is it not that he encapsulates the Yankee essence more comprehensively than any among us? That his intellectual orbit embraces the mundane and the metaphysical alike—potato blight and original sin, cobbler’s workbench and the Over-soul? That his polymathic engagement mirrors our own versatility, yet his mysticism counterbalances the gravitational pull of our unyielding pragmatism?

We call it a remarkable thing, because we Yankees are often seen as loving bold and flashy talk, and his way couldn’t be further from that. Folks think of us as practical, more likely to care about a new kind of air-tight stove than about what Plato had to say. But our favorite teacher’s kind of practicality is nothing like the down-to-earth advice you’d find in *Poor Richard’s Almanack.* If he has any group of admirers, it’s more like that ideal community of philosophers that Plotinus dreamed of. And if he ever wrote an almanac, his advice to farmers might sound like this: “October: *Indian Summer;* now’s the time to harvest your early Vedas.”

So, what’s his secret? Isn’t it that he outdoes us all in the best Yankee way? That he can connect with everything we care about? He’s just as comfortable talking about potato blight as he is about original sin, as familiar with making shoes as with the Over-soul. Just like we try our hand at all sorts of trades, he’s explored all kinds of knowledge. And maybe most important of all, his mysticism gives us something to balance out how overly practical we tend to be.

We are reckoned a practical folks, who would rather hear about a new air-tight stove than about [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet our favorite teacher's practicality is not in the least of the Poor Richard variety. If he have any Buncombe constituency, it is that unrealized commonwealth of philosophers which Plotinus proposed to establish; and if he were to make an almanac, his directions to farmers would be something like this: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to get in your early Vedas." What, then, is his secret? Is it not that he out-Yankees us all? that his range includes us all? that he is equally at home with the potato-disease and original sin, with pegging shoes and the Over-soul? that, as we try all trades, so has he tried all cultures? and above all, that his mysticism gives us a counterpoise to our super-practicality?

We designate it a singular fact, for we Yankees are often perceived as devotees of the ostentatious rhetoric, and nothing could be more antithetical to that than his approach. We are considered pragmatists, inclined to prefer discourse on the latest innovations, such as a new air-tight stove, over the musings of Plato; yet, the practicality of our esteemed educator diverges profoundly from the utilitarianism of Poor Richard. Should he possess any constituency of Buncombe, it would be that aspirational commonwealth of philosophers envisioned by Plotinus. Were he to compose an almanac, his agricultural counsel might resemble: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the moment to harvest your early Vedas." What, then, constitutes his esoteric secret? Is it not the case that he transcends our quintessential Yankee identity? That his intellectual purview encompasses the entirety of our existence? He navigates the realms of potato blight and original sin, of cobbling shoes and the concept of the Over-soul, as we dabble in various trades while he immerses himself in diverse cultures? Above all, his mysticism serves as a counterbalance to our hyper-practical dispositions.

We call it a singular fact because we Yankees often like to show off. Yet, nothing could be further from that than his style. We are seen as practical folks who prefer hearing about a new air-tight stove rather than deep thoughts from philosophers like Plato. But our favorite teacher’s practicality is nothing like Poor Richard’s. If he has any followers, it’s the dreamers and thinkers like Plotinus imagined. If he were to make a calendar, his advice for farmers might say, "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to read your early Vedas." So, what’s his secret? Isn’t it that he out-Yankees us all? His thinking includes us all. He’s comfortable talking about everything from potato blight to original sin, from making shoes to the idea of the Over-soul. Just as we try our hand at many jobs, he explores all kinds of cultures. Most importantly, his mystical side offers a balance to our overly practical ways.

We refer to it as a singular fact because we Americans are often perceived as embracing a flamboyant style, which is entirely contrary to his approach. We are seen as pragmatic individuals, more interested in hearing about an efficient stove than the philosophical musings of Plato. However, our esteemed teacher’s practicality diverges significantly from the common sense associated with Poor Richard. If he were to appeal to any constituency, it would be the imagined community of philosophers suggested by Plotinus. Should he draft an almanac, his advice to farmers might read: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to delve into your early Vedas." So, what is his underlying secret? Is it not that he surpasses us in our own domain? His breadth encompasses us all, comfortably navigating discussions from agricultural challenges to profound theological concepts, from the mundane act of shoe-making to the notion of the Over-soul? Just as we explore various trades, he has engaged with diverse cultures. Most importantly, his mysticism serves as a necessary balance to our hyper-practical tendencies.

The ancient proverb which declares that love is blind is only a half truth: love is only blind of one eye. The expression "blind hate" also contains only a half truth: hate has one powerful eye. When either of these passions is present, it insatiably sets about discovering new objects to feast upon. The lover collects and broods upon the graces of his beloved from the rising of the lark to the going down of the evening star. The husband who thoroughly loses his temper with his wife at the breakfast table busies himself all day long recollecting and amassing all the grievances that he has felt against her during the last ten years; and he returns at night in a sullen rage and with an almost superhuman insight into the deficiencies of his helpmate. So men of letters set out with gusto and find a relish in every wayside berry; or they set out on the satirical foot with a bad taste in their mouths, and for a whole generation occupy themselves with malicious caricatures of their mistress, Life.

The venerable aphorism that proclaims love’s blindness is, upon inspection, a partial truth at best: love is blind in but one eye, leaving the other to rove and revel. Likewise, the phrase "blind hate" is similarly deceptive, for hatred possesses a singular but piercing vision. These twin forces—love and hate—are insatiable seekers, endlessly questing for nourishment to sustain their fervor. The lover, entranced by the object of his affection, spends his hours tracing the contours of her virtues, his thoughts flitting from dawn’s lark to the twilight’s fading star, assembling a treasury of her charms. Conversely, the enraged husband, having quarreled with his wife over morning coffee, dedicates his day to summoning forth a litany of past grievances, constructing from the shards of ten years’ worth of dissatisfaction a monument to his wrath. By nightfall, he returns home, armed not merely with anger but with an almost preternatural acuity for cataloging her every flaw. And so, too, the writer embarks—whether enraptured or embittered—on the pathways of observation: one moment savoring the delicate sweetness of life’s incidental graces, the next consumed by a bitter appetite, producing caustic caricatures of existence, as though Life herself were a mistress deserving of scorn rather than devotion.

Love, they say, is blind, but it stumbles only half-shrouded in the dark, one eye wide open, fixed on its quarry. And hate, oh, hate—the single blazing eye of it sees too much, too sharply, too cruelly. These twin tyrants, love and hate, are ravenous hunters, prowling always for fresh prey to feed their hunger. The lover? He’s a collector, his thoughts a magpie’s hoard of his beloved’s charms, polished and adored from the lark’s first song to the soft sigh of the evening star. The husband scalded by anger at breakfast? He spends his day dredging up the ghosts of quarrels past, grievances long buried but now risen and marching. By nightfall, he storms home, a thundercloud, clutching a decade’s worth of her flaws, each one gleaming with the cruel clarity of rage.

And those who wield the pen—the men of letters—are no different. When enamored of Life, they pluck every roadside berry, their words sweet and sun-warmed, a hymn to existence. But let bitterness take root, and they become bitter cartographers, sketching Life in caricature, their ink venomous, their pages steeped in bile. A generation may pass, and still they labor, their mistress maligned, their malice unyielding, their passion turned inside out.

The proverb asserting that love is blind is only partially accurate; love is blind in only one eye. Similarly, the phrase "blind hate" is equally incomplete, as hate possesses one exceptionally keen eye. When either passion arises, it engages with relentless intensity in seeking new objects to consume. The lover devotes himself to contemplating the virtues of the beloved, from dawn to dusk. Conversely, a husband who loses his temper with his wife during breakfast may spend the entire day reflecting on and compiling grievances accumulated over the past decade. By evening, he returns home filled with resentment and an almost extraordinary ability to perceive his wife’s flaws. Similarly, literary individuals approach life with enthusiasm, finding enjoyment in every minor detail along their path. Alternatively, they may adopt a satirical stance, harboring distaste, and dedicate decades to producing spiteful distortions of their subject, which in this case is Life itself.

The old saying that love is blind tells only half the story: love is blind in just one eye. Similarly, the term "blind hate" offers only a partial truth, for hate wields a single, piercing eye. When either of these forces takes hold, it hungrily seeks fresh fuel to sustain its fervor. The lover, from the first call of the lark to the final shimmer of the evening star, gathers and meditates on every charm of the beloved. Meanwhile, the husband who erupts in anger over breakfast spends his day ruminating and compiling a decade’s worth of grievances against his wife. By nightfall, he returns home, seething, with an almost uncanny ability to pinpoint her flaws. Writers, too, embark on their journeys with fervent purpose, savoring every roadside discovery with delight—or, if bitterness grips them, they set out with a sour palate and devote years to crafting biting caricatures of their muse, Life.

The old adage claiming that love is blind tells but half the story: love is merely blind in one eye. Similarly, the phrase "blind hate" holds only partial truth, for hate peers through one piercing eye. When either of these forces takes hold, it relentlessly seeks out fresh subjects to consume. The lover spends his hours tracing and cherishing the virtues of his beloved, from the lark's first song to the fading glow of the evening star. Meanwhile, the husband who flies into a rage at breakfast devotes his day to dredging up every grievance from the past decade, returning home steeped in resentment and armed with an almost preternatural ability to pinpoint his wife's flaws. In much the same way, writers embark eagerly, savoring even the smallest fruits of inspiration along the roadside, or, soured by bitterness, set forth with mockery in their hearts, crafting spiteful caricatures of their muse, Life, for years on end.

The lover, enraptured, gathers and lingers over the virtues of his beloved from dawn’s first lark-song to twilight’s fading star. Meanwhile, the husband who loses his temper with his wife over breakfast spends his day meticulously recalling every grievance borne across a decade, returning home steeped in resentment and armed with a near-supernatural acuity for her shortcomings. Likewise, the writer may embark with fervor, savoring every roadside gem, or set forth in bitterness, the sourness within breeding a lifetime of sharp, satirical sketches of his muse, Life. Whether enamored or embittered, each finds sustenance in their relentless pursuit of the world's offerings.

Love is often said to be blind, yet this notion only scratches the surface; it is blind in one eye, ever seeking the beauty in its beloved. Conversely, hate wields a fierce, unyielding gaze. When either passion ignites, it craves new targets to consume. A lover, enraptured, revels in the cherished qualities of their beloved, from dawn’s first light to the twilight’s gentle embrace. Yet, the husband who loses his temper over a trivial breakfast squabble finds himself ensnared in a web of resentment, obsessively recalling every slight and grievance from the past decade. By nightfall, he returns, cloaked in a shadow of bitterness, wielding an almost supernatural clarity about his wife's shortcomings. Meanwhile, writers embark on their quests with fervor, savoring every delightful detail they encounter, or they may tread a darker path, armed with cynicism, crafting biting critiques of the very essence of Life. In this dance of love and hate, emotions intertwine, revealing the complex tapestry of human experience—a thrilling adventure where both light and shadow dwell.

There was a still more pregnant reason for my desertion of the camp of musical criticism. I seemed always to be about ten years ahead of most of the other critics and the orchestral conductors who make out programs. I missed the reviling of Wagner in New York, but I have watched the pundits of the press revile, in turn, Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, only, in the end, after their ears, through repeated hearings, had grown accustomed to the new clang-tints, to accept these composers as part of the sacred hierarchy. My orchestral education was carried on under Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Now Thomas was not a great conductor, although he always gave honest musical readings of his scores, but he had one great virtue: he believed that new music should be heard. He performed, therefore, every important European composition as soon as possible after it had been performed abroad. As a result, when I arrived in New York in 1906, having listened to and appraised nearly all the major works of Strauss and Debussy composed up to that date, I was amazed to discover that the New York critics were still fighting about these composers, first underpraising them and a little later overpraising them, for Strauss, at any rate, to my mind, has never been a whit superior as a composer to such a man as Liszt. In Paris, in 1913, I heard Stravinsky's [Sacre du Printemps](https://en.wikisource.org/w/index.php?title=Sacre_du_Printemps&action=edit&redlink=1) and a good deal more of this Russian's music. I wrote a panegyric about Le Sacre du Printemps in my first book,[[1]](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Red#cite_note-1) published in 1915. In 1924, Le Sacre du Printemps has at last been performed in New York and the critics have at last accepted Stravinsky. So, indeed, apparently, have the conductors who, until the past four years, hardly ever permitted his name to be emblazoned on their programs. The job of music critic in New York, therefore, is certainly not an ideal occupation for a man with imagination and foresight.

There was an even stronger reason why I gave up being a music critic. I always seemed to be about ten years ahead of most other critics and the conductors who chose what to put on concert programs. I wasn’t around to see the backlash against Wagner in New York, but I did watch the critics take turns attacking Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky. Yet, in time, as they heard the new sounds over and over, they got used to them. Eventually, they accepted these composers as part of the musical elite. 

My own exposure to orchestral music came from Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Thomas wasn’t a great conductor, though he always gave honest performances of the works he conducted. But he had one admirable quality: he believed in giving new music a chance. He made sure to perform every major European piece as soon as he could after its debut abroad. Thanks to him, by the time I arrived in New York in 1906, I had already heard and formed opinions on nearly all of the important works by Strauss and Debussy up to that point. I was shocked to find that New York critics were still arguing about these composers. At first, they didn’t give them enough credit, and then they went too far in the other direction. Personally, I’ve never thought Strauss was any better as a composer than someone like Liszt.

In Paris, in 1913, I had the chance to hear Stravinsky’s *The Rite of Spring* and more of his music. I praised *The Rite of Spring* in my first book, published in 1915. But it wasn’t until 1924 that *The Rite of Spring* finally made it to a New York stage, and only then did the critics and conductors start to embrace Stravinsky. Before that, conductors hardly ever included his work in their programs.

With all this in mind, it’s clear to me that being a music critic in New York is not the ideal job for someone who has imagination and can see what’s coming.

A still more profound impetus catalyzed my departure from the domain of musical criticism. It became evident that my aesthetic sensibilities operated on a temporal wavelength perpetually ahead of the prevailing judgments of both critics and the orchestral architects who curate the concert repertoire. My encounters with the cyclical vilification and subsequent canonization of composers unfolded like a ritualistic dance of delayed comprehension. Though I bypassed the era of Wagner’s denunciation in New York, I bore witness to the critical establishment’s successive repudiations of Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky—each initially dismissed, only to be sanctified once repeated exposure had acclimated ears to the unfamiliar tonal architectures. 

My own education in orchestral music was cultivated under the stewardship of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. While Thomas himself was not a conductor of transcendent brilliance, his interpretative integrity and a singular philosophical commitment elevated his legacy: he espoused the conviction that the unfamiliar and the avant-garde deserved their moment in the acoustic arena. Thus, under his baton, every seminal European composition found its way into performance shortly after its continental debut, ensuring that Chicago resonated with the echoes of progress. Consequently, when I arrived in New York in 1906, armed with firsthand familiarity with the principal works of Strauss and Debussy, I was startled to discover that the city’s critics were embroiled in puerile skirmishes over these composers—alternately undervaluing and overexalting them. Strauss, for instance, struck me as no more accomplished than Liszt, despite the hyperbolic fluctuations of critical regard.

By 1913, in Paris, I experienced Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps*—a seismic eruption of musical innovation—and immersed myself in the broader corpus of his creations. My inaugural book, published in 1915, extolled *Le Sacre* as a watershed moment. Yet it was not until 1924 that New York audiences heard this masterpiece, and not until then did the city’s critics and conductors reluctantly align their judgments with the inevitable. The cultural inertia of New York’s musical criticism, its sluggish reconciliation with the future, renders it an inhospitable vocation for one possessed of vision and audacity.

There was an even stronger reason why I gave up being a music critic. I always seemed to be about ten years ahead of most other critics and the conductors who chose what to put on concert programs. I wasn’t around to see the backlash against Wagner in New York, but I did watch the critics take turns attacking Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky. Yet, in time, as they heard the new sounds over and over, they got used to them. Eventually, they accepted these composers as part of the musical elite. 

My own exposure to orchestral music came from Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Thomas wasn’t a great conductor, though he always gave honest performances of the works he conducted. But he had one admirable quality: he believed in giving new music a chance. He made sure to perform every major European piece as soon as he could after its debut abroad. Thanks to him, by the time I arrived in New York in 1906, I had already heard and formed opinions on nearly all of the important works by Strauss and Debussy up to that point. I was shocked to find that New York critics were still arguing about these composers. At first, they didn’t give them enough credit, and then they went too far in the other direction. Personally, I’ve never thought Strauss was any better as a composer than someone like Liszt.

In Paris, in 1913, I had the chance to hear Stravinsky’s *The Rite of Spring* and more of his music. I praised *The Rite of Spring* in my first book, published in 1915. But it wasn’t until 1924 that *The Rite of Spring* finally made it to a New York stage, and only then did the critics and conductors start to embrace Stravinsky. Before that, conductors hardly ever included his work in their programs.

With all this in mind, it’s clear to me that being a music critic in New York is not the ideal job for someone who has imagination and can see what’s coming.

There was, lurking in the shadows of my departure from the critic’s chair, a reason more potent than all others, an ember that burned hotter than mere dissatisfaction or fatigue. I seemed forever to stride ten years ahead of the pack—the critics, the maestros, the steely-eyed architects of concert programs—always ten steps forward, always alone. I missed the venomous uproar that Wagner provoked in New York, but I stood witness to the press’s ritual savaging of Richard Strauss, then Debussy, then Stravinsky, as if they were wild beasts to be tamed. And yet, as the years wore on, as the ears of these learned men grew calloused by the battering waves of new tonal colors, each of these composers was duly anointed, their crowns polished, their names carved into the sacred marble of musical canon.

My own apprenticeship in orchestral music unfolded under the banner of Theodore Thomas in Chicago—a man whose baton, though not touched by greatness, was guided by honesty. He read scores faithfully, but what set him apart was his fierce conviction that the new deserved its day in the sun. If some brilliant work had just unfurled its wings in Europe, Thomas would see to it that it landed swiftly on American soil. Thanks to him, by the time I set foot in New York in 1906, my ears had already drunk deeply from the wells of Strauss and Debussy, their major works laid bare before me. Yet to my astonishment, I found New York’s critics still embroiled in petty skirmishes over these composers—first dismissing them with sneers, then, in a dizzy swing of the pendulum, showering them with excess praise. Strauss, I dare say, has never risen above the level of Liszt in my eyes.

In Paris, 1913, I heard Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps*, a feral, electrifying tempest of sound that shook me to my marrow. I sang its praises in my first book, published two years later. And now, in 1924, New York has finally caught up—*Le Sacre* has been performed, the critics have folded their arms in begrudging approval, and the conductors, once timid, now dare to write Stravinsky’s name in bold upon their programs. But for a man with imagination and the gift of foresight, the critic’s desk in New York is a poor perch indeed.

It seemed that the conductors had too, as they had rarely included his name in their programs until the last few years. Because of all this, being a music critic in New York was not the best job for someone with imagination and vision.

A deeper, more visceral reason gnawed at my soul, urging me to abandon the camp of musical criticism. I felt as if I were always ten years ahead of the pack—those critics and orchestral conductors fumbling about like blindfolded children. Ah, how I missed the cacophony of Wagner’s vilification echoing through New York’s streets! Yet, I witnessed the same fate befall Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, as the press pundits hurled their barbs. But give it time, like a fine wine steeping in darkness, and the public's ears would soften, embracing these composers as if they were sacred relics. 

My musical education bloomed under the watchful eye of Theodore Thomas in the vibrant heart of Chicago. Now, let’s be clear: Thomas wasn’t a titan of the podium, but he wielded one glorious truth—new music deserved an audience. He brought forth every vital European composition, fresh from its birth abroad, into the eager hands of our own hungry ears. So, when I landed in New York in 1906, my heart racing with anticipation, I was stunned to find the critics still engaged in a bitter duel over these composers—first casting them into the shadows, then dragging them into the spotlight, only to let Strauss linger in an uneasy limbo, a mere whisper of greatness alongside Liszt.

In Paris, 1913, I found myself entranced by Stravinsky's *Sacre du Printemps*, enveloped in the wild, untamed beauty of his music. My pen flowed with praise for the piece in my debut book, published in 1915—a heartfelt ode to a masterpiece! But lo and behold, it wasn’t until 1924 that *Le Sacre du Printemps* finally graced the New York stage, and oh, how the critics, like reluctant moths, began to embrace Stravinsky’s genius. Conductors, too, had finally donned the armor of acceptance, after years of shunning his name like a dark secret. Thus, the role of a music critic in New York, a place where imagination and foresight are often cast adrift, was hardly a sanctuary for a visionary spirit.

My own orchestral awakening unfolded under the guidance of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Though Thomas may not have been the most illustrious conductor, his unwavering commitment to presenting new music was unparalleled. He championed every significant European composition, ensuring that these vital works reached eager ears without delay. Therefore, upon my arrival in New York in 1906, I was struck with astonishment at the critics still embroiled in debates over these composers, swinging from undervaluation to overzealous praise. In my view, Strauss has never soared above the likes of Liszt. Thus, the role of a music critic in New York reveals itself as far from ideal for one who dares to dream and envision a future enriched by the boldest of sounds.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute at the county-seat; and there distinguished guests of the superintendent taught the teachers fractions and spelling and other mysteries,—white teachers in the morning, Negroes at night. A picnic now and then, and a supper, and the rough world was softened by laughter and song. I remember how— But I wander. There came a day when all the teachers left the Institute and began the hunt for schools. I learn from hearsay (for my mother was mortally afraid of fire-arms) that the hunting of ducks and bears and men is wonderfully interesting, but I am sure that the man who has never hunted a country school has something to learn of the pleasures of the chase. I see now the white, hot roads lazily rise and fall and wind before me under the burning July sun; I feel the deep weariness of heart and limb as ten, eight, six miles stretch relentlessly ahead; I feel my heart sink heavily as I hear again and again, "Got a teacher? Yes." So I walked on and on—horses were too expensive—until I had wandered beyond railways, beyond stage lines, to a land of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the coming of a stranger was an event, and men lived and died in the shadow of one blue hill.

First, there came the Teachers' Institute, perched proudly at the county-seat like a rooster on its roost. There, in the superintendent’s court of knowledge, his chosen sages unraveled the tangled skeins of fractions and spelling, those cryptic arts—white teachers in the light of morning, Negro teachers cloaked in night’s velvet. A picnic here, a supper there, laughter and song weaving a balm over the day’s sharp edges. I could tell you more—oh, how I could—but my thoughts slip away like a kite torn loose.  

And then, like a great migration, the teachers scattered, loosed from the Institute, hunting their schools as if they were quarry. My mother, who harbored a terror of firearms, had no tales of duck hunts or bear stalks, but I swear there’s no sport keener than chasing a country school. I can still see it: the roads, pale as bone, writhing under July’s cruel sun, rising, falling, twisting like something alive. The slog of it—mile after mile, ten then eight then six, each step heavier than the last. And always that crushing refrain, “Got a teacher? Yes.”  

Onward I trudged, horses being luxuries of another world, until the tracks and stage lines vanished behind me, and I entered the wilds. Here, where the sky leaned down to kiss a solitary blue hill, strangers were rare as comets. Here, in a land of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, life seemed to hover, suspended, between the sharp snap of birth and the quiet rustle of death.
