First, there was a Teachers' Institute held at the county seat. The superintendent invited special guests who taught the teachers about fractions, spelling, and other subjects—white teachers attended in the morning, while Black teachers came at night. Sometimes there were picnics and suppers, moments where the hard edges of life softened with laughter and song. I remember how—well, never mind. Eventually, the Institute ended, and the teachers set out to find schools to teach. People say hunting ducks, bears, or even men can be exciting (though my mother was too scared of guns to talk much about it), but I believe those who've never hunted for a school in the countryside have missed one of life's odd adventures.

I can still picture the roads stretching ahead, dusty and white, twisting up and down under the blistering July sun. I can still feel the heavy weariness in my body and the ache in my heart as mile after mile passed. And I can hear the disappointment of the repeated answer: "Got a teacher? Yes." So I kept walking—horses cost too much—and wandered far beyond the railways and stagecoach routes. I ended up in a place full of varmints and rattlesnakes, where strangers were rare, and people lived and died in the shadow of a single blue hill.

At the outset, there was a Teachers' Institute held at the county seat. During this event, distinguished guests invited by the superintendent provided instruction to teachers on subjects such as fractions, spelling, and other academic topics. White teachers attended these sessions in the morning, while African American teachers participated in the evening. Occasionally, a picnic or a supper was organized, introducing moments of levity where the harsh realities of life were temporarily softened by laughter and music. I recall vividly—though I digress. Eventually, the time arrived when all the teachers departed from the Institute to begin the search for schools. While I lack personal experience with hunting (as my mother had a pronounced fear of firearms), I have heard it said that pursuing ducks, bears, or even other humans can be an enthralling activity. However, I am convinced that an individual who has never sought out a rural school has yet to experience a particular kind of challenge. I can still picture the dusty, sun-scorched roads rising, falling, and twisting endlessly under the oppressive heat of July. I can recall the profound exhaustion that gripped both my body and spirit as mile after mile stretched ahead—ten, eight, six—unrelenting. Time and again, my spirits would sink as I was met with the same response: "Do you already have a teacher? Yes." Thus, I continued my journey on foot, as horses were beyond my financial means, wandering farther and farther—beyond the reach of railroads and stage lines—into a remote region inhabited by wildlife and rattlesnakes. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a notable occurrence, and life and death unfolded quietly under the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

Occasionally, a picnic or a supper would intersperse the routine, softening the harsh edges of life with laughter and song. I recall—though I digress. The time arrived when all the teachers departed the Institute to embark on the pursuit of schools. From hearsay (my mother, deeply fearful of firearms, avoided such matters), I understand that hunting ducks, bears, or even men holds a certain fascination, yet I contend that anyone who has not hunted for a rural school remains unacquainted with a truly singular thrill. I can still see the shimmering roads before me, lazily undulating and winding under the oppressive July sun; I can feel the profound exhaustion in both body and spirit as the miles—ten, then eight, then six—unfolded without mercy. My heart falters anew as I hear the repeated refrain, "Got a teacher? Yes." And so I trudged onward—horses being far beyond my means—until my path carried me past railway lines, beyond stage routes, into a realm teeming with "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of a stranger was a rare occurrence, and life and death played out in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

Eventually, the day arrived when every teacher departed the Institute to begin the quest for schools. From hearsay (as my mother harbored an enduring fear of firearms), I gather that hunting ducks, bears, and even men holds a certain allure, but I am convinced that the pursuit of a country school offers its own unique thrill. The memory of those white, sun-drenched roads winding lazily before me under the oppressive July heat remains vivid; the weighty weariness of my heart and limbs as miles—ten, eight, six—stretched mercilessly ahead is something I will never forget. The heaviness returned each time I heard the inevitable refrain, “Got a teacher? Yes.” And so, I trudged forward—horses being far beyond my means—until I ventured beyond railroads and stagecoach lines, into a realm of "varmints" and rattlesnakes. There, the arrival of a stranger was a notable event, and life unfolded in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

There was, to begin with, a Teachers' Institute at the county seat—a gathering where the esteemed guests of the superintendent revealed the secrets of fractions, spelling, and other enigmas. Mornings belonged to white teachers, nights to Negroes. Between lessons, the air softened with the warmth of picnics, suppers, and the unguarded joy of laughter and song. I could recount—ah, but my thoughts meander. Then came the moment when the teachers left the Institute, embarking on the quest for schools. They say (and I cannot confirm, for my mother shuddered at the mere mention of firearms) that hunting ducks, bears, or men is thrilling, but I am certain that anyone who has not sought a rural school has yet to taste the true exhilaration of the chase. Even now, I can see the shimmering, white-hot roads uncoiling endlessly beneath the searing July sun, feel the deep ache in my limbs and spirit as mile after mile—ten, eight, six—stretched mercilessly ahead. My heart falters anew at the memory of hearing, again and again, "Got a teacher? Yes." So I walked, endlessly—horses, an impossible luxury—until I left behind the railways, the stage lines, and entered a wilderness teeming with varmints and rattlesnakes. There, in a land where a stranger’s arrival was a rarity, and lives unfolded and ended beneath the eternal shadow of one cerulean hill, I trudged on.

From what I hear (for my mother harbored a deep fear of firearms), hunting ducks, bears, or even men can be quite thrilling. So I trudged onward—horses being beyond my means—until my path led me past the reach of railways and stage lines, into a land teeming with "varmints" and rattlesnakes. Occasionally, a picnic or a supper would punctuate the routine, and for fleeting moments, the harshness of life melted into the warmth of laughter and song. I recall—though I digress. Soon came the time when the teachers departed from the Institute, embarking on the quest to find schools. Though I have only heard tales of duck, bear, and man hunts (my mother, terrified of guns, kept me away), I am certain that pursuing a rural schoolhouse holds its own peculiar thrill. I can still envision the white, sun-drenched roads stretching endlessly before me, their undulating paths winding through the oppressive heat of July. I can still feel the bone-deep fatigue that crept into my body as ten miles dwindled to eight, then six, the distances unyielding. I recall the disheartening refrain echoing again and again: "Got a teacher? Yes." And so, on I trudged—horses being far beyond my means—venturing past the reach of railways and stage lines, into a wilderness of "varmints" and rattlesnakes. There, where strangers were a rarity, and lives unfolded and ended in the quiet shadow of a solitary blue hill, I continued my solitary chase.

Picnics blossomed like wildflowers, laughter danced on the breeze, and song wrapped the rough world in a soft embrace. Ah, but I digress. 

Then came the day when all the teachers, restless as the wind, left the Institute, embarking on a quest for schools. That’s where true adventure lies! I can feel the white-hot roads unfurling like a ribbon of molten silver beneath the relentless July sun, rising and falling, twisting like a serpent. Based on what I have heard—since my mother held a deep-seated fear of firearms—I understand that the pursuit of ducks, bears, and even men can be quite captivating. Yet, I am convinced that anyone who has never sought a country school is missing out on a unique adventure. I vividly recall the scorching July sun beating down on the white-hot roads as they undulated and twisted before me. The physical and emotional fatigue weighed heavily on me as I traversed mile after relentless mile—ten, eight, six—feeling my spirit sink with each encounter that ended with the discouraging words, "Got a teacher? Yes." I continued my journey on foot, as horses were too costly, venturing far beyond railways and stage lines into a wilderness inhabited by "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of a stranger was a noteworthy occurrence, and lives were lived and lost beneath the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

In the beginning, the county-seat hosted a Teachers' Institute, where esteemed guests, invited by the superintendent, imparted knowledge to teachers on fractions, spelling, and other intriguing subjects—white educators in the morning, and their Black counterparts in the evening. Occasionally, there were picnics and supper gatherings, moments where the harshness of life was momentarily softened by laughter and song. But I digress. A pivotal day arrived when all the teachers departed from the Institute to embark on a quest for schools. I’ve heard, as my mother harbored a deep fear of firearms, that pursuing ducks, bears, and even people can be quite captivating. Yet, I firmly believe that anyone who has never sought out a rural school is missing out on a uniquely thrilling adventure. I can vividly envision the sun-scorched roads stretching out before me, undulating like a mirage beneath the relentless July heat. I feel the weight of exhaustion settling in as ten, eight, six miles stretch out endlessly ahead; my spirits plummet with each repeated inquiry, "Got a teacher? Yes." Thus, I trudged onward—horses were a luxury I could not afford—until I found myself wandering beyond railways and stage lines, entering a realm populated by "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of a stranger was a significant occurrence, and life unfolded in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

In those sacred hours, white teachers shared their wisdom in the morning, while at night, Negro educators brought their own light. Occasionally, laughter and song filled the air during picnics and suppers, softening the rough edges of our world. Yet, as I reminisce, I find myself drifting away from the essence of that time. 

Then came the day when all the teachers departed the Institute, embarking on a quest for schools. I have heard tales—my mother, fearful of firearms, kept me away from such adventures—of the thrill in hunting ducks, bears, and even men. But I believe the man who has never searched for a country school has yet to experience the true joys of the chase. Then came the day when all the teachers departed the Institute, embarking on a quest for schools. Through the whispers of others, I glean that the pursuit of ducks, bears, and even men can be thrilling, but I believe that one who has never sought out a country school has yet to grasp the true joys of the chase. Yes.” So I trudged on, for horses were a luxury I could not afford, wandering beyond railways and stage lines into a realm teeming with “varmints” and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of a stranger became a notable event, and men lived and died under the enduring gaze of one blue hill.

Sprinkled over hill and dale lay cabins and farmhouses, shut out from the world by the forests and the rolling hills toward the east. There I found at last a little school. Josie told me of it; she was a thin, homely girl of twenty, with a dark-brown face and thick, hard hair. I had crossed the stream at Watertown, and rested under the great willows; then I had gone to the little cabin in the lot where Josie was resting on her way to town. The gaunt farmer made me welcome, and Josie, hearing my errand, told me anxiously that they wanted a school over the hill; that but once since the war had a teacher been there; that she herself longed to learn,—and thus she ran on, talking fast and loud, with much earnestness and energy.

Scattered across the hills and valleys were cabins and farmhouses, tucked away from the world by the forests and the rolling eastern hills. It was there that I finally came upon a small school. Josie had told me about it. She was a thin, plain young woman, about twenty years old, with a dark-brown face and thick, coarse hair. I had crossed the stream at Watertown and stopped to rest under the big willow trees. Afterward, I made my way to a little cabin in a field, where Josie was taking a break on her way to town. The lean farmer welcomed me, and when Josie heard why I had come, she eagerly shared that they needed a school on the other side of the hill. She said there had been a teacher there only once since the war. She spoke quickly and passionately, saying how much she herself wanted to learn.

Scattered across the undulating landscape of hills and hollows, cabins and farmhouses reclined in solitary isolation, veiled from the larger world by the dense embrace of forests and the undulating eastern ridges. Amidst this seclusion, I eventually encountered a modest schoolhouse, the existence of which Josie had revealed to me. Josie, a slender and unassuming young woman of twenty, bore a dark-brown visage and hair coarse as unyielding rope. My journey had taken me across the Watertown stream, where I paused beneath the expansive willows, and then onwards to a humble cabin nestled in a small clearing, where Josie was temporarily resting before continuing her errand into town. The lanky farmer greeted me with a rough-hewn hospitality, and upon hearing my purpose, Josie spoke with fervent urgency. She described a yearning for education in the community beyond the hill, lamenting that, since the war, a teacher had come but once. Her own hunger for knowledge burned brightly as she poured out her words, swift and impassioned, her earnestness carrying the weight of her hopes.

My bands of silk and miniver Momently grew heavier; The black gauze was beggarly thin; The ermine muffled mouth and chin; I could not suck the moonlight in. Harlequin in lozenges Of love and hate, I walked in these Striped and ragged rigmaroles; Along the pavement my footsoles Trod warily on living coals. Shouldering the thoughts I loathed, In their corrupt disguises clothed, Morality I could not tear From my ribs, to leave them bare Ivory in silver air. There I walked, and there I raged; The spiritual savage caged Within my skeleton, raged afresh To feel, behind a carnal mesh, The clean bones crying in the flesh.

My ribbons of silk and pale miniver tightened, their weight a slow-growing ache; the gauze, flimsy as charred paper, offered no comfort. The ermine swallowed my face whole—no moonlight could seep past its suffocating clasp. I stumbled, a harlequin caught in jagged diamonds of love and loathing, wrapped in these tattered riddles, these striped ruins of thought. Each step, cautious, pressed my soles against coals that hissed and lived. Laden with the vile masquerade of ideas I detested, I could not peel morality from my ribs, could not bare the ivory truth to the silver-breathing sky. I strode, I fumed—a feral spirit throttled inside its bone cage, thrashing to escape the sinew’s prison, to free the white cry of marrow muffled by hungering flesh.

Laden with the vile masquerade of ideas I detested, I could not peel morality from my ribs, could not bare the ivory truth to the silver-breathing sky. I strode, I fumed—a feral spirit throttled inside its bone cage, thrashing to escape the sinew’s prison, to free the white cry of marrow muffled by hungering flesh.

The swathes of silk and miniver encasing me accrued an oppressive gravity, moment by moment; the frailty of black gauze betrayed its insufficiency, while the ermine smothered both breath and articulation, denying my lips the communion of moonlight. As a harlequin tessellated in the paradoxical geometry of love interwoven with hate, I traversed this labyrinth of striped, threadbare absurdities; my soles, cautious emissaries, negotiated a path over embers that seemed to pulse with sentience. Reluctantly, I bore the weight of thoughts I abhorred, shrouded in the grotesque masquerade of corrupted virtue—a moral integument I could not excise to expose the gleaming scaffolding of ribcage beneath, crystalline in the argent air. Thus I moved, seething with a primal fury as the feral spirit imprisoned within my anatomy rebelled anew, anguished by the dissonance of unblemished bone entombed in sullied flesh.

I wore my silks and furs, but they felt heavier with each moment. The thin black veil did little to shield me, and the thick fur wrapped my face so tightly I couldn’t breathe in the cool moonlight. Like a jester caught between love and hate, I moved through the streets in my tattered, mismatched clothes. Each step felt like walking on burning embers. I carried thoughts I despised, dressed up in lies I couldn’t peel away. Morality clung to me, bound tight to my chest, impossible to strip off and let my ribs gleam bare in the open air. I walked on, angry and restless, a wild spirit trapped inside my bones. I could feel the clean, aching skeleton beneath my flesh crying out for freedom.

Like a jester caught between love and hate, I moved through the streets in my tattered, mismatched clothes. Each step felt like walking on burning embers. I carried thoughts I despised, dressed up in lies I couldn’t peel away. Morality clung to me, bound tight to my chest, impossible to strip off and let my ribs gleam bare in the open air. I walked on, angry and restless, a wild spirit trapped inside my bones. I could feel the clean, aching skeleton beneath my flesh crying out for freedom.

My silken bands, my miniver, Grew burdensome, weightier; The black gauze, frail and thin, Stifled moonlight from within; The ermine cloaked my mouth, my chin. Harlequin of sharp extremes, Love and hate in fractured seams, I stumbled through these tattered roles; Each step upon the pavement coals Burned deep beneath my wary soles. I bore the thoughts I longed to spurn, Their vile veneers my ribs did churn; Morality, though loathed, stayed near, Bound tight, refusing to disappear— A cage of ivory in air. And so I walked, and so I burned; A savage spirit, inward turned, Lashed within this carnal mesh, Bone and soul ensnared in flesh.

My bands of silk and miniver  
Pressed tighter with each passing stir;  
The black gauze thinned to wretched lace,  
While ermine veiled my mouth and face—  
I could not drink the moon’s embrace.  

Harlequin in shards of strife,  
I tread the jagged paths of life,  
Adorned in stripes and frayed designs;  
My soles, on coals, traced wary lines.  

Shouldering thoughts I could not stand,  
Draped in deceit by their own hand,  
Morality clung sharp, ensnared,  
Refusing ribs to strip and share  
Their silver gleam in ivory air.  

I walked, enraged, a spirit bound;  
The savage caged within me frowned,  
Chafed by this mesh of carnal skin—  
Clean bones beneath cried out within.

Bands of silk, threads of weight,  
Miniver cloaked in heavy freight.  
The black gauze thin, a starving veil,  
Ermine smothered breath, the moonlight stale.  
No light to sip, no lunar grail.  

Harlequin steps, love and spite,  
Lozenges twisting in striped flight.  
Ragged tales my soles did trace,  
Warily treading the fiery space,  
Living coals, a burning race.  

Thoughts I bore in tainted guise,  
Morality, a thorn that ties,  
Ribs encased, no air to bare,  
Ivory dreaming silver air.  

Rage walked with me, a savage flare,  
Caged within, raw spirit’s glare.  
Behind the mesh, my flesh did cry,  
Bones clean beneath, yearning sky.

My silken bands and miniver Grew burdensome, a weight to bear; The black gauze thin, a threadbare veil; The ermine stifled breath, so frail— No moonlight’s kiss could pierce the pale. Harlequin, in lozenged guise Of love and hate, with wary strides, I dragged through ragged rigmaroles; My soles, alight on burning coals, Moved cautious where no mercy shoals. Shouldering thoughts I could not quell, Corruptly dressed, I knew too well Morality’s unyielding snare— My ribs could not be laid out bare, Gleaming ivory in air. And so I walked, and so I burned; The savage soul within me yearned, A captive bound in flesh’s mesh— Clean bones lamenting in their flesh.

Bearing thoughts I shunned to know, Their twisted masks a shadowed show, Morality clung, firm, unfair, Bound tight against my ribs, laid bare— Ivory gleaming, gasping air. So I prowled, and so I burned; The caged soul’s primal ache returned, To sense, beneath this fleshly mesh, The stark bones crying in their flesh.

Bearing thoughts I could not stand, Their veils of rot by reason spanned, I could not strip morality’s hold From ribs that gleamed like air-bleached gold, Ivory locked in argent cold. So I walked and burned within, A savage soul, caged deep in skin; Flesh-bound, clean bones cried in vain, Trapped beneath their carnal chain.

Miniver thickened, pulling ever;  
Black gauze stretched, frail and thin,  
Ermine smothered jaw and chin—  
I yearned to drink the moonlight in.  
Harlequin, a patchwork guise  
Of love and hate, beneath dark skies,  
I stumbled through this jagged prose;  
My soles, cautious, where fire glows,  
Each step on coals that seared and froze.  
I bore the thoughts I longed to shed,  
Corruption cloaked in veils of dread;  
Morality clung, raw and rare,  
Ribs aching for the silver air.  
I strode, I burned, a tempest staged,  
The savage spirit, tightly caged,  
Its clean bones screamed, trapped in the flesh,  
A rebel thrashing carnal mesh.

Silken bands grew leaden, dense,  
Miniver's weight a cruel suspense;  
Black gauze, fragile as whispered sin,  
Ermine smothered mouth and chin—  
Moonlight's nectar, locked within.  

A harlequin of love and spite,  
I strode through lozenges of night,  
Ragged stripes, a woven maze,  
Each step burned on coal-lit haze.  
I bore the thoughts I feared to see,  
Dressed in masks of mockery.  

Morality clung, a bone-deep snare,  
Ribs caged in silver, stripped to air.  
I raged, a savage spirit bound,  
A skeleton's howl, raw and profound.  
Flesh, a veil, a carnal screen—  
Clean bones cried for skies serene.

My silken bands and miniver Grew heavier, a weight to bear; The black gauze clung, thin and stark; The ermine smothered breath and spark; I could not sip the moonlight dark. Harlequin in shards of strife, Of love and hate, I paced my life In striped and ragged rituals; My soles recoiled from burning calls, The pavement’s coals, alive with thralls. I bore the thoughts that turned my gaze, Wrapped in their vile, decaying haze; Morality, my ribs confined, Would not release, though I resigned To silver air my ivory mind. My bands of silk and miniver  
Pressed heavier with each shiver;  
The black gauze, a frail disguise,  
The ermine veiled my lips and eyes;  
I hungered for the moonlit skies.  
Harlequin in tangled hues  
Of love and hate, I paced confused—  
Stripes and tatters draped my soul;  
Each cautious step on burning coal  
Bore thoughts I loathed, yet couldn’t unroll.  
Morality clung deep, a thorn,  
Its tainted robes I could not scorn,  
Nor strip my ribs to silver air,  
Exposing ivory stark and bare.  
Thus I walked, a tempest caged;  
This primal howl within enraged—  
Its clean, raw bones still trapped beneath  
The flesh that binds and steals its breath.

Silk bands and miniver draped, yet strained,  
Weighting me as shadows waned;  
The gauze, so thin, dissolved the night,  
While ermine smothered breath and light—  
The moon’s cool kiss stayed out of sight.  

A harlequin of love and spite,  
I stalked in patchwork, torn and tight;  
Each step on cobbles, sharp and seared,  
Where embered whispers hissed and leered.  
Grim thoughts I bore, their masks obscene,  
Clung tightly, veiling what lay clean—  
Morality, though rawly bared,  
Would strip ribs pale in silver air.  

There I strode, and there I burned,  
A savage spirit, caged and spurned;  
Its cry, behind this fleshly bind,  
Was bone-deep, clean, yet cruelly confined.

For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love, Or chide my palsy, or my gout, My five gray hairs, or ruined fortune flout, With wealth your state, your mind with arts improve, Take you a course, get you a place, Observe his honor, or his grace, Or the king's real, or his stamped face Contemplate; what you will, approve, So you will let me love. Alas, alas, who's injured by my love? What merchant's ships have my sighs drowned? Who says my tears have overflowed his ground? When did my colds a forward spring remove? When did the heats which my veins fill Add one more to the plaguy bill? Soldiers find wars, and lawyers find out still Litigious men, which quarrels move, Though she and I do love. Call us what you will, we are made such by love; Call her one, me another fly, We're tapers too, and at our own cost die,

For the sake of all that is sacred, restrain your tongue, and grant me the liberty to love—or if you must speak, direct your reproaches to my trembling limbs, my aching joints, the sparse remnants of my youth’s hair, or the wreckage of my fortune. Exalt your opulence, refine your intellect through cultivated arts, chart your ambitions, secure your station, attend to titles and dignities, or muse upon the sovereign’s visage, whether embossed in coin or borne in flesh. Pursue what pleases you, commend what you will, but leave me to this quiet passion. Who suffers harm from my love? What fleets have foundered beneath the weight of my sighs? Whose lands have my tears submerged? Did my winter breath ever thwart a burgeoning spring? Have the fires coursing through my veins compounded the burden of pestilence? Soldiers still march to war, lawyers still uncover disputes, and quarrels remain the province of litigious minds—yet she and I persist, undisturbed, in our love. Call us what names you wish; love has wrought us thus. Say she is one creature and I another, that we are insects drawn to fleeting flames; we are candles that willingly consume ourselves in pursuit of light.

Please, just let me love. Don’t scold me for my trembling hands or aching joints. Don’t mock my few gray hairs or my poor luck with money. Go ahead and focus on your own life—build up your wealth, sharpen your mind, chase your ambitions. Pay attention to honors, titles, or even the king’s image on a coin. Do whatever pleases you, but let me have this love.

Who’s really hurt by it, anyway? Has any merchant’s ship sunk because of my longing? Has my sadness flooded someone’s fields? When have my chills ever delayed spring’s arrival? Or has the heat in my veins made sickness worse for anyone? Soldiers still go to war. Lawyers still find quarrels to argue over. Life goes on, even though she and I love each other.

Say what you want about us—we are what love has made us. Maybe we’re like moths, or candles burning down by our own light.

For the sake of propriety, restrain your speech and permit me to love. If criticism is required, address my trembling hand, my gout, my few gray hairs, or mock the decline of my fortune. Direct your energies to enhancing your wealth, cultivating your intellect through the arts, pursuing a career, securing a position, or admiring the dignity of a nobleman or the king, whether in his person or as represented on coinage. Occupy yourself with these endeavors, provided you allow me the freedom to love. 

What harm, I ask, is caused by my affection? Have my sighs sunk any merchant’s ships? Has anyone claimed that my tears have flooded their land? Did the coldness of my disposition delay an early spring? Has the heat within my veins added to the toll of any pestilence? Soldiers continue to find wars, and lawyers continue to discover contentious individuals who fuel disputes. None of this is altered by the love shared between her and me. 

Call us whatever you wish; we are shaped by love. Label her as one creature and me as another. We are also like candles, expending ourselves at our own expense to sustain our light.

For mercy’s sake, hush your voice, and let me adore,  
Or mock my trembling hand, my aching frame,  
My silvered locks, or fortune turned to shame.  
With riches grow your stature, your mind with skill refine,  
Seek your path, secure your post,  
Honor his rank, or his grace engross,  
Or ponder the king’s true visage, or his image stamped in coin.  
Do what you will, embrace your choice—  
Only grant me this: to love.  

Why grieve, why cry, for harm my love has never wrought?  
What ships from merchants’ fleets have sunk by my sigh’s gust?  
What fields have drowned beneath my tears?  
Have my chills undone a budding spring?  
Do the fires surging in my veins  
Add weight to the scourge of fevered pain?  
Wars are sought by soldiers, quarrels by law’s hand—  
Yet she and I remain steadfast in love.  

Call us as you please; love shapes us thus.  
Name her one thing, me another: frail as flies,  
Or tapers burning bright, consuming at our own expense—  
And still, we die for love.

For heaven's sake, hush now, and let me adore,  
Or scorn my trembling hand, my aching core,  
Mock my thinning crown or fortune’s shattered store.  
Pursue your wealth, refine your mind with art,  
Chart your own path, secure your part,  
Attend his grace, his honor’s glow,  
Or dwell on the king’s visage—stamped or real—  
Approve what you will, just grant me this zeal:  
To love as I love.  

Oh, tell me, who suffers from my flame?  
What merchant’s fleet has my sighs consumed?  
Whose fields have my tears ever flooded or doomed?  
Have my chills unseated spring’s advance,  
Or my heated veins spread plague by chance?  
Soldiers find battles, lawyers still find strife,  
Yet she and I seek only this life—  
To love as we love.  

Call us what you will; love molds us so.  
Call her one name, and me its twin,  
We’re candles burning, spent from within,  
And gladly we glow.

For heaven’s sake, be silent and let me love,  
Critique my frailties—my trembling hand,  
The sparse gray hairs, or fortune’s crumbled stand.

Shall I compare thee to a summers day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, And summers lease hath all too short a date; Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines, And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; And every fair from fair sometime declines, By chance or natures changing course untrimm'd; But thy eternal summer shall not fade, Nor lose possession of that fair thou owst; Nor shall death brag thou wanderst in his shade, When in eternal lines to time thou growst: So long as men can breathe or eyes can see, So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

Could I liken you to a summer’s day?  
You outshine it, softer, steadier in your grace.  
May’s tender blossoms tremble in rough winds,  
And summer, brief tenant, soon packs its bags.  
The sun, that blazing tyrant, sometimes glares too hot,  
And other days his golden face is veiled in shadow.  
All beauty, sooner or later, slips away,  
Caught in the nets of chance or time’s relentless hand.  
But your summer, your brightness, will not wither;  
The treasure of your fairness shall not fade.  
Death won’t dare to boast you roam his darkened halls,  
For in these undying lines, you’ll walk with time.  
As long as breath is drawn and eyes can see,  
These words will live—and give you life in me.

Should I compare you to a summer’s day? You are lovelier and more gentle. Summer’s winds can be rough, shaking the tender buds of May, and summer itself is far too short. Sometimes the sun blazes too hot, and other times its golden light is dimmed. All beauty fades eventually, whether by chance or the natural passage of time. But your beauty won’t fade; it will stay with you always. Death won’t boast of you being lost in its shadow because your essence will live on in these lasting words. As long as people can breathe and see, this poem will endure, keeping your memory alive forever.

Sometimes the sun blazes too hot, and other times its golden light is dimmed. All beauty fades eventually, whether by chance or the natural passage of time. But your beauty won’t fade; it will stay with you always. Death won’t boast of you being lost in its shadow because your essence will live on in these lasting words. As long as people can breathe and see, this poem will endure, keeping your memory alive forever.

Could I compare you to a summer’s day?  
You’re far more lovely, far more steady, too—  
For May’s bright buds are tossed by winds at play,  
And summer’s lease too swiftly bids adieu.  

At times the sun’s fierce gaze ignites the skies,  
Yet clouds may veil its once resplendent face;  
All beauty wanes, as time and fate revise,  
Unraveling nature’s hand-stitched, fleeting grace.  

But your eternal summer won’t grow pale,  
Nor will you lose the charm you rightly claim;  
Not even death shall cast its chilling veil,  
For time enshrines you in immortal frame.  

So long as breath and sight in humankind,  
This lives, and you in these lines are enshrined.

Might I liken thee to a summer’s day? Thou art more fair, more steady in thy sway: For winds may wrestle May’s dear blooms away, And summer’s tenure fades in swift decay; At times the sun’s fierce gaze grows far too bright, And oft his golden visage veils in grey; All beauty wanes, its course succumbed to blight, By fortune’s whim or nature’s slow disarray; Yet thine eternal summer shall endure, Thy radiance held beyond time’s fleeting hand; Nor shall death boast thou walkst within his lure, While lines immortal bind thee to this land: So long as breath persists and eyes can gleam, This lives to hold thee, thou art life’s own dream.

This verse shall keep your splendor ever told.

Shall I liken thee to a day in June?  
Thy grace outshines, thy heart beats more in tune:  
The gusts may steal the blossoms from the trees,  
And summer’s span is fleeting as a breeze.

By chance or nature’s hand, all will decay.

Thy radiance held, thy loveliness remain;

For in these verses, time thy soul defies.  
As long as breath is drawn and sight is free,  
So long lives this, and this breathes life to thee.

Shall I compare thee to a summers day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, And summers lease hath all too short a date; Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines, And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; And every fair from fair sometime declines, By chance or natures changing course untrimm'd; But thy eternal summer shall not fade, Nor lose possession of that fair thou owst; Nor shall death brag thou wanderst in his shade, When in eternal lines to time thou growst: So long as men can breathe or eyes can see, So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

Shall I weave a tapestry of words, comparing you to a sun-drenched day? You shine brighter, more serene, an oasis of beauty! Wild winds toss the tender buds of May, while summer’s fleeting reign slips through our fingers, too brief to grasp. Sometimes, the blazing eye of heaven scorches too fiercely, and often, its golden glow is veiled in shadows; even the fairest bloom can falter, lost to chance or nature’s whims. Yet your eternal summer, oh, it shall never wither, nor lose the radiant treasure you possess! Death shall not boast of your wandering in his gloom, for in these timeless verses, you thrive! As long as breath fills lungs and eyes drink in light, so long lives this, and through it, you breathe forever.

Stella is sick, and in that sick-bed lies Sweetness, that breathes and pants as oft as she; And grace, sick too, such fine conclusions tries That sickness brags itself best graced to be. Beauty is sick, but sick in so fair guise That in that paleness beautys white we see; And joy, which is inseparate from these eyes, Stella now learns (strange case!) to weep in thee. Love moves thy pain, and like a faithful page, As thy looks stir, runs up and down, to make All folks prest at thy will thy pain to assuage; Nature with care sweats for her darlings sake, Knowing worlds pass, ere she enough can find Of such heaven stuff, to clothe so heavenly mind.

Stella lies in her sickness, a fevered throne where Sweetness gasps and trembles, echoing her every breath. Grace, frail and fever-struck, spins such delicate threads of thought that even illness boasts, crowned by her touch. Beauty, too, wears sickness like a veil, pallid yet radiant, a white so pure it blinds the eye. And Joy—Joy, that loyal companion to her gaze—has turned traitor; Stella learns the bitter art of weeping, her tears strange visitors in those eyes. Love, restless servant, dances in her pain, darting like a shadow to summon the world’s hands to soothe her suffering. Nature herself bends, sweating over her masterpiece, frantic to preserve her treasure, knowing millennia would fail to forge such celestial cloth for a soul so impossibly divine.

Stella is unwell, and lying there in her sickbed, even her sweetness seems to breathe and struggle along with her. Her grace, also afflicted, works so delicately that sickness almost feels proud to be touched by it. Joy, which is usually tied to her bright eyes, now takes a strange turn as Stella learns to cry. Nature works tirelessly for her, sweating with care for this precious soul, knowing it would take ages to gather enough of heaven’s finest essence to match the spirit of one so heavenly.

Stella lies ill, and in her bed remains  
A sweetness breathing soft with labored sighs;  
And grace, though frail, still seeks through thoughtful strains  
To show that sickness wears its finest guise.  
Beauty, though wan, in pale complexion stays,  
Revealing whiteness fair in sorrow’s hue;  
And joy, once constant in her gaze, now sways,  
Strange fate, as tears in her soft eyes accrue.  
Love heeds her pain, a loyal page at hand,  
Responding swift to every glance she makes;  
All strive to ease her suffering at command,  
While Nature, toiling, for her darling aches.  
Such essence rare, so pure, demands all care,  
For worlds may pass, yet none like her compare.

Stella lies ill, and within that frail repose, Sweetness itself breathes tenderly, as she. Grace, too, is worn, yet marvels still compose, For sickness claims such beauty as its plea. Though Beauty wanes, her pallor softly glows— A whiteness fair, unveiled in her decay. And Joy, once bound to Stella’s radiant shows, Now weeps with her, a sorrowed, strange display. Love stirs her pain, a servant swift and true, Following her gaze, it bends the world to care. All hearts, compelled, her suffering pursue; Nature herself, in labored, anxious prayer, Strives to unearth, through time’s unyielding grind, The heaven-stuff to clothe her heavenly mind.

Stella lies ill, her breath and pulse so faint, Sweetness itself now falters, matching her; And grace, though frail, contends with such restraint That sickness seems adorned in its demure. Beauty, too, wanes, yet bears so fair a hue That pallor lends her white a deeper glow; And joy, bound close to those dear eyes of blue, Strange tears now learns from Stella’s pain to flow. Love stirs her ache, like some devoted squire, Attending swift to soothe her heart's command; While nature labors, driven to aspire To craft for her a fitting, rare demand. Worlds may yet pass before she dares to find A form so pure to clothe such heavenly mind.

Stella lies ill, and in her frailty dwells  
A tender sweetness, sighing as she does;  
Grace, too, is weak, yet draws such fine parallels  
That sickness boasts its favor, claiming thus.  
Her beauty wanes, yet pallor softly shows  
A fairness pure, as white as beauty’s hue;  
And joy, once tied to those clear eyes, now flows  
To tears—Stella, strange sorrow weighs on you.  
Love stirs your pain, attending like a page,  
Each glance commanding hearts to soothe your strife;  
While Nature strives, with care and tireless stage,  
To craft, from all the world, enough for life—  
For none but heaven’s rarest threads suffice  
To clothe a mind so noble and precise.

Stella lies ill, and with her illness stays  
A sweetness breathing soft as she does now;  
And grace, though sick, still shapes such perfect ways  
That sickness claims itself adorned somehow.  
Beauty, though pale, reveals in fair disguise  
A whiteness showing beauty's truest hue;  
And joy, once bound within her radiant eyes,  
Learns now, through tears, a sorrow strange yet true.  
Love shares her pain, and, like a loyal aide,  
Responds to every glance, each need to meet,  
Bidding all near her hasten, unafraid,  
To ease the anguish borne by one so sweet.  
Nature, with fervent care, exerts her will,  
Aware no age could craft such grace distilled.

Stella lies ill, her breath frail and low,  
Yet sweetness stirs with each weary sigh;  
Grace, though ailing, weaves such tender glow,  
Sickness itself wears pride, exalted high.  
Beauty wanes, but in its pale disguise,  
A truer white of beauty’s hue is shown;  
And joy, bound once to Stella’s radiant eyes,  
Now learns to weep—strange tears for her alone.  
Love bends to serve, a page at her command,  
Tracing her gaze, restless in its care,  
Summoning all to soothe with gentle hand.  
Nature, frantic, toils in her despair,  
Knowing an age could scarcely hope to find  
Such heaven’s essence for so rare a mind.

Stella lies ill, and in her fevered rest,  
Sweetness still breathes and sighs with every turn;  
Grace, though unwell, makes sickness manifest  
As something proud, her touch a prize to earn.  

Beauty grows pale, yet in that pallor gleams  
A whiter truth, a softer, tender hue;  
And joy, once bound to those bright eyes, now streams  
Through tears that teach her sorrow’s aching view.  

Love, her page, moves swiftly through her pain,  
Chasing each glance to soothe her aching will;  
All hearts bend low, her burdens to contain,  
While Nature labors, striving still and still—  
For worlds may pass, and yet she scarce can find  
Enough divine to clothe such radiant mind.

Stella lies frail, her breath a trembling hymn,  
Sweetness entwined with each faint rise and fall;  
Grace, too, succumbs, yet dares to touch the rim  
Of sickness crowned, its shadow proud and tall.  
Beauty, though wan, a softer truth reveals—  
Pale shades that frame her white, unyielding flame;  
And joy, now pierced, from sorrow gently steals,  
Her tears—unbidden pearls—speak Love’s own name.  

Pain stirs thy Love, a page with steadfast pace,  
Each glance it follows, eager to obey,  
And all the world bends low to find its place,  
To soothe the ache that holds thy light at bay.  
Nature toils on, her craft a fleeting art,

Stella lies ill, and in her frail repose, Sweetness still breathes, still stirs, though labored so; And grace, though wan, such tender beauty shows That sickness claims its finest guise to know. Beauty, though pale, wears white so pure, so rare, Her faint complexion seems a fairer art; And joy, once steadfast, falters in despair, As Stella learns through tears a weeping heart. Love tends her pain, a loyal page in haste, Quick to her glance, to soothe her aching frame; All efforts serve, no moment goes to waste, For Nature toils, her darling to reclaim. Worlds could not yield, though ages sought to find, Such sacred cloth for such a sacred mind.

Stella lies ill, and in that bed confined,  
Sweetness itself breathes labored, soft, and slow.  
Grace falters too, its sharpness redefined,  
Till sickness wears its finest guise to show.  
Beauty, though pale, transforms in such a way  
That whiteness gleams more pure than ever seen;  
And joy, once constant in her gaze’s sway,  
Now learns through tears what sorrow’s depths may mean.  
Love fuels her ache, a page who will not tire,  
Chasing her will, to soothe what pain may rise.  
Nature bends low, her efforts all aspire  
To guard this gem, unmatched beneath the skies.  
For worlds would fail, through endless searching blind,  
To weave such grace for such a heavenly mind.

Stella lies soft in sickness, her breath a trembling hymn,  
Sweetness bound within her, sighing with her dim.  
Grace falters too, yet in its frail descent,  
Sickness itself wears pride, by beauty’s consent.  

Her pallor blooms, a delicate, ghostly white,  
Beauty reshaped, still radiant in its quiet fight.  
And joy, ever bound to the light of her eyes,  
Now bends to sorrow, where soft tears arise.  

Love, steadfast, moves through her aching frame,  
A loyal shadow, chasing her flickering flame.  
It stirs the hearts of all, who rush to her aid,  
Nature herself trembles, her labor displayed.  

For in Stella, the world’s rarest treasures reside—  
A soul so celestial, no stars can outshine her tide.

Stella lies ill, and in her frail repose  
Sweetness breathes faint, as oft as she does sigh;  
Grace, too, falls sick, yet such fine trials shows,  
That sickness boasts its favor drawn thereby.  
Beauty grows pale, but pale in such a way  
That in its pallor, purer white is seen;  
And joy, once steadfast in her gaze's sway,  
Now learns to weep—a marvel unforeseen.  
Love stirs her pain, like some devoted aide,  
Her fleeting looks incite its constant care,  
While throngs attend her whim to lend their aid,  
And Nature strains to guard the form so rare.  
She grasps no worlds could yield enough refined  
To garb with worth such heaven-wrought a mind.

Thou art my lute, by thee I sing, My being is attuned to thee. Thou settest all my words a-wing, And meltest me to melody. Thou art my life, by thee I live, From thee proceed the joys I know; Sweetheart, thy hand has power to give The meed of lovethe cup of woe. Thou art my love, by thee I lead My soul the paths of light along, From vale to vale, from mead to mead, And home it in the hills of song. My song, my soul, my life, my all, Why need I pray or make my plea, Since my petition cannot fall; For Im already one with thee!

You are my lute, and through your strings I sing; my spirit hums in harmony with yours. You lift my words like birds on trembling wings, and melt my heart to music's coursing shores. You are my life, the breath through which I grow, the wellspring of my joy, my pain, my fire; your touch contains the gift of love's warm glow, or bitter draughts that burn but never tire. You are my love, my guide along the way, my lantern through the meadows and the mist; from valley's hush to hilltop's golden play, you lead me home to songs that can't resist. My soul, my song, my all—what need have I to beg or plead? I'm woven through your sky.

My song, my spirit, my life, my everything—why should I ask for more? I am already one with you!

Perspective never withers from their eyes; They keep that docile edict of the Spring That blends March with August Antarctic skies: These are but cows that see no other thing Than grass and snow, and their own inner being Through the rich halo that they do not trouble Even to cast upon the seasons fleeting Though they should thin and die on last years stubble. And they are awkward, ponderous and uncoy . . . While we who press the cider mill, regarding them We, who with pledges taste the bright annoy Of friendships acid wine, retarding phlegm, Shifting reprisals (til who shall tell us when The jest is too sharp to be kindly?) boast Much of our store of faith in other men Who would, ourselves, stalk down the merriest ghost. The lens through which they apprehend the world remains unclouded, unbetrayed by time's attrition; they embody the tranquil decree of Spring, that seamless alchemy which weds March's thaw to August's polar constellations. These beings—mere cattle—discern no reality beyond the dual expanse of pasture and frost, nor beyond the quiet, unfathomed contours of their own essence, mediated through a luminous aura they neither question nor impose upon the transient rhythm of seasons, even as they languish, skeletal, upon the brittle remnants of last year's growth. They are ungainly, deliberate, devoid of guile or pretension.  

Yet we, engaged in the alchemy of cider's labor, gaze upon them from our station; we, whose friendships ferment into a tart elixir of pledges and reproach, whose repartees and retaliations spiral ceaselessly (until, perhaps, the blade of wit severs its kindness altogether), pride ourselves on a fragile faith in our fellow beings—though we, no less than they, would pursue with equal fervor the laughing specter of our undoing. And above it all looms Mizzentop, its pallid grandeur tiered in stoic symmetry, each cinquefoil dormer a sentinel, its strata of windows gazing impassively toward the vanishing echoes of what once was.

Their vision remains unclouded, consistently retaining the calm assurance of spring’s decree, which harmonizes the transitional days of March with the Antarctic skies of August. These creatures—merely cows—perceive nothing beyond the grass beneath them, the snow surrounding them, and the essence of their own existence. They view all of this through an untroubled radiance, unconcerned with casting that light upon the fleeting seasons, even as last year’s stubble grows sparse and their vitality diminishes. They are cumbersome and inelegant in their movements.  

Meanwhile, we, engaged in the labor of the cider mill, observe them. We, who partake in the bittersweet complexities of friendship—symbolized by the sharp tang of wine—are encumbered by shifting retaliations and unresolved tensions. Until the jest becomes too cutting to remain kind, we take pride in our confidence in others, even as we, ourselves, might pursue the remnants of joy like hunters chasing the faintest specter.  

Above them, the grand structure of Mizzentop rises—a white hostelry, floor upon floor, culminating in cinquefoil dormer windows. Its tiered rows of openings gaze outward, impassively, toward the memories of what once was.

Their view never fades; they hold to the simple rule of Spring that links March to August under icy skies. These are just cows, focused only on the grass beneath them, the snow around them, and the quiet world within. They don’t worry about the glow of life they carry, even as the seasons pass and last year’s dried stalks leave little to sustain them. They are clumsy and heavy, without charm or pretense.

Meanwhile, we, busy at the cider press, watch them from afar. We, who sip the sharpness of friendship’s bittersweet wine, pulling back against the irritations it brings. We trade jabs and uneasy jokes, never knowing when the humor becomes too cruel to be kind. We pride ourselves on trusting others, even as we chase down the ghosts of happiness with reckless intent.

Above it all rises old Mizzentop, the grand white inn. Its floors stack high, ending in dormer windows shaped like flowers. Row upon row of windows stare out, unchanging, toward what used to be.

Their gaze retains unyielding perspective; They heed Spring’s quiet decree, weaving March to August beneath Antarctic skies. These simple cattle observe no beyond—only grass, snow, and their essence, framed by an untouched halo that does not disturb passing seasons, even as stubble thins to echo their decline. Clumsy, unwieldy, and unrefined, they stand apart.

We, meanwhile, labor at the cider mill and watch them, tasting the sharp tang of camaraderie’s bittersweet draught—a wine both acid and slow to flow, tempered by shifting grievances. Who can say when jest cuts too deep to linger kindly? Yet we pride ourselves on faith—faith in others who, like us, might chase even the liveliest specter to its end.

Above them looms Mizzentop, pristine and grand, its floors ascending toward cinquefoil dormer windows. Portals stack to stoic ceilings, tier upon tier, gazing outward, fixed on distant days now gone.

Their gaze holds steadfast, never dimming sight; They keep Spring’s quiet promise, soft and pure, That weaves March warmth with August's polar light. These simple cows, their lives so calm, demure, Know grass and snow, and their own depths secure, Framed by a glow they neither shape nor see, Unbothered by the fleeting seasons’ lure, Even as stubble yields to scarcity. They lumber heavy, graceless, plain, and slow… While we, who press the mill and taste the sting Of friendship’s sharp ferment, a tempered woe, Barter reprisals, jest’s keen edges bring— How far before the laughter cuts too deep? Yet pride ourselves on faith in bonds we claim, Trusting in men whose shadows still may creep, Stalking the ghosts of joy too thin to name. Above them looms Mizzentop, grand and stark, Its walls ascend, tiered heights of stoic grace, White gables pierce the sky, a watchful ark, Windows aligned to frame the past's embrace.

Their gaze holds steadfast, unyielding to decay;  
They carry Spring’s quiet decree within,  
Binding March to August’s Antarctic gray:  
These are but cows, their world fenced in  
By grass, by snow, by the pulse that begins  
And ends in their own serene reflection,  
Unbothered by the seasons’ fleeting spins,  
Though last year’s stubble spells their desolation.  

They lumber, clumsy, devoid of coy finesse…  
While we, who grind the cider’s pressing strain,  
We, who sip the tartness of bonds that oppress,  
Trading barbs (until who can gauge the pain,  
When humor cuts too deep to entertain?),  
Claim richer trust in men we hold so dear,  
Yet, like them, chase phantoms down memory’s lane,  
Ghosts mocked in jest, though laughter masks our fear.  

Above, old Mizzentop looms, stately and vast,  
Its white expanse rising, floor upon floor,  
Cinquefoil dormers porthole skies overcast,  
Ceilings stacked stoic, gazing evermore.

Their gaze does not falter, nor dim with time; They hold the quiet decree of the Spring, Where March and Antarctic August combine: These are but cows, seeing no other thing Than snow and grass, and their own depths aglow Through halos they neither shape nor disturb— Even as seasons ebb, even as they grow Thin and fade, grazing last year’s brittle curb. They are unwieldy, solemn, and unadorned… While we, who spin the cider press, observe them— We, who sip the tart bite of bonds well-worn, Swallowing sour joys, feigned salves for the phlegm Of time’s reprisals (how long till the jest Cuts too deep for warmth?) parade faith’s banners, Certain our trust in men outlasts the rest— We, who’d chase down even the ghostliest clamor. Above them looms Mizzentop, austere and white, Tier on tier, its cinquefoil dormers peer Through stoic portholes, ceiling stacked on height, Windows staring toward what was, sharp and clear.

Perspective lingers, unfading from their gaze; They hold the gentle decree of the Spring That marries March to August’s polar haze: These are but cattle, fixed upon one thing— Grass and frost, and their own quiet being Through the warm aura they neither disturb Nor cast upon the fleeting, fragile seasons, Though they may thin and perish on old turf. Perspective lingers, evergreen in their gaze; They hold Spring’s gentle decree, unbreaking, That marries March to August’s polar haze: Mere cows, their world is grass and snow, unshaking In their quiet truth, unbothered by the glow Of halos they wear, unwittingly bestowed. Seasons fleeting—thin stubble, frost below— Yet they stand, vast and steady, as if they know.  

Awkward, weighty, unpolished in their plod... While we, cider-pressing hands stained and sore, We, who sip friendships' tartness, call it God, And trade sharp jesting till kindness frays the core— We boast our faith, while ghosts we stalk implore Merriment, brittle as frosted leaves in flight.  

Above them, Mizzentop rises, sheer and grand: Floors climbing skyward, cinquefoil dormers bright. Its porthole windows stare across the land; Stoic tiers of ceilings stack their measured height.

Vision, unyielding, lingers in their eyes; They hold to Spring’s serene decree, unbending, Where March and August share Antarctic skies. These cattle, bound to grass and snow, unending, Peer inward, haloed thoughts untroubled by The fleeting seasons—stubble thin, decayed, They stand, resigned, as time and years pass by. Awkward, weighty forms, ungraceful, unswayed.  

And we, who tend the cider press, observe— With pledges sip the tartness friendship brings, Its acid flow that tests what faith preserves; Retaliations shift like shadowed wings, Till jest’s keen edge cuts deeper than intended. We boast of trust among our fellow men Yet stalk the specters mirth has apprehended, Whispered truths dissolving in the bright amen.  

Above them, Mizzentop’s grand palace gleams— Its cinquefoil dormers pierce the stoic height. Floor by floor, its ceilings stretch through dreams; Long panes gaze outward, steady in their light, Out toward the past, where pale horizons teem.

Their gaze holds steady, unyielding to decay; They keep the quiet mandate of the Spring, That fuses March with August’s polar grey. These are but cattle, fixed on one sure thing— Grass and snow, and a self-contained array Of being, framed in halos they don’t strain To cast upon fleeting seasons on display, Though stubble thins and last year’s fields remain. They move with bulk, ungainly, without art... While we, who turn the cider press, observe, We, who with promises sip the tart Departures of friendship’s sharp reserve, Trading rebukes (who knows when jest’s edge swerves Too far for kindness?), often pride our trust In others’ faith, though we ourselves might curve To stalk the specter of joy turned to dust. Above them looms old Mizzentop, its grand White edifice stacked floor by floor, with shape And symmetry toward dormers that command Portholes in ceilings, stoic in their scrape.

Their gaze, unyielding, holds perspective’s bloom;  
A tender law of Spring they softly keep,  
Where March and August blend in polar gloom.  
These are but cows, their vision fixed, their sweep  
Bound to the grass and snow, their inner glow  
Untouched by fleeting seasons’ fragile rush,  
Even as last year’s stubble thins below,  
And life retreats beneath time’s subtle hush.  

Clumsy, heavy, graceless they remain,  
While we, who spin the cider’s biting yield,  
We, tasting friendships sharp and sweet refrain,  
Shift bonds and bruises, barely standing shield.  
We laugh, yet wonder when the jest turns cruel,  
Boasting of faith in men, though shadows stalk  
The liveliest ghost beneath our brittle rule—  
A fragile pact, a splintered, brittle talk.  

Above them looms old Mizzentop’s array:  
Its stoic tiers, white walls, cinquefoil dormers,  
Each window stares, unblinking toward decay,  
Ceilings that stack toward heights that time transforms.

Their gaze does not falter, nor perspective fade; They hold Spring’s quiet decree, serene and mild, That marries March to August’s glacial shade: Cows, unseeing but for grass, undefiled By thought of seasons’ swift and fleeting trial, Peer through the golden glow they do not bend To shape or scatter, though the fragile aisle Of stubble starves and leads them to their end. They move ungainly, solemn, coarse of frame… While we, who turn the press and sip the sting Of friendship’s tart refrain, still stake our claim On faith in men, though laughter’s barbed edge bring Retorts unkind (when will the jest relent?), And boast of bonds while shadows haunt our track. Above them Mizzentop’s ascent, content To pierce the sky in stoic, tiered white stack. Its cinquefoil dormers, portholes proud and bright, Survey the past through lofty, frozen height.

Their gaze holds firm, unweathered by the years; They bear Spring’s quiet creed, a softened thread That knots March’s thaw to August’s polar spheres: Just cattle, lost to all but grass, widespread Snowfields, and their own depths, a light unsaid Circling their days untouched, unmarked by reason, Blind to the seasons’ march, though hunger’s dread Thins them to shadows clinging to spent season. Flaunting our trust in men—though truth’s a ghost That haunts us back, a specter we deserve, Mocking the faith we claim to cherish most. Above, old Mizzentop’s white sprawl ascends, Floor upon floor to cinquefoil eyes that stare— Tiered windows watch as heights refuse to bend, Stoic, unmoved, to time’s unraveling air.

The lens through which they perceive reality remains untouched by the erosion of time; they adhere to a serene mandate of renewal that unites the transitional months of March with the frigid expanse of Antarctic August. These creatures, mere cattle, are ensnared in a singular existence, fixated solely on the dichotomy of verdant grass and the starkness of snow, their consciousness encased within a luminous sphere they neither question nor extend to encompass the ephemeral seasons, even as they wither upon the remnants of last year's harvest. Clumsy and unrefined, they are oblivious to the complexities surrounding them. In contrast, we, the architects of the cider mill, scrutinize their existence, partaking in the sharp, invigorating essence of camaraderie distilled into a tart elixir, stifling our own inertia. We engage in a delicate dance of retribution, pondering the limits of jest, while professing our belief in humanity, even as we ourselves pursue the most elusive specters of mirth. Towering above them, the venerable Mizzentop stands as a grand edifice, each floor meticulously ascending to meet the sky, its windows gazing out toward a bygone era.

I never knew the earth had so much gold The fields run over with it, and this hill Hoary and old, Is young with buoyant blooms that flame and thrill. Such golden fires, such yellowlo, how good This spendthrift world, and what a lavish God! This fringe of wood, Blazing with buttercup and goldenrod. You too, beloved, are changed. Again I see Your face grow mystical, as on that night You turned to me, And all the trembling worldand youwere white. Aye, you are touched; your singing lips grow dumb; The fields absorb you, color you entire . . . And you become A goddess standing in a world of fire!

I never guessed the earth could hold such gold—  
Fields spilling over, a molten flood, this hill,  
Ancient, cold,  
Now crowned with blooms that burn and pulse and thrill.  
Such wild infernos, yellow’s endless tide,  
This world, a spendthrift’s dream, God’s open hand!  
The woods abide,  
Their edges blazing—goldenrod, buttercup stand.  

And you, my love, have shifted, changed anew—  
Your face, aglow, returns to that strange light,  
As once it drew  
The trembling world—and you—into pure white.  
Yes, you are touched; your singing lips fall still,  
The fields consume you, drench you in their fire…  
Until you fill  
The air, a goddess wreathed in flame’s desire!

Such brilliant colors, such richness; this generous world reflects a benevolent deity. This border of trees is illuminated with buttercups and goldenrods. You, too, have transformed. I again observe your face becoming ethereal, reminiscent of that evening when you turned towards me, and the entire world appeared radiant and pale. Indeed, you are altered; your once expressive lips are now silent. The fields envelop you, saturating you completely, and you emerge as a goddess within a realm of brilliance.

Care for thy soul as thing of greatest price, Made to the end to taste of power divine, Devoid of guilt, abhorring sin and vice, Apt by Gods grace to virtue to incline. Care for it so as by thy retchless train It be not brought to taste eternal pain. Care for thy corse, but chiefly for souls sake; Cut off excess, sustaining food is best; To vanquish pride but comely clothing take; Seek after skill, deep ignorance detest. Care so, I say, the flesh to feed and clothe That thou harm not thy soul and body both. Care for the world to do thy body right; Rack not thy wit to win thy wicked ways; Seek not to oppress the weak by wrongful might; To pay thy due do banish all delays. Care to dispend according to thy store, And in like sort be mindful of the poor. Care for thy soul, as for thy chiefest stay; Care for thy body for thy souls avail; Care for the world for bodys help alway; Care yet but so as virtue may prevail. Care in such sort that thou be sure of this: Care keep thee not from heaven and heavenly bliss.

Attend to thy soul as the jewel of utmost worth, fashioned for communion with the transcendent and attuned to the harmonies of divine potency. Let it remain untainted, abhorring the corrosive pull of vice, and by grace be drawn toward the luminous arc of virtue. Guard it with vigilance, lest through careless indulgence it be cast into the abyss of perpetual anguish.

Tend to thy corporeal frame, yet subordinate its care to the soul’s higher claim. Eschew excess; moderation sustains both flesh and spirit. Adorn thyself with humility, for clothing should serve propriety, not vanity. Seek knowledge, and let ignorance be thy foe, cultivating the mind without impoverishing the essence of thy being.

Engage with the world not as an end but as a means. Pursue no path of cunning that veers toward oppression or cruelty; wield thy strength not to subdue but to uphold justice, and discharge thy obligations without delay. Distribute thy wealth with discernment, remembering the plight of the impoverished as thou measurest thy own sufficiency.

In all thy cares, let virtue dictate thy measure. Care for the soul, the body, and the world as interconnected spheres, yet never permit thy attentions to these lesser domains to eclipse the celestial inheritance awaiting thee. Let thy care be the tether that secures thee to heaven and its eternal beatitude.

Guard thy soul, treasure beyond all worth,  
Forged to revel in the spark divine.  
Let no shadow stain its holy birth,  
Turn it from sin, let virtue’s stars align.  
Guard it so no reckless step or snare  
Should hurl it down to the abyss of despair.  

Tend thy flesh, yet for thy soul’s fair throne;  
Shun excess—plain bread will serve thee best.  
Dress to humble pride, not boast alone,  
Seek wisdom’s light, let ignorance be detest.  
Feed and clothe the body, but take great care—  
To harm thy soul would lay both to despair.  

Turn to the world, but only as is just;  
Twist not thy wit to carve out crooked gains.  
Spare the weak from the hammer of thy lust,  
And give what’s due, let not greed forge thy chains.  
Spend with thy means, yet still thy heart implore:  
Forget not the poor who linger at thy door.  

Guard thy soul—it holds thy life’s true stay.  
Guard thy flesh, but for thy soul’s delight.  
Turn to the world, but tread the narrow way,  
Let virtue guide and keep thy path aright.  
Guard all with care, and let this truth be plain:  
No care should bar thee from the heavenly plain.

Guard thy soul as treasure of highest worth,  
Designed to taste the grace of power divine,  
Free from all guilt, abhorring vice from birth,  
Drawn by God's favor toward virtue's design.  
Preserve it well, lest through neglectful care  
It falls to know eternal pain's despair.  

Tend to thy body, yet for thy soul's aim;  
Shun excess—simple sustenance is best.  
Clothe with restraint, to vanquish pride's bold claim;  
Seek knowledge deep, let ignorance find rest.  
So feed and clothe thy flesh with measured hand  
That harm to neither soul nor form withstand.  

Engage the world with justice as thy guide;  
Twist not thy wit to chase unrighteous gain.  
Let not thy strength oppress the weak in pride;  
Pay debts on time, let no delay remain.  
Spend as thou hast, with thoughts upon the poor,  
Balancing wisely what thy hands procure.  

Care for thy soul, thy surest anchor here;  
Care for thy body, serving soul's accord.  
Care for the world, as means to persevere;  
Yet care with virtue's triumph as reward.  
Attend in ways that keep thy purpose clear:  
Let not thy care bar heaven's blissful sphere.

Hold thy soul close, a treasure sublime,  
Wrought for the taste of celestial design,  
Free from guilt, spurning shadow and grime,  
By grace, its virtue destined to shine.  
Guard it well, lest reckless tides betray,  
And cast it into eternal dismay.  

Cherish thy form, yet soul above all;  
Deny excess—let sustenance suffice.  
Wrap pride in modesty, let grandeur fall;  
Seek wisdom’s fire, spurn ignorance’s ice.  
So tend thy flesh, with measured care bestowed,  
That both thy body and spirit be whole.  

Honor the earth, but shun wicked schemes;  
Strain not thy mind for paths unjustly paved.  
Oppress not the frail, nor chase cruel dreams;  
Render thy dues, let debts be waived.  
Spend as thy store allows, yet spare a thought,  
To aid the poor—give what kindness has wrought.  

Lift thy soul as thy anchor, thy light;  
Serve thy body, but for its sacred aim.  
Walk the world gently, with virtue in sight;  
Let righteousness kindle, and sin be tamed.  
So care, with grace, that heaven’s doors remain  
Unbarred for thee, free of sorrow or stain.

Guard thy soul as treasure most esteemed, Designed to taste the power of the divine, Free from guilt, from sin and vice redeemed, By grace inclined toward virtue's noble line. Guard it, lest by heedless course of care It meet the pangs of everlasting despair.  

Attend thy flesh, but chiefly for soul's gain; Curb excess—choose sustaining food as best; To humble pride, wear clothing modest, plain; Pursue the skill that ignorance detests. Attend thy needs, yet ever seek to prove Thy body and thy soul in balanced love.  

Engage the world to meet thy body's need; Strain not thy mind for paths of wrongful gain; Oppress no weak, nor let thy might exceed; Pay what thou owe, and banish all delay. Spend wisely, keeping store within thy sight, And hold the poor within thy guiding light.  

Guard thy soul, thy anchor and thy stay; Tend thy body for thy soul's advance; Serve the world to aid thy flesh's way; Yet act that virtue holds its steadfast stance. Guard all with care, but chiefly mark this truth: Care must not bar thee from eternal youth.

Guard well thy soul, the treasure most profound,  
Shaped to embrace the touch of power divine,  
Unstained by guilt, where virtue’s roots are found,  
By grace inclined to spurn all sin’s design.  
So tend it well, lest by neglectful art  
It taste of torment's sting and rend thy heart.  

Cherish thy frame, yet for thy soul’s intent;  
Shun glutton’s call—let sustenance suffice.  
Clothe not in pride, but garb most reverent,  
And seek the light where ignorance hides twice.  
Feed flesh and clothe it with a measured care,  
Harm not the balance both must rightly share.  

Regard the world, yet keep thy dealings clean;  
Twist not thy mind to schemes of wrongful might.  
Oppress not those who weakly tread unseen;  
Delay no dues, pursue what’s just and right.  
Spend wisely, yet the poor must know thy share;  
Their need shall call thy heart to meet them there.  

Hold fast thy soul—it is thy steadfast guide;  
Thy body serve, yet for its spirit’s gain.  
The world sustain, but let not virtue slide;  
Let righteousness o’er fleeting wealth remain.  
So let thy care on earth be rightly placed,  
That heaven’s bliss thy journey’s end may grace.

Guard thy soul, thy most treasured grace,  
Crafted to revel in divine embrace.  
Shun all guilt, let sin take flight,  
By God’s mercy, steer to virtue’s light.  
Guard it well, lest careless strife  
Condemn its taste to eternal life.  

Tend thy frame, yet for the soul’s gain;  
Trim excess, let modest fare sustain.  
Let pride be quelled by humble dress,  
And wisdom sought, ignorance suppress.  
So nurture flesh with measured care,  
That soul and body harm not bear.  

Serve the world with justice and right;  
Turn not thy mind to wicked delight.  
Crush no weakling with wrongful force,  
Pay thy dues without remorse.  
Spend with prudence, yet ever be  
Mindful of those in poverty.  

Guard thy soul, thy anchor strong;  
Guard thy body, but not for long,  
Save it serves thy spirit’s need—  
Let virtue thrive, let sin recede.  
Guard all, yet know this truth divine:  
Let care not steal heaven’s design.

Guard thy soul, a treasure of supreme worth,  
Shaped to partake in power divine and pure;  
Free from guilt, rejecting vice from its birth,  
Guided by grace, toward virtue to endure.  
Guard it with care, lest heedless steps betray,  
And lead thee where eternal grief holds sway.  

Tend to thy flesh, yet for thy soul's own good;  
Shun excess—simple sustenance is wise.  
Let modest dress subdue vain pride as it should;  
Pursue true knowledge, ignorance despise.  
So care for body, clothing, food, and rest,  
That neither soul nor flesh be dispossessed.  

Engage the world to meet thy body's need;  
Seek no false gain nor tread on others’ right.  
Avoid oppression born of selfish greed;  
Discharge thy debts without unjust respite.  
Spend as thou canst, and let the poor partake;  
Such mindful care thy earthly path will make.  

Guard thy soul, thy foremost anchor and guide;  
Tend to thy body, serving soul’s demand.  
Use worldly goods to keep thy needs supplied,  
Yet live by virtue, held in firm command.  
So care, that heaven’s bliss thy goal remains,  
And worldly toil no lasting loss sustains.

Guard thy soul, the treasure most divine,  
Made to ascend where sacred powers shine.  
Flee from sin, let vice be cast away,  
And, by God's grace, let virtue guide thy way.  
Attend it well, lest careless steps betray,  
And lead thee down to pain that shall not sway.  

Cherish thy flesh, but for thy soul’s own good;  
Cut off excess, choose simple, wholesome food.  
Let pride be quelled; wear garments clean, yet plain,  
Pursue true wisdom, ignorance disdain.  
Provide thy care, but measure all with thought,  
That soul and body both be left unsought.  

Tend to the world, yet with a tempered hand;  
Refrain from schemes where wicked gains expand.  
Oppress not those whose strength may waver thin,  
And swiftly pay the debts thy honor wins.  
Spend with restraint, and aid the poor in need,  
Let modest charity thy heart precede.  

Guard thy soul, thy anchor through all days;  
Feed thy body, yet in virtue’s praise.  
Attend the world, but only so that grace  
May guide thy steps toward heaven’s vast embrace.  
Let care, in balance, lead thee to the skies,  
Where bliss eternal waits and never dies.

Guard well thy soul, a treasure of high worth,  
Shaped to partake of powers divine and pure;  
Shun guilt, and turn from sin's degrading mirth,  
By grace inclined to virtues that endure.  
Watch o’er it lest thy heedless steps invite  
A fate that leads to everlasting night.  

Tend to thy frame, yet for thy soul take care;  
Restrain excess; let simple fare suffice.  
Pride’s vanquishment in modest garb declare;  
Seek wisdom’s light, and ignorance despise.  
Sustain thy flesh and clothe it prudently,  
Yet harm not both by acting recklessly.  

Deal with the world to meet thy body's needs;  
Warp not thy wit to tread in wicked schemes.  
Oppress no weakling through unjust misdeeds;  
Pay debts in time; let sloth not mar thy dreams.  
Spend with due thought, in measure to thy store,  
And share thy fortune with the needy poor.  

Cherish thy soul, thy anchor and thy guide;  
Care for thy body to preserve its aid.  
Engage the world, yet let thy virtue bide,  
And all thy dealings by its rule be weighed.  
Be sure in this: thy care must not dismiss  
The path that leads to heaven’s eternal bliss.

Guard well thy soul, thy treasure most supreme,  
Formed to embrace the touch of the divine,  
Untainted, scorning vice’s shadowed scheme,  
Inclined through grace toward virtue’s sacred line.  
Shield it, lest through careless, fleeting chase,  
It stumble toward eternal, dark disgrace.

Wear modest garb, let pride no foothold take;

Thus nourish flesh and clothe it with due care,  
That soul and body both no harm may bear.  

Serve this wide world, but in just measure stay;  
Twist not thy wit for paths of crooked gain.  
Oppress none weakly; shun such wrongful sway.  
Discharge thy debts; let not delay remain.  
Give as thou hast, and aid the needy poor;  
So spend thy wealth, yet keep thy spirit sure.  

Cherish thy soul, thy steadfast, guiding light;  
Cherish thy body for thy soul’s own aim.  
Serve thou the world to guard thy body’s right;  
Yet let not virtue’s flame be dimmed by shame.  
Care thus, that heaven’s path thou surely see:  
Let care itself not steal thy bliss from thee.

Guard thy soul, a treasure most divine,  
Shaped to embrace a power vast and pure,  
Untouched by sin, to virtue’s path incline,  
By grace and wisdom made to stand secure.  
Guard it with care, lest reckless steps betray  
Its peace to endless night, its light to sway.

Let pride be quelled; adorn in modest thrall;  
Seek knowledge deep, and ignorance detest.  
So feed and clothe the body’s earthly form,  
That soul and frame both weather life’s storm.  

Engage the world with fairness as thy creed;  
Bend not thy mind to wicked, fleeting gain.  
Oppress no weak, nor fuel ambition’s greed;  
Pay debts with haste, and honor’s course maintain.  
Spend wisely from thy store, yet always share,  
And keep the poor in heart and constant care.  

Guard thy soul, thy anchor and thy guide;  
Tend thy body for the spirit’s aim;  
Care for the world, yet let thy heart decide  
To walk where virtue glows, unmoved by shame.  
Let thy care rise, but never dim the flame—  
The path that leads to heaven’s endless claim.

Tend to thy soul, a treasure of highest worth,  
Shaped for the taste of divine power's birth.  
Free it from guilt, let no vice take its part,  
By grace inclined to virtue's steadfast heart.  
Guard it with care, lest reckless ways conspire  
To cast it deep in eternal fire.  

Nurture thy flesh, yet for the soul's true gain;  
Curb excess—simple sustenance sustain.  
Dress with humility, let pride's hold break,  
Seek wisdom's light, and ignorance forsake.  
Feed and clothe thy form with measured grace,  
So neither soul nor body lose their place.  

In worldly dealings, act with grounded care;  
Twist not thy wits for crooked paths unfair.  
Oppress not the weak with might's wrongful sway;  
Pay what is due without unjust delay.  
Spend as thy means allow, yet mind the poor;  
Balance thy wealth to open virtue's door.  

Cherish thy soul, thy anchor and thy guide;  
Care for thy body that thy soul may abide.  
Engage the world, but only so to aid,  
That virtue's light through all remains displayed.  
Let every care align with this firm bliss:  
No care should bar thee from heaven's promised kiss.
