Nurture thy spirit as the most precious entity, crafted to ultimately partake in the divine essence, untainted by guilt and repulsed by moral decay, naturally inclined, through divine grace, toward virtue. Tend to it with such fervor that it is not condemned to endure eternal torment. Attend to thy corporeal vessel, yet prioritize the well-being of the soul; eliminate excess, for moderation sustains, and to conquer hubris, adorn thyself with modest raiment; pursue knowledge, while casting aside the shackles of ignorance. Attend to the nourishment and attire of the flesh in such a manner that neither soul nor body suffers detriment. Engage with the world to ensure the corporeal thrives; do not torment your intellect in pursuit of nefarious ends; refrain from subjugating the vulnerable through unjust power; expunge procrastination in fulfilling your obligations. Allocate resources judiciously, remaining ever mindful of the destitute. Care for your soul, as it is your paramount foundation; care for your body for the benefit of the soul; care for the world for the continual aid of the body; yet let your care be such that virtue remains supreme. Exercise care in this manner, ensuring that your attentiveness does not obstruct your path to celestial realms and the bliss of the divine.

Nurture your soul as the most valuable treasure, Designed to experience divine power, free from guilt, Rejecting sin and vice, inclined by God's grace toward virtue. Tend to it diligently, lest your reckless ways lead to eternal suffering. While caring for your body is important, prioritize the well-being of your soul; Moderation in sustenance is wise, as excess can be detrimental. Overcome pride with modest attire; cultivate wisdom and shun ignorance. Strive to nourish and clothe the flesh without harming your soul or body. Attend to the world to maintain your physical health; do not exhaust your mind in pursuit of unjust gains. Avoid oppressing the vulnerable with wrongful strength; fulfill your obligations without procrastination. Spend according to your means, and remember the less fortunate. Care for your soul, as it is your ultimate support; tend to your body for the sake of your soul; engage with the world for your physical benefit. Yet, ensure that virtue remains your guiding principle. Care in such a way that you safeguard against anything that would distance you from heaven and its eternal bliss.

Nurture your soul, the treasure of your being, Crafted to embrace the divine's embrace, Free from guilt, rejecting vice's deceiving, By the grace of God, to virtue you’ll race. Tend to it fiercely, guard against the pain, Let not your reckless ways bring you disdain. Cherish your body, yet let soul take lead; Trim excess, find sustenance in restraint; Conquer pride, let clothing be modest indeed; Seek wisdom’s light, shun ignorance's taint. Care for both flesh and spirit, intertwined, That neither soul nor body suffers strife. Honor the world, let justice be your guide; Don’t twist your mind for wicked gains or lies; Do not oppress the weak with ruthless pride; Act swiftly, pay your dues, let no delay arise. Spend wisely, mindful of your store, And in like measure, reach out to the poor. Care for your soul, your anchor in the storm; Care for your body, for your soul’s embrace; Care for the world, nurturing life’s form; Yet let your virtue guide you in this race. Care so that you may surely find this bliss: Let not your heart stray from heaven’s gentle kiss.

Attend to the world for your body’s sake; Do not exhaust your mind for wicked gain; Oppress not the weak, for justice must awake; Fulfill your duties, dispel delay’s disdain. Spend wisely, mindful of what you possess, Remember the poor, let generosity bless.

Time only cause of my unrest By whom I hopd once to bee blest How cruell art thou turned? That first gavst lyfe unto my love, And still a pleasure nott to move Or change though ever burned; Have I thee slackd, or left undun One loving rite, and soe have wunn Thy rage or bitter changing? That now noe minutes I shall see, Wherein I may least happy bee Thy favors soe estranging. Blame thy self, and nott my folly, Time gave time butt to bee holly; True love such ends best loveth Unworhty love doth seeke for ends A worthy love butt worth pretends Nor other thoughts itt proveth: Then stay thy swiftnes cruell time, And lett mee once more blessed clime To joy, that I may prayse thee: Lett mee pleasure sweetly tasting Joy in love, and faith nott wasting And on fames wings Ile rayse thee: Never shall thy glory dying Bee untill thine owne untying That time noe longer liveth; Tis a gaine such tyme to lend: Since soe thy fame shall never end Butt joy for what she giveth.

O Time, the restless hand that shakes my peace,  
Once I thought you’d grant me sweet release,  
But how sharp and strange you’ve grown!  
You breathed the spark into my heart’s first flame,  
Yet now you stand, unmoved, the same,  
A cold king on a stony throne.  

Did I falter? Did I fail some vow,  
A sacred rite I’ve broken somehow,  
To earn your cruel disdain?  
For now, each fleeting moment mocks,  
No joy unlocks, no love unshocks—  
Your favor turns to pain.  

Blame yourself, not my heart’s design;  
You gave the hours to grow divine,  
For love that seeks no gain.  
The shallow heart may chase an end,  
But true love holds, will not pretend,  
And walks a truer plane.  

So halt your rush, relentless Time,  
Let me climb, once more, the heights sublime,  
To taste joy’s sweetest kiss.  
Let love stand pure, let faith remain;  
Then I’ll sing your endless reign,  
And raise your name in bliss.  

Your glory shall not dim or sever,  
So long as love endures forever—  
A gift that Time redeems.

Time, the sole cause of my unrest,  
From whom I once hoped to be blessed,  
How mercilessly you have turned—  
You who first gave life to my love,  
Yet remain unmoved, unchanging,  
Though ever consuming in desire.  

Have I been negligent or failed  
In fulfilling love's proper rites,  
Thus provoking your anger and bitter change?  
Now no moment shall I witness  
In which I may be even slightly blessed,  
Your favor so distantly withdrawn.  

Do not hold my folly to account;  
The fault lies with you, not with me.  
Time was given to serve holiness,  
For true love pursues no selfish ends.  
It seeks only worth and sincerity,  
Testing no thoughts beyond these.  

Therefore, restrain your cruel speed,  
And grant me once more a blessed ascent  
To joy, that I may honor you.  
Let me savor pleasure without decay,  
Rejoice in steadfast love and faith,  
And through my praise, lift your renown.  

Your glory shall endure undiminished,  
Lasting until your own cessation,  
When time itself shall cease to be.  
Such time, freely lent, is no loss,  
For it ensures your fame eternal,  
And joy in what you bestow.

Time, you were meant to bring me peace,  
A gift I once hoped would never cease.  
But how cruel you've become,  
You gave life to my love, it's true,  
Yet now you stand still, unmoved too,  
While my heart burns, undone.  

Did I neglect some sacred vow?  
Have I failed you somehow?  
Is that why you've turned so cold?  
No longer granting even a moment,  
Not a single instant of contentment,  
Your kindness withheld, untold.  

Don't blame me—look to yourself.  
Time exists not for selfish wealth.  
True love seeks no fleeting ends;  
It only shows what worth it holds,  
Not chasing gains or stories told,  
But proving what it defends.  

So slow your pace, relentless time,  
Let me climb again to joy’s high rhyme,  
To taste sweet pleasure once more.  
Let love and faith renew their place,  
And I’ll lift your name with endless praise,  
Your fame forever secure.  

Your glory shall never fade away,  
Not until your final day,  
When time itself is gone.  
Grant us moments rich and true,  
For lending time is gain for you,  
In joy, your name lives on.

Time, architect of my disquiet,  
Once the lodestar of my aspiring spirit—  
By what grim alchemy hast thou transfigured,  
Thou who first didst quicken the flame of my devotion,  
Yet now, immutable in thy austere pleasure,  
Dost watch unyielding as I smolder?  
Have I, through negligence or omission,  
Failed some sacred ordinance of affection,  
And thus provoked thy tempestuous caprice?  
For now, no fragment of thy measure permits me  
Even the faintest reprieve of felicity,  
Thy countenance grown so estranged.  

Accuse not my frailty but thine own essence,  
For thou wert allotted to sanctify moments, not to squander them.  
True love, uncorrupted, seeks no terminal purpose,  
But rests in the equilibrium of worth itself;  
Unworthy love, by contrast, craves utility,  
And thereby betrays its shallow constitution.  

Pause, then, thou merciless arbiter of flux,  
And grant me once more the summit of delight,  
That I might hymn thy virtues anew:  
Permit me the nectar of unblemished love,  
Faith undiminished, joy undiluted,  
And I shall lift thee upon fame’s eternal wings.  

Thy renown shall not fade while thou endurest,  
For only with thine own cessation shall it falter—  
Yet such a gift as love’s renewal, freely bestowed,  
Would render thy glory imperishable,  
And enshrine thy name in the annals of joy unending.

Time, the sole tormentor of my peace,  
Once a harbinger of hoped release—  
How cruelly you’ve turned, unkind!  
You gave life to love’s tender bloom,  
Yet now, unmoved by joy or gloom,  
You keep your course confined.  

Have I neglected some vital rite,  
Or failed in love’s steadfast fight,  
Thus earning your bitter disdain?  
No longer will moments appear  
Where happiness might draw me near;  
Your favor’s estranged domain.  

Blame yourself, not my erring heart—  
Time was granted to keep love’s art  
Holy, unspoiled and pure.  
True love seeks no fleeting goals;  
Worthy love in worth extols,  
And no false thoughts endure.  

Pause your haste, relentless time,  
Let me ascend once more the climb  
To joy, where praise might flow.  
Let sweet pleasure, love’s delight,  
Faith unspent, and fames take flight,  
Raise your name aglow.  

Your glory shall never fade—  
Its end by your own hand delayed,  
So long as time persists.  
Grant this boon, and fame shall rise;  
A joyous gift, love sanctifies,  
Eternal in what it gifts.

Time, the root of my unease,  
Once my hope, now harsh disease—  
How cruel your turn to spite!  
You sparked the flame of love’s first glow,  
Yet stir no joy, no ebb, no flow,  
Though passion burns outright.  

Have I faltered, left undone  
Some sacred vow, some promised sun,  
To earn your bitter turning?  
No moment now can mercy bring,  
No fraction where my heart might sing,  
Your favor ever spurning.  

Blame yourself, not my intent;  
Time was lent for love’s ascent—  
True love seeks no swift ends.  
Unworthy hearts chase fleeting aims,  
While worthy love, steadfast, remains,  
Through trials it defends.  

So halt your stride, relentless Time,  
And grant this soul a blessed climb  
To joy, to praise your name.  
Let me taste sweet pleasure’s flight,  
Faithful love unspoiled by blight,  
And lift you into fame.  

Your glory shall not fade nor sever,  
Till you unbind yourself forever,  
When Time itself must cease.  
Such lending yields eternal cheer,  
For through you joy remains sincere,  
A gift that grants release.

Time, the root of all my strife,  
Once a giver, now a knife—  
How harsh thy heart has turned.  
Thou, who breathed love’s first sweet flame,  
Now stand unyielding, cold, the same,  
Though ever my soul burned.  

Have I failed, or left undone  
Some sacred vow, some rite unsung,  
To earn thy bitter ire?  
Now no moment shall I find  
Where joy and peace might soothe my mind—  
Thy favor doth expire.  

Blame not my heart, but thine own sway;  
Thou gav’st me hours but to obey  
And love in holy kind.  
True love seeks naught but pure intent,  
While lesser hearts chase fleeting ends—  
No such thoughts fill my mind.  

Then halt thy march, relentless Time,  
And grant me once more love’s sweet climb,  
That I might sing thy praise.  
Let me taste joy, unspoiled, complete;  
In faithful love, let life be sweet,  
And fame thy name shall raise.  

Thy glory, Time, shall never fade,  
While thou dost lend, thy worth is made—  
Eternal what thou gave.

Time, sole architect of my distress,  
Once a vessel of promised rest,  
How coldly now you turn away—  
You birthed my love, ignited fire,  
Yet hold unchanged, without desire,  
Though scorched, I cannot sway.  

Have I neglected some sacred vow,  
Some tender rite unfulfilled till now,  
That stirs your wrath to motion?  
No fleeting moment can I find  
Where joy or peace may grace my mind—  
Your favor’s bitter erosion.  

Fault lies with you, not folly’s snare;  
Time gave time to seek things rare,  
For love that truth esteems.  
Unworthy hearts chase fleeting ends,  
But noble love no pretense bends,  
And thrives on noble dreams.  

O cruel time, suspend your flight,

Sweet shades why doe you seeke to give delight To mee who deeme delight in this vilde place Butt torment, sorrow, and mine owne disgrace To taste of joy, or your vaine pleasing sight; Show them your pleasures who saw never night Of greife, wher joyings fauning, smiling face Appeers as day, wher griefe found never space Yett for a sigh, a grone, or envies spite; Butt O on mee a world of woes doe ly, Or els on mee all harmes strive to rely, And to attend like servants bound to mee, Heat in desire, while frosts of care I prove, Wanting my love, yett surfett doe with love Burne, and yett freeze, better in hell to bee.

O shadowed calm, why cast your tender grace  
On one who sees in this grim, wretched space  
No hint of sweetness—only sorrow’s trace,  
A bitter torment clothed in soft embrace?  
Go, offer joy to those whose hearts know none  
Of grief’s deep night, where gladness, like the sun,  
Smiles full and bright, unbroken, never shunned,  
And sighs nor groans nor envy’s spite have run.  

But here—on me—a thousand sorrows weigh,  
As if all harm had marked me as its prey,  
Bound close, like servants chained to misery.  
I burn with want, yet freeze in care’s cruel bind,  
Starved of my love, yet drowning in love’s tide—  
Flames in my heart, frost in my soul—oh, why?  
Better to sink where hell’s raw fires reside.

O shadows, why do you labor to bestow solace upon one who discerns no solace within this desolate sphere, but only the burdens of anguish, shame, and the hollow echoes of disgrace? To savor joy or your alluring semblance is but a cruel jest. Reserve your gifts for those unacquainted with grief’s relentless dominion, where joy’s deceitful visage reigns unchallenged, and sorrow has never carved a refuge for sighs, groans, or the venom of envy. Yet upon me descends a tempest of woes, as though every ill conspires to anchor itself to my existence, attending me as bound vassals. Desire’s fire consumes, yet care’s frost encases me. Bereft of love, I am glutted by its absence, burning while frozen—an agony that makes infernal exile seem a kinder fate.

Why chase delight, dear shadows, in this place,  
Where all I find is torment, sorrow’s trace,  
And my disgrace? Your joy-filled, fleeting grace  
Serves not a heart that knows no soft embrace.  

Show your bright pleasures to those free of night,  
Where smiles of joy, like day, bring purest light,  
Where grief’s sharp edge has never sought to bite,  
Nor sighs nor groans nor envy mar their sight.  

But here, alas, a world of woes takes hold,  
All harms converge, their weight relentless, cold,  
Attending me like servants chained and bound—  
Heat stirs desire, yet care chills all around.  

I crave my love, yet drown in love’s excess,  
Burning and frozen in torment’s cruel press.  
Better to dwell in hell than this unrest.

Oh, tender shades, why cast your charm on me,  
Who finds no solace in this wretched space?  
Here, joy turns bitter, sorrow takes its place,  
And every grace warps into agony.  
Bestow your wonders where no grief can tread,  
Where laughter reigns and shadows dare not creep,  
Where hearts unbroken know no need to weep,  
And light eternal crowns each path they’ve led.  
But on my soul, a universe of pain  
Descends, as if all sorrow stakes its claim,  
A loyal throng of anguish at my side.  
I burn with yearning, frozen by despair,  
Bereft of love, yet drowning in its snare—  
A fire and frost where even hell might hide.

Gentle shades, why yearn to bring delight  
To one who finds this wretched place a blight,  
Where torment reigns, and sorrow takes its flight,  
And joy’s faint gleam is lost in endless night?  
Bestow your charms where grief has never tread,  
Where laughter blooms and smiling faces spread,  
Where hearts know not the heavy weight of dread,  
Nor envy’s pang, nor tears that must be shed.  
But lo, on me, all woes converge and stay,  
As if all harms were sworn to block my way,  
Bound like attendants, shadowing my plea.  
I burn with yearning, yet in frost I dwell,  
Love starves me still, though love I taste too well—  
To burn and freeze, oh, better to know hell.

Why, gentle shadows, do you seek to please  
This heart that finds no joy in such a place—  
A realm of torment, sorrow, and disgrace,  
Where fleeting brightness offers no true ease?  
Reveal your charms to those untouched by night,  
Where grief ne’er dwells, and joy’s alluring face  
Reigns unopposed, its radiance full of grace,  
Unbroken by a sigh or envy’s spite.  
But woe, a crushing world now burdens me,  
As harm collects and binds itself to be  
A constant thrall, attending at my side:  
Desire’s fierce flame, yet frozen care’s embrace—  
Bereft of love, though drowning in its trace—  
I burn and freeze; hell’s torment seems more mild.

Gentle shadows, why do you aim to bring delight  
To one who deems this wretched place a blight,  
Where torment dwells, disgrace and sorrow bite—  
No joy to savor, nor your fleeting light?  
Reveal your charms to those who’ve never known  
The night of grief, where joy, with gleaming tone,  
Reigns unbroken, where pain has never grown  
To birth a sigh, a groan, or envy’s moan.  
But oh, on me a world of woes does lie;  
All harms conspire, as if to me they’re tied,  
Bound like servants, trailing close behind.  
Desire burns while frost of care consumes;  
I long for love, yet love itself entombs—  
To burn and freeze; in hell, I'd fare less blind.

Reigns undisturbed, unmarked by pain’s cruel trace,

I freeze and burn—hell’s torment seems more fair.

To one who finds no solace in this sphere,  
But sorrow's grip, disgrace, and torment near,  
Where joy’s sweet taste turns bitter, lost, amiss?  
Show your soft pleasures to those free of night,  
Whose days know not the weight of grief’s embrace,  
Where smiles and mirth unbroken find their place,  
Untouched by sighs, or envy’s stinging bite.  
Yet woe’s vast tide consumes and binds my soul,  
As harm’s cold hands upon my spirit toll,  
Their ceaseless watch, a servitude of pain.  
Desire’s fierce heat, yet frozen cares remain,

To burn and freeze—hell’s depths could lift me higher.

Gentle shadows, why extend your sweet embrace  
To one who finds no solace in this space,  
Where joy turns bitter, sorrow leaves its trace,  
And every pleasure mirrors my disgrace?  
Unveil your charms to hearts untouched by pain,  
Where laughter blooms, unspoiled by grief’s refrain,  
Where day holds sway, and night does not complain,  
Unmarked by envy’s groan or sigh’s domain.  
Yet woe’s vast tide engulfs me, pressing near,  
As if all harm were bound to settle here,  
Attending close, like vassals sworn to serve.

Scorched as I freeze; hell’s depths might show more nerve.

Gentle shades, why offer solace here, To one who finds in this harsh sphere No joy, but anguish, shame, and searing pain? Your charms are wasted, vainly shown, On hearts untouched by grief unknown— Where mirth and ease in constant reign Have never yielded space to sorrow’s chain, Nor envy's sting, nor groaning moan. Yet on me lies a ceaseless weight, As if all ills conspired by fate To bind themselves as servants near. I burn with longing's fervent fire, While frost of care chills each desire— Consumed by love, yet love I fear. To scorch and freeze, this bitter spell— Perchance 'tis kinder still in hell.

Delight to one who finds this wretched space  
A den of sorrow, torment, and disgrace,

Where laughter blooms and sorrow finds no place,  
A world unmarred by envy’s cruel bite.

As if all harm were bound to me, controlled—  
A captive to desires that scorch and chill.  
I yearn, yet care’s cold frost consumes my breath;  
I drown in love, yet burn and starve to death.  
Perhaps in hell, this heart might find its will.

Sweet shades why doe you seeke to give delight To mee who deeme delight in this vilde place Butt torment, sorrow, and mine owne disgrace To taste of joy, or your vaine pleasing sight; Show them your pleasures who saw never night Of greife, wher joyings fauning, smiling face Appeers as day, wher griefe found never space Yett for a sigh, a grone, or envies spite; Butt O on mee a world of woes doe ly, Or els on mee all harmes strive to rely, And to attend like servants bound to mee, Heat in desire, while frosts of care I prove, Wanting my love, yett surfett doe with love Burne, and yett freeze, better in hell to bee.

Oh, sweet shades, why do you flutter like butterflies, eager to sprinkle joy upon my weary soul, who finds delight here an illusion, a cruel trick of fate? In this wretched realm, where sorrow's shadows twist and turn, tasting joy feels like a bitter mirage, a teasing glimpse of light. Show your pleasures to those who’ve never known the night of grief, where laughter dances freely, and sorrow never dares to dwell. 

But alas, a mountain of woes looms over me, or perhaps I am the magnet for misfortune, my every desire a servant to my pain. I feel the heat of longing in my veins, while the cold grip of despair bites at my heart. Yearning for my love, yet drowning in a flood of longing, I burn and freeze, caught in this hellish tug-of-war. Better to be lost in the inferno than endure this frozen despair.

Whoso list to hunt, I know where is an hind, But as for me, helas, I may no more. The vain travail hath wearied me so sore, I am of them that farthest cometh behind. Yet may I by no means my wearied mind Draw from the deer, but as she fleeth afore Fainting I follow. I leave off therefore, Sithens in a net I seek to hold the wind. Who list her hunt, I put him out of doubt, As well as I may spend his time in vain. And graven with diamonds in letters plain There is written, her fair neck round about: Noli me tangere, for Caesar's I am, And wild for to hold, though I seem tame.

For those inclined to pursue, I disclose the locus of a certain hind; yet, alas, such endeavors are forsworn to me. The fruitlessness of this toil has burdened me beyond endurance, leaving me among those who trail most distantly behind. And yet, no exhaustion, however profound, can sever my restless thoughts from the fleeting quarry. She evades, and I, enervated, give chase—but to persist is folly, for I attempt to enclose the ungraspable, to snare the wind within a net. Let any who would hunt her be assured: his labor shall prove no less futile than mine. Encircling her neck, inscribed in unyielding diamond script, one finds this decree: *Noli me tangere*—I belong to Caesar. Though she appears docile, she is untamable, an essence irreducibly wild.

Whoever wishes to hunt, I am aware of a hind’s location; however, as for myself, alas, I can do so no longer. The futile effort has exhausted me to such a degree that I am among those who fall the farthest behind. Yet, despite my weariness, I am unable to detach my thoughts from the deer. As she runs ahead, I pursue her weakly, only to abandon the chase, for it is akin to attempting to trap the wind in a net. To any who wish to pursue her, I assure you that he will similarly waste his time. Around her fair neck, inscribed in plain lettering and adorned with diamonds, are the words: "Do not touch me, for I belong to Caesar, and though I may appear tame, I am untamed and wild."

For those inclined to pursue, I disclose the locus of a certain hind; yet, alas, such endeavors are forsworn to me. The fruitlessness of this toil has burdened me beyond endurance, leaving me among those who trail most distantly behind. And yet, no exhaustion, however profound, can sever my restless thoughts from the fleeting quarry. She evades, and I, enervated, give chase—but to persist is folly, for I attempt to enclose the ungraspable, to snare the wind within a net. Let any who would hunt her be assured: his labor shall prove no less futile than mine. Encircling her neck, inscribed in unyielding diamond script, one finds this decree: *Noli me tangere*—I belong to Caesar. Though she appears docile, she is untamable, an essence irreducibly wild.

Whoever wishes to hunt, I am aware of a hind’s location; however, as for myself, alas, I can do so no longer. The futile effort has exhausted me to such a degree that I am among those who fall the farthest behind. Yet, despite my weariness, I am unable to detach my thoughts from the deer. As she runs ahead, I pursue her weakly, only to abandon the chase, for it is akin to attempting to trap the wind in a net. To any who wish to pursue her, I assure you that he will similarly waste his time. Around her fair neck, inscribed in plain lettering and adorned with diamonds, are the words: "Do not touch me, for I belong to Caesar, and though I may appear tame, I am untamed and wild."

For those who chase, a hind I know, yet I refrain—alas, no longer can I strive. The futile chase has left my soul drained; I lag behind, the last in line. Still, my thoughts cannot unbind from the fleeing deer. Though wearied, I pursue, faltering. Yet I desist, for grasping her is like ensnaring the wind. To any who seek her, know this truth: your efforts, like mine, shall prove in vain. Upon her neck, where diamonds frame the words, it is engraved, plain to see: "Noli me tangere, for Caesar claims me his own." She is untamed, fierce to keep, though her guise seems gentle.

Who dares to chase? I know where roams a hind, Yet as for me, alas, I chase no more. The fruitless toil has left my spirit sore; I'm one who lags, the farthest left behind. Yet still my weary thoughts refuse to find Escape from her—the deer who flees before. Though faint, I follow. But I hunt no more, For catching wind within a net's unkind. Who seeks her still, I urge him—do not doubt— His time shall waste, as mine has done in vain. Around her neck, in diamonds etched and plain, These words declare, her beauty wrapped about: "Noli me tangere—for Caesar’s I reign, Untamed I move, though tame I may appear."

Whoe'er desires the chase, I know a hind, Yet as for me, alas, I hunt no more. This fruitless toil hath struck my spirit sore, And I am ever trailing far behind. Still, from her form my weary heart unbind I cannot, though she flees; I faint, implore, Yet follow on. At last, I must forswore— For in a net, the fleeting wind I’d bind. Who seeks her still, I warn with certain breath, His hours shall waste as mine in bitter pain. Around her neck, in diamonds clear and plain, These words are etched, a mark of living death: Noli me tangere, for Caesar's claim, Though seeming tame, she’s wild, a burning flame.

Who yearns to chase, I know where rests the hind, Yet I, alas, am bound to chase no more. The futile toil has left my spirit sore, I am of those who lag the farthest behind. Yet still my weary heart cannot unwind, Drawn to the deer as she escapes before— Breathless, I follow. I relent, therefore, For in the net I clutch the fleeting wind. Who seeks her hunt, I leave him without doubt, He’ll waste his hours as vainly as I’ve spent. Around her neck, in diamonds’ bright descent, The words are etched, the truth spelled plain throughout: “Noli me tangere, for Caesar’s I am, Wild to behold, though seeming soft, I stand.”

Who longs to chase, I know where hides the hind, Yet as for me, alas, I hunt no more. The fruitless toil has wearied me so sore, I am the one who lags the furthest behind. Yet still my restless heart cannot unbind From her swift flight; though worn, I still implore, Yet I must cease—this chase I do abhor, For in my grasp I only clutch the wind. Let others seek her; let them know the truth— Their efforts, too, shall crumble, spent in vain. Around her neck, in diamonds clear and plain, These words declare, forbidding all pursuit: “Touch me not, for Caesar’s claim I bear, Wild to possess, though tame I may appear.”

Whoever longs to chase, I know where runs a hind,  
Yet I, alas, can hunt no more. Resigned,  
I falter, worn by toil unkind,  
Trailing as one forever left behind.  

Though weary, my restless heart won’t part,  
Entwined with her as she darts ahead.  
Breathless, I stagger, my hope near dead—  
A futile net to snare the wind’s swift art.  

Let any who dare pursue her flight  
Know well they’ll squander their strength in vain.  
For round her neck, in diamonds plain,  
These words gleam sharp, a fierce delight:  

"Touch me not, for Caesar’s claim I bear,  
Untamed, though tameness I may wear."

Whoever longs to hunt, I know a doe— Yet as for me, alas, I chase no more. The fruitless toil has left my spirit sore; I lag behind where swifter hunters go. And still, my weary heart cannot forego The fleeting hind, though each pursuit I deplore, Breathless, I follow—but I try no more, For snaring wind is all my nets may know. To those who hunt her now, I plainly say: Like me, you’ll find your labor spent in vain. Around her neck, in diamond script’s refrain, These words are carved, their meaning clear as day: “Touch me not, for I to Caesar belong, Untamed I run, though I seemed tame all along.”

To those who chase, I know where rests a hind,  
Yet I, alas, may hunt her nevermore.  
This futile toil has worn me to the core,  
And left me trailing farthest from behind.  
Still, though my weary heart might seek to find  
Release, it clings; she flees, and I adore,  
Pursuing faintly, faltering as before—  
A net to snare the wind is all I bind.  

Let any hunter try, though doubt remains,  
For chasing her is but a fruitless plight.  
Around her neck, in diamonds etched and bright,  
The truth declares, in words that pierce like chains:  
"Noli me tangere, for Caesar’s claim  
I am, unyielding, wild despite my frame."

Whoever longs to chase, I know a hind, But as for me, alas, I hunt no more. The futile effort leaves my spirit sore, I lag behind, the farthest of my kind. Yet still, my weary heart remains inclined To track the deer, though ever she may soar. Breathless, I follow, though my hope implores— To grasp the wind in nets is folly blind. Whoever seeks her, doubt not what I say: Pursue her, waste your hours as I have done. Around her neck, in diamond script, there’s one Clear truth inscribed to keep all hearts at bay: Noli me tangere, for Caesar’s claim I bear, Untamed in essence, though I seem so fair.

Who yearns to chase, I know where waits a hind, Yet for myself, alas, I can no more. Such fruitless toil has left my spirit sore, And I am one who lags the farthest behind. Yet still, my weary heart cannot unbind Its pull toward her, though she flees before. Breathless, I follow—then pursue no more, For in a net, I strive to trap the wind. Let those who seek her hunt without a doubt; Their efforts, too, will prove a waste of time. Around her neck, inscribed in gems that shine, A message clear declares her fate aloud: "Noli me tangere—for Caesar’s I am, Untamed at heart, though tame I may appear."

Whoever longs to chase, I know a hind, Yet I, alas, can hunt her nevermore. The futile effort leaves me faint and sore, Among the slowest, farthest left behind. And still my weary heart cannot unwind From chasing her, though she flees swift before— My strength gives out; I falter, seek no more, For in a net I strive to grasp the wind. Let others hunt her, though I stake my claim: Their toil, like mine, will surely end in vain. Around her neck, in diamond script, a chain Declares to all her untouchable name: "Noli me tangere, for Caesar's I remain, Wild to pursue, though tame I may appear."

Who craves the chase, I know where rests a hind, Yet I, alas, pursue her nevermore. This futile striving wounds me to the core; I trail behind, the last of all my kind. And though my weary thoughts no solace find, Still drawn to her, though fleeting evermore, Faint, I press on, though sense bids me implore To cease, for nets can’t snare the roaming wind. Let hunters seek her; futile is their plight, Their labors spent for naught but endless strain. Encircled by her neck, in diamonds plain, These words inscribed restrain all eager might: Noli me tangere, for Caesar’s claim I bear— Untamed, though seeming tame beyond compare.

Whoso list to hunt, I know where is an hind, But as for me, helas, I may no more. The vain travail hath wearied me so sore, I am of them that farthest cometh behind. Yet may I by no means my wearied mind Draw from the deer, but as she fleeth afore Fainting I follow. I leave off therefore, Sithens in a net I seek to hold the wind. Who list her hunt, I put him out of doubt, As well as I may spend his time in vain. And graven with diamonds in letters plain There is written, her fair neck round about: Noli me tangere, for Caesar's I am, And wild for to hold, though I seem tame.

To those who seek the elusive quarry, I possess knowledge of its whereabouts; yet, alas, I find myself incapable of further pursuit. The futile endeavor has exhausted my spirit to an extreme, placing me among the ranks of the most distant followers. Nevertheless, I cannot detach my weary consciousness from the figure of the hunted, as it escapes before me, compelling me to pursue in a state of exhaustion. Thus, I relinquish my efforts, for striving to capture the ethereal is akin to ensnaring the wind. To those who aspire to hunt her, I offer this certainty: their time is destined for futility. Inscribed in diamonds upon her graceful neck is a clear decree: "Noli me tangere, for I belong to Caesar, and though I may appear subdued, I remain wild at heart."

My true-love hath my heart and I have his, By just exchange one for the other given: I hold his dear, and mine he cannot miss; There never was a bargain better driven. His heart in me keeps me and him in one; My heart in him his thoughts and senses guides: He loves my heart, for once it was his own; I cherish his because in me it bides. His heart his wound received from my sight; My heart was wounded with his wounded heart; For as from me on him his hurt did light, So still, methought, in me his hurt did smart: Both equal hurt, in this change sought our bliss, My true love hath my heart and I have his.

My beloved possesses my heart as I possess his, an equitable transaction wherein each bestowed the other’s essence in perfect parity. His heart, cradled within me, renders us a singular entity, indivisible in purpose and being; mine, entrusted to him, directs his thoughts and animates his perceptions. He treasures my heart, recognizing its origin within his own chest; I honor his, for it resides in the sanctuary of my soul. His heart suffered its wound, inflicted by the mere radiance of my gaze; mine, in turn, was pierced by the echo of his pain. As his anguish emanated from me toward him, so it reverberated within me, a reciprocal torment binding us in shared vulnerability. This symmetry of sorrow forged the foundation of our felicity: my beloved holds my heart, and I hold his.

My true love’s heart beats in my chest, and mine in his—a perfect barter, sealed with breath and blood. I cradle his tender heart, and he holds mine fast, as if the world itself would crumble were it misplaced. No deal was ever struck with such divine precision. His heart nests within me, stitching us into one seamless thread; my own dwells in him, steering his thoughts like stars to their courses. He treasures my heart, for it once sang his name; I adore his, for it hums softly in my ribs. His heart was pierced, felled by the arrow of my gaze; mine shuddered, wounded by his pain. The hurt we bore was twin-born, a mirrored ache that bound us to our joy. My true love’s heart beats in mine—and mine in his.

Our hearts have traded, his with mine,  
A pact of love, a bond divine.  
I guard his heart, he holds mine near,  
A truer trade was never clear.  

His heart in me, we merge as one,  
My heart in him, our fates begun.  
He treasures mine, once his to own,  
I prize his heart, within me sown.  

His heart was struck by my first glance,  
My heart was pierced, a mirrored stance.  
As his pain sprang from me to him,  
I felt his ache within my skin.  

In shared wounds, bliss found its start,  
My true love holds my heart, my heart.

My true-love holds my heart, and his I claim,  
A sacred trade, exchanged with equal trust.  
I guard his close; my own, he does the same,  
A pact unbroken, steadfast, fair, and just.  
His heart in me binds both in union tight;  
My heart in him directs his soul’s desires.  
He treasures mine, once his by sacred right;  
I prize his own, where all my hope transpires.  
His heart was pierced when first my gaze did fall;  
My heart felt pain as his began to bleed.  
For in his hurt, I knew my own in thrall—  
Our mirrored wounds united, love decreed.  
In equal pain, we found our perfect grace:  
My true-love holds my heart in his embrace.

Walking the fields a wantcatcher I spied, To him I went, desirous of his game: Sir, have you taken wants? Yes, he replied, Here are a dozen, which were lately taen. Then you have left no more. No more? quoth he. Sir I can show you more: the more the worse; And to his work he went, but 'twould not be, For all the wants were crept into my purse. Farewell friend wantcatcher, since 'twill not be, Thou cannot catch the wants, but they catch me.

Roaming the fields, I glimpsed a wantcatcher bent to his craft,  
And to him I strode, hungry for his haul.  
"Good sir," I asked, "have you snared some wants?"  
"Aye," said he, "a dozen, freshly seized."  
"Then none remain?" I pressed.  
"None? Oh, there are more," he laughed,  
"But more's a curse, not a prize."  
Back he turned to his snaring, yet all was in vain—  
For sly wants had slithered into my purse instead.  
"Farewell, hunter," I sighed, "your nets are undone;  
The wants chase me now, and I cannot outrun."

"Farewell, friend wantcatcher; it seems futile. You cannot seize the wants; they ensnare me instead."

Now in the West the slender moon lies low, And now Orion glimmers through the trees, Clearing the earth with even pace and slow, And now the stately-moving Pleiades, In that soft infinite darkness overhead Hang jewel-wise upon a silver thread. And all the lonelier stars that have their place, Calm lamps within the distant southern sky, And planet-dust upon the edge of space, Look down upon the fretful world, and I Look up to outer vastness unafraid And see the stars which sang when earth was made.

The crescent moon, slender as a whisper, sinks into the western hush, while Orion flickers between branches, his stride steady, deliberate, sweeping the earth clean. Above, the Pleiades drift like queens, their brilliance strung on threads of silver spun through the endless velvet dark. Farther still, the southern stars, solitary and serene, burn like distant lanterns, while faint planet-dust fringes the edges of infinity. They gaze upon this restless, troubled world, and I, unshaken, lift my eyes to the cosmic vastness, to the ancient choir of stars that sang when the earth was born.

Potuia, potuia White grave goddess, Pity my sadness, O silence of Paros. I am not of these about thy feet, These garments and decorum; I am thy brother, Thy lover of aforetime crying to thee, And thou hearest me not. I have whispered thee in thy solitudes Of our loves in Phrygia, The far ecstasy of burning noons When the fragile pipes Ceased in the cypress shade, And the brown fingers of the shepherd Moved over slim shoulders; And only the cicada sang. I have told thee of the hills And the lisp of reeds And the sun upon thy breasts, And thou hearest me not, Potuia, potuia Thou hearest me not.

Potuia, Potuia,  
Pale goddess of the silent tomb,  
Bend to my sorrow, O breathless isle of Paros.  
I am no stranger trailing the hems of propriety,  
No bound disciple cloaked in ritual.  
I am your kin, your long-lost flame,  
A voice from the furnace of forgotten time, crying out,  
And yet you do not hear.  

I have murmured to you in the hollow of your aloneness,  
Of our fevered days in Phrygia,  
Those molten hours when the sun burned high,  
The pipes fell silent beneath cypress shadows,  
And the shepherd’s hands, dark as earth,  
Brushed against narrow shoulders—  
Only the cicada sang.  

I have spoken of hills that whispered,  
Reeds that sighed,  
The sun that kissed your skin like prayer,  
Yet you hear me not, Potuia, Potuia,  
You do not hear.

Potuia, Potuia, White goddess of the grave, Please hear my sorrow, O quietness of Paros. I don’t belong with those gathered near you, With their robes and solemn ways. I am your brother, Once your lover, calling out to you, But you do not answer. I’ve spoken to you in your lonely places About our love in Phrygia, Those blazing afternoons When the delicate pipes Fell silent beneath the cypress trees, And the shepherd’s hands Grazed soft shoulders, While only the cicadas sang. I’ve reminded you of the hills, The rustle of reeds, And the sun warming your skin. But still, you do not hear me. Potuia, Potuia, You do not hear me.

Potuia, Potuia,  
Pale arbiter of sepulchral repose,  
Extend thy mercy to this desolation,  
O voiceless Parian sanctity.  
I am estranged from those who kneel at thy hem,  
Draped in their ceremonials and proprieties;  
For I am not mere supplicant,  
But thy kin,  
Thy erstwhile paramour, imploring thee across the abyss,
